name: Cova des Pinar
starting_point: Cemetery of La Vileta (Palma)
access: Own vehicle, or EMT bus line 8 (Puerta de San Antonio - Son Roca)
equipment: 15 m rope, 1 sling, 1 carabiner, 1 rope protector
description: A moderately-sized cave on the summit of Puig Rodó near La Vileta, richly decorated with formations throughout giving a baroque appearance. Features gours, stalagmitic masses, columns, and crawlways reaching a maximum depth of -14 m. Considered of both speleological and archaeological interest.
full_description:
Introduction

This is a cavity of not too large dimensions, situated on the summit of Puig Rodó, near one of the paths that connects La Vileta with the Coves del Pilar and the San Pancracio mines.

Description

Once inside, we will immediately realize that we are in a cavity fully covered with formations, as they surround us everywhere, giving a sense of baroque extravagance in cave form. We can begin the route through the western end, being careful as the floor in this area is quite slippery. As we advance over a floor carpeted with stones, we pass different stalagmitic masses on our right. After passing a couple of gours, we will find the small entrance that gives access to a small room that can be entered without progression equipment. It is not a place of great interest, but it is part of the cavity. The deepest part of the cave in this area is a little further below at -8 m. We can continue the visit heading NE, first passing over a small gour and then a larger one. On the left and on a ledge, formations pile up, occupying a large part of the central zone of the cave. Once past the gour, we can continue our route either by skirting the aforementioned ledge or through a crawlway of approximately half a dozen meters that runs beneath it. Whether we take one option or the other, the destination is the same: the interior of a small pit from which we will tackle the final section. To do so, we will have to overcome a low-ceiling area that will require us to put our crawling skills into practice until we reach -14 m, the maximum depth of the cave. We will return the same way until reaching the large gour area, from where we can venture into the central zone of the cave where the greatest number of formations are found, including notable stalagmitic masses, columns, gours, etc.

The Cova des Pinar can be considered of speleological interest, as well as archaeological.

As it is located in a prolific area for caves, we can head to any other one to complete the day.

name: El Forat 502
starting_point: Ma-2210 road, Km 13, past the tunnel on the left. Walk along the road to some pines, then ascend the path to the old road to Cala Murta. Head towards Puig Fumat, reach Coll de sa Creu, descend via zigzag, then climb towards Puig Gaballó summit. A cairn near the entrance helps locate it, partially hidden by a lentisk bush.
equipment: For Sala d'en Pek: 50 m rope, 2 bolts for anchor head, 2 more for rebelays, 2 protectors, 4 carabiners. For Sala Voltors: 10 m rope, 2 slings, 2 carabiners (natural anchors). For Pou Cabro: no information provided.
description: One of the most beautiful caves on the island, located near Cala Figuera and Cala Murta in the final stretch of the Serra de Tramuntana. Features a broken great column, a calcite-covered Myotragus Balearicus skull, coral formations, and richly decorated chambers including the Sala d'en Pek, Sala Voltors, and the Pou del Coral. Requires vertical progression equipment with multiple rebelays.
full_description:
We find ourselves before one of the most beautiful cavities on the island, which is also set in one of the most beautiful landscapes of the "Serra de Tramuntana," specifically in its final stretch near the coves of "Cala Figuera" and "Cala Murta" as well as "Puig Fumat."

Once the small entrance has been located near some bushes, we only need to set up the anchor head using the two bolts found near it. Before beginning the descent, a protector must be used to prevent rope abrasion at the entrance. After a 4-meter descent, we will find on our right a spit where we will install the first rebelay, and seven meters lower a new spit for the second and last rebelay. We continue descending approximately half a dozen meters until positioning ourselves on the surface of the great broken column. A protector will once again help us to make the final descent a dozen meters further down. We are in the "Sala d'en Pek" next to the great column; another piece of column lies next to it on the ground. Although everything seems to indicate it belongs to the great column, I do not believe this is the case, since if we were to add it to the column and try to stand it upright, this would not be possible because the ceiling would prevent it.

We can begin the visit by heading toward the "Sala Voltors." To do so, with our backs to the piece of column, we will walk straight ahead with a slight tendency to the right until finding the skull of Myotragus Balearicus which, in part together with the broken column mentioned earlier, identifies the cavity. Said skull is wedged between two rocks and covered in calcite. Next to it is a tiny nest of "pisoliths" in their initial phase measuring only a few centimeters in diameter, composed of small polished pebbles that still retain their original and varied colors, some of them almost transparent — a true jewel.

We continue ascending among the chaos of blocks until reaching a higher, highly decorated elevation where some stalagmites of considerable size stand out, with conical shapes. A little further ahead and to our right, we begin to see at a lower level the "Sala Voltors," which we will access by first anchoring on a stalagmite and then, a little lower, making another rebelay on another stalagmite to cover just a few meters. The rest of the descent to reach the room can be done by downclimbing — but with great care. The floor of the "Sala Voltors" is as chaotic as the rest of the cave but beautifully decorated, well worth pausing to contemplate. At this same spot, the two openings that will allow us to descend the "Pou del Coral" open up. These openings are very small, though one more so than the other, and passing through them is difficult, especially depending on the build of the speleologist, requiring us to remove our progression equipment since we won't need it during the downclimb, even though we will pass through some delicate points. After descending several meters, multiple delicate coral formations of brown color begin to make their appearance — the color that pervades the entire cavity. The descent becomes increasingly difficult until continuing is impossible.

Back in the "Sala Voltors," we return the same way until reaching the spot where the conical stalagmites are, and from there we can descend to our left to make a detour toward the great broken column, being able to contemplate along the way various formations of great beauty and singularity, with a beautiful curtain standing out.

Once at the starting point, we can visit the "Sala de sa Mosca" by heading toward the "Myotragus" skull, passing it, and then entering a well-decorated gallery, gradually approaching in a gentle descent to the balcony from which the room is visible. From this spot, we can descend into it either with or without equipment; if we choose the second option, great care is needed. The "Pou Cabro" — its name says it all. We did not visit it, perhaps because we had already had our full dose of rockfalls with the visit we made the previous week to the "Avenc del Infern" in the "Serra de Na Burguesa" (Calvià).

name: Avenc de Can Caliu
starting_point: MA-1015 road, Km 1.140
access: Own vehicle. Can also be accessed on foot from Palma Nova.
equipment: 1 rope of 40 m and another of 10 m, 2 bolt plates and 2 protectors
description: An interesting pothole on a hillside with a 4 m diameter entrance. The mainly aerial descent leads to a detrital cone at a 42% slope. The main chamber (15 x 35 m) features abundant pure white stalactites and eccentric formations, with a beautiful decorated chimney in the deepest section.
full_description:
Description.

Situated on a hillside and facing in the direction of the place that gives it its name, the approximately 4 m diameter entrance of this interesting pothole can be found. The descent, mostly aerial, places us on a detrital cone with a slope of around 42%.

On the main level of roughly 15 x 35 m, reconstructive processes are not lacking; in fact, in some parts of the cavity they are abundant and of a very pure white color, with some eccentric stalactites standing out. As in many other cavities in the Serra de na Burguesa, subsidence processes due to ground collapse are also visible in this cavity. Worth highlighting is a beautiful chimney located in the deepest part of the main level whose walls are beautifully decorated.

A small shaft in the deepest part, which can be accessed with or without a rope, allows us to reach the deepest part of the pothole, which is well worth visiting as it is rich in formations, many of them eccentric.

Right at the top of the aforementioned shaft, there is the possibility of climbing up to some fantastic corners with a great profusion of formations in an excellent state of conservation.

Rigging

A wild olive tree can serve to set up the anchor point at the top, and a little further down there are two spits, the first at the top of the shaft and the second just a bit lower where an easy rebelay can be made. While descending, we will encounter two easily detectable spots to place rope protectors before reaching the top of the detritus cone.

name: Avenc de Es Mirador
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: Vertical progression kit, one 50 m rope (rigged to a pine tree above), one sling, at least one carabiner for the single natural-anchor deviation on a small stalagmite, three protectors
description: A typical SW-NE fracture-origin pothole in the Serra de na Burguesa with a narrow entrance (3 m x 0.5 m). The interior is formed by large piled blocks, some coated in flowstone. The ceiling is covered in white stalactites, and the angled roof has produced numerous curtain formations of varying sizes. The main chamber measures 36 x 28 m and is classified as a site of speleological interest.
full_description:
Near one of the many abandoned cart tracks found in this mountain range, 270 m above the Na Carnisera scree slope and about 350 m from the relay antennas, this small pothole is found, which has as its companion about 50 m away the Coveta de s'Ullastre (459685 E, 4377340 N).

The Avenc de Es Mirador meets all the requirements to be identified with the rest of the multiple potholes found in the Serra de na Burguesa area, formerly called the Serra de Porto Pi, since like its companions it is formed by a SW-NE fracture in the terrain. Its exact orientation is 30° N. The dimensions of its entrance are slightly less than three meters wide by fifty centimeters high.

Access to the pothole is done by sliding down a gentle and narrow ramp for a few meters until reaching the vertical that will take us to the first and only rebelay, for whose installation we will use a small stalagmite that meets the safety requirements. We will also place a protector a little below the knot. Already at this point, we will have the opportunity to observe the morphology of the cavity in which large blocks piled one upon another form the floor, some of them already covered in carbonate forming flowstones. The visit to this cavity does not give a sense of danger, as occurs in others in the area, but even so great care must be taken when moving, given the possibility that some of the many loose stones could come free, with the consequent danger for companions below.

The first formations of interest consist of a ceiling carpeted with numerous white stalactites of different sizes. The use of the rope ends at the minus twenty-five meter mark, where the ground becomes more stable and level while the large blocks are above us, so we can move "comfortably" through the cavity, scrutinizing its corners and finding interesting formations in some of them. The incline of the pothole's ceiling has caused the formation of numerous curtain formations of all types and sizes, not to mention the stalagmites and stalactites.

The deepest part of the cave is full of small stones that prevent seeing whether the cavity continues, despite it not giving that impression.

In summary, as I said at the beginning of this description, the Avenc de Es Mirador is faithful to the style of those in this area of the island — characteristics that make many regular practitioners of this activity dislike visiting them due to the fear instilled by the quantity of unstable blocks piled up inside. The concretions are not excessively abundant, but there are enough to keep us entertained for a while taking photographs before beginning the ascent. The dimensions of the main level are 36 m long by slightly less than 28 m at its widest point.

The Avenc de Es Mirador is considered a site of speleological interest.

name: Avenc de Fra Rafel
starting_point: Menut II recreational area
route_type: Predominantly horizontal (in the partial section described)
equipment: 3 ropes (1x70m, 2x30m), 1 sling, carabiners, 4 bolts (one for the anchor head if not using the natural rock bridge)
description: One of the most interesting, complex, and beautiful caves on Mallorca, reaching -145m depth. This description covers only the 'Pou Classic' zone, the least technically demanding section, though it still requires hanging over a 60m shaft. The magnitude rating reflects only this partial description of the cave.
full_description:
Description

We are before one of the most interesting, complex, and beautiful cavities on the island of Mallorca, although this description will only cover a part of the cavity, specifically the zone called "Pou Classic." This is possibly the least technically demanding zone, although one should not let one's guard down at any moment, as the visit to this zone of the cavity will require us to be suspended over a shaft of more than 60 m. If the aim is to know this pit in depth, the book "Mallorca Belleza en la Oscuridad" (Mallorca Beauty in the Darkness) describes the cavity magnificently.

name: Avenc de Na Pola
starting_point: C/ Son Duran. Vehicle can be parked beside a small plaza.
best_season: Any, as it is very close to the town of Alaró.
equipment: 1 rope of 30 m for the pit, 1 rope of 40 m for the rest, 1 bolt, 5 carabiners, slings, and several protectors.
description: A 41-meter deep predominantly vertical pit near Alaró with beautifully decorated galleries and chambers. After a 21-meter aerial descent, visitors can explore the Pellet Gallery (a raptor roost), the Cut Chamber with impressive lance-shaped stalactites up to 2 meters long, and the Window Chamber with abundant formations. The deepest point is the small Bones Chamber at -41 m, floored with red clay. The pit has unfortunately been used for disposing of dogs.
full_description:
DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

If we want direct access to the pit — that is, if we do not want to set up fractionations — the branch of a holm oak can serve us to set up the head of the installation, and we can secure it at some of the spits found near it.

After a 21 m aerial descent — close to the wall, which we can reach at many points — we will find ourselves at the base of a debris cone of approximately 4 m in depth where multiple bone remains of dogs accumulate, since unfortunately this pit has served and continues to serve as a final destination for these animals. A reasonable amount of ceiling and wall concretion can be seen in this area of the cavity. On the east side there is a gallery of approximately 5 m in length with a small chamber at the end filled with bones.

To continue the visit, we have to climb 3 m up the wall to reach the window that is clearly visible on the west side of the pit. We find ourselves in the Galeria de ses Egagròpiles (Pellet Gallery), the home and dining area of a nocturnal raptor, at least during the time of our visit, where we found a large quantity of owl pellets scattered throughout this gallery of 2 x 10 m, nearly flat and well decorated, which anticipates what we will encounter throughout the rest of the cave.

At the end of the gallery, after passing by two beautiful columns, a step of a couple of meters brings us to the Sala des Tall (Cut Chamber), measuring 4 x 3 x 5.5 m, which like the Pellet Gallery is beautifully decorated with concretions, especially in the ceiling area where lance-shaped stalactites abound, some reaching 2 m in length. At the back of the chamber, a window allows us to look into the Sala de sa Finestra (Window Chamber). This is a chamber of 4.5 x 4 x 7 m which, following the pattern of the cave, abounds in all types of formations. At the bottom of the chamber, a narrow passage of approximately 0.60 x 0.35 m allows us to access the Saleta dets Ossets (Small Bones Chamber). After overcoming a small ramp, this is a small chamber of 8 x 4 x 4 m that functions as a sink, with an almost completely flat floor covered in red clay.

On the walls of the chamber, marks left by the water level that was reached at some point can be observed. In a corner of the chamber, hundreds of rodent bones accumulate. This point marks the -41 m level, being the deepest point of the cavity.

name: Avenc de s'Embut
starting_point: Parking near Nuestra Señora de la Paz and Mirador de Na Burguesa restaurant (Génova). Follow the path past the radio antennas toward Coll des Pastors. After the first high-voltage tower (on the left), climb a small hill — the pothole is 90 m off the path at the end of the descent.
equipment: 2 ropes (25 m for entrance shaft, ~60 m for handline), 4-5 protectors, 7-8 carabiners, several slings as deviators
description: Considered perhaps the most beautiful cave in the Serra de na Burguesa. The funnel-shaped entrance leads through the hazardous 'Tub de Ses Pedres' (rock tube — only one person at a time due to rockfall danger) into the spectacular 'Sala de las Columnas' with a 13 m column, forests of stalactites, eccentric formations, and speleothems of various colors. Reaches 95 m depth at the bottom of the column hall.
full_description:
We are before what is perhaps the most beautiful cavity of all those currently known in the Serra de na Burguesa. It is neither the one with the greatest development nor the deepest, as in all of that it is surpassed by the Avenc de l'Infern, located near the Puig d'en Bou with its 800 meters of development and its -132 meters of depth. Where it has nothing to envy any cavity in the area is in its beauty, as I said previously, since in its depths there is no shortage of formations of all types that will not leave us indifferent and where we can dedicate hours to photography.

The topography accompanying this description has been kindly provided by F. Grácia, which improves upon the previous one made in 1977 by J. Damians and J. Fernández, which was little more than a sketch. Miquel Ángel Barceló. ENDINS, 22 (1998).

As its name indicates, the entrance to the pothole is found in a depression in the terrain shaped like a funnel at one end of the doline. The anchor for the rigging will be set up on the wall directly opposite the entrance, where two parabolts with their corresponding plates are already installed, and if we want additional security, a little higher up and to the left on another block of stone there is also a spit installed with its corresponding plate.

As soon as we begin the descent, we will place the first protector to protect ourselves from an obvious friction point even while still on the exterior, and another just inside the entrance a little further down. Just three meters further, a new parabolt already installed will allow us to set up the first rebelay, from which we will begin an aerial descent of about 17 meters that will deposit us on a ramp that will force us to balance like a tightrope walker until we manage to stay upright and climb a small ramp where we can wait calmly for the rest of our companions. We find ourselves in a reasonably well-lit chamber of about 15 x 19 m in which the first formations already appear, with a beautiful parietal flowstone standing out. On a rock at the top of the so-called "Tub de Ses Pedres" (Stone Tube) we will rig the second rope, at the -25 m mark. We will place a deviator on a stalagmite to be able to continue descending.

Before continuing with the description of the descent, something very important must be made clear: the entire descent, as its name indicates, is a kind of channel with the floor in some places covered with stones that, with the passage of the caver, tend to come loose and hurtle down at great speed, potentially endangering the safety of anyone in their path. It is not recommended — rather, under no circumstances should more than one person be using this stretch at a time, as the possibility of "raining" stones is certain.

We will place the third deviator using a sling on a parietal flowstone in a place where there is a small alcove in which only two people can remain safe from falling stones. Therefore, before the arrival of a third person, someone must leave the spot. From here we will use some more deviators, taking care to protect the friction points, until we position ourselves at the top of the "Sala de las Columnas" (Hall of Columns) at approximately the -60 meter mark, from where we can continue moving without any type of support.

Right where the "Tub de Ses Pedres" ends and the "Sala de las Columnas" begins, one can observe above our heads some formations to which a piece of floor flowstone is attached on their underside. This seems to indicate that in past times the floor was at that level and was possibly blocking the passage into the "Sala de las Columnas." Today, however, we can pass freely as the ceiling height is considerable. From the top of the "Sala de las Columnas" where we find ourselves, the view is spectacular, reminding me in a way of the Cova del Mármol, since from above one seems to be in an amphitheater from which the entire hall is surveyed. We will continue descending through the hall, always seeking the most suitable route, while we encounter a thick column affected by the solifluction process with vertical displacement. Not far from there, another column of 13 meters in height delights our eyes. Other formations are affected by the solifluction process at the right end of the hall and in other places, while the ceiling remains covered by a forest of stalactites, some of a very pure white color. On the left side, eccentric formations abound, as well as a large stalagmite and stalactites of different colors, some of them still active. I was able to locate only a single gour in the hall.

Only in the deepest part of the hall does the cavity become horizontal, though on a floor covered with stones and formations that have ended up in this place. Right at this spot there is a small shaft of approximately 5 meters that can be accessed by downclimbing without the use of vertical progression equipment. In it a fine column stands out. At the lowest point, we find ourselves at the -95 meter mark — the maximum depth of the pothole.

name: Avenc de sa Coma Fosca
starting_point: Upper area of Binicaubell street
equipment: 1 rope of 25m, 2 slings, 1 protector, rope or sling of 16m for deviation
description: A small 13.5m deep chasm with two access openings, featuring a collapse-type entrance hall (11x14.5x8m) filled with debris, large stalactites, and animal bone remains. The cave contains beautiful parietal flowstones, botryoidal speleothems with fine coralloids, and a significant stalagmitic formation. A hidden second chamber (Sala Amagada) is beautifully decorated with columns, eccentric formations, fistular stalactites, and a dry gour.
full_description:
Description

A small chasm of 13.5 m in depth located at the head of sa Coma Fosca, with two access mouths. The larger one exceeds three meters in length by one in width, while the smaller one, slightly over a meter deep, gives way to a small ramp with considerable slope, from where the floor is reached after an 8 m free-hanging section. The orientation is N-S.

Entrance Hall (Sala de S'Entrada)

Whether we use one entrance or the other, upon reaching the floor we will land on a pile of debris where abundant blocks of different sizes accumulate, stalactites — some of great size — as well as bone remains of both caprines and canids. The hall is of the collapse type, with a more or less oval shape and dimensions of 11 x 14.5 x 8 m.

The reconstructive processes of the hall are abundant, with beautiful parietal flowstones standing out, botryoidal speleothems on which very fine coralloids develop, as well as considerable ceiling ornamentation, in addition to the stalagmitic formation located right below the entrance.

In the deepest zone of the hall, and therefore of the cave, there is a sinkhole where the marks left by water on its walls are clearly visible, at a height of approximately one meter, and where continuity may exist.

Hidden Hall (Sala Amagada)

There are two accesses to this hall. One is located right above the sinkhole of the Entrance Hall; the other is accessed a little further to the right after climbing a couple of meters to reach a narrow opening where clearing work was carried out. This access is very narrow and not suitable for everyone. The dimensions of the hall are 4 x 4.5 x 2 m and it is beautifully concretioned, to the point that it is difficult to move through it without risking breaking some of its speleothems, among which stand out a large quantity of columns, stalactites, eccentric formations, and fistular stalactites. Stalagmites are also present, as well as a beautiful dry gour whose interior is filled with a large quantity of stalactite fragments. The cornices of the gour are very well developed and in good condition.

name: Avenc de s'Esquerda
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 461006 E, 4378133 N. DATUM WGS84: 460904 E, 4377933 N
equipment: North entrance: 50 m rope, 1 bolt for installation, 2 slings or cords (one for a carabiner next to the bolt, another for the rebelay on the ledge), plus a 40+ m rope. South entrance: 50 m rope, 1 bolt for installation and another for a rebelay
description: A spectacular 65 m deep chasm discovered in 2008 in the Serra de Na Burguesa, with two entrances (North and South). The main chamber reaches 50 m in height and is richly decorated with speleothems from floor to ceiling, with a large bat colony. Notable finds include remains of Myotragus balearicus, and a striking stalagmite that grew both vertically and horizontally from a small base, creating one of the most beautiful formations seen.
full_description:
Story of a discovery.

On the morning of February 9, 2008, having nothing to do until lunchtime and finding myself bored, the idea came to me of going for a walk through the Serra de Na Burguesa to try to locate some cavity in an area near the Cova del Tuputut, since according to a member of a forestry brigade who had been working in the area, he had apparently discovered one or another hole in the ground, communicating this to his brother and in turn his brother to me. Before reaching the indicated area, I left the path and had the luck of finding a small chasm that J. Encinas subsequently confirmed was uncatalogued, so it had already been worth going out that day. Once I had taken the coordinates of the cave, which later came to be called "Pouet Escollerat de l'Ase," I continued my way to the area where supposedly I would have no great difficulty finding some of the holes seen by the forestry brigade. What would be my surprise and displeasure when, after combing the area, I had to leave without finding a trace of any of them. On my way home, I passed again in front of the small chasm found a few hours earlier, cursing my bad luck for not having found anything despite the directions. Again I decided to continue my return off the path, though not far from it, while at the same time the idea was going through my head of the possibility that a stroke of luck might allow me to find something before definitively rejoining the path. But suddenly, when I no longer harbored any hope, I thought I observed a depression in the terrain, so I immediately approached the place and could make out a crack in the ground about six meters long by about two meters wide. Without knowing what was hidden inside, my expression already changed, going from being a little angry with myself for not having been able to find anything in the indicated area to being happy regardless of what the crack might hold inside — which perhaps would be nothing special since it was at a straight-line distance of approximately one hundred meters from the path and I had no news that anything important was located in that place.

Back at my home, I contacted J. A. Encinas so he could confirm whether he had any knowledge of the existence of any cave at the coordinates I had taken with my GPS, and he later confirmed, once he had checked his maps, that nothing was catalogued in that place, so we agreed to go take a look as soon as possible.

First exploration.

On March 21, 2008, five of us finally headed to the place with the intention of exploring the crack in question, so we installed a bolt for the rope placement as well as a new safety installation on a natural anchor in the rock, and J. A. Encinas, G. Herraez, and yours truly, J. Bermejo, entered its interior. During the descent through the crack, partly covered by rocks of all sizes fallen from the outside, and while descending I did not harbor great hopes regarding the final result since throughout the entire route everything was more of the same, until everything changed when I finally "landed" on a pile of debris from which I could observe with some clarity what awaited us. After a superficial exploration and assessing the importance of the find, we decided to exit to the outside to return another day and thus finish the exploration while also producing the survey of the cave.

The remains of a hut are found near the south entrance of the chasm, which was surely built in that exact spot and no other in order to take advantage of the thermals from the chasm and heat the hut naturally on cold days. If we look carefully at the surroundings of the hut, it is also possible to see the beginning of the flowstones.

Second exploration

On April 13, 2008, we decided to finish the exploration of the chasm, so again the same three people who had accessed it the first time gathered to do so, beginning by making a new installation at the second entrance or South entrance, which M. L. Redondo found by chance when moving some rocks on the first day of exploration while waiting for us to come out. This second entrance is located a few meters to the south following the crack, with dimensions of 0.40 x 0.50 m.

As no natural anchor was found near the entrance, a bolt had to be installed, just as at what we shall call the main entrance or North entrance, where it is also necessary to install a rebelay at about ten meters depth on a natural anchor that J. A. Encinas found there. If we cannot find it, then we will have no choice but to install a bolt.

North Entrance

The mouth of the cave is approximately 6 x 2 meters in size and is oriented north to south. To access it, a bolt must be installed in the rock, also securing the rope a second time on a natural anchor in the rock that must be found, and if that is not possible, the best would be to install a second bolt.

A little more than a meter from the mouth of the chasm, we will find a step that can also be accessed without a rope, from which we will begin a rappel of approximately 11 meters that will place us on a kind of ledge formed by rocks, some of great size, that have been falling from the outside and that cover most of the crack.

At the north end of this ledge, a hole seems to indicate the continuation of the cave, but it is actually at the south end where it continues, once a rebelay is installed to continue the descent using a natural anchor. As we continue descending little by little, the natural light disappears until reaching total darkness that the headlamp takes care of illuminating, and throughout the entire route one can observe that the theoretical wall where we rest our feet is formed by threatening rocks fallen from the outside that have gradually been blocking a large part of the chasm's crack. After descending a little less than 32 meters from the previously mentioned ledge and 45 meters from the outside, we will find ourselves at the top of an enormous cone of debris that marks the beginning of the horizontal section of the chasm.

South Entrance

A rope of approximately 50 m will be necessary to descend through this entrance, where at about four meters depth we will pass next to a bridge that separates us from the other entrance, and at about ten meters we will need to install a rebelay to then let ourselves drop to the main chamber, just two or three meters from the other entrance, on top of the debris cone.

Floor plan

The floor plan of the chasm consists of a main chamber divided in two by a stalagmitic barrier, the larger part being the one containing the debris cone, which reaches a length of eighteen meters by as many meters wide.

We immediately realize that we are in a special place where for a long time water has done its work, as the chamber, with a height of fifty meters, is concretioned from base to ceiling where bats flutter about, frightened by our presence.

The slope of this chamber is considerable since the accumulation of rocks and animals fallen from the outside have been filling the floor.

Right at the deepest part of the chamber, in a beautiful corner and to the left as we descend, lie the remains of a Myotragus balearicus, also surrounded nearby by many other bones of different animals.

To the right of the Myotragus balearicus remains are some layers of strata that possibly have something to say about the history of the chasm, and a little further to the right the cave continues through a narrow crack that descends nine meters accessible without vertical progression equipment and at least another seven meters in which it is impossible to advance due to its narrowness.

A great stalagmitic wall separates this chamber from another parallel one, with a precious and impressive column standing out at the upper end of the two chambers.

A kind of balcony on which one can contemplate small stalagmites growing at its base is found above our heads at about eight meters altitude, impossible to access without climbing equipment.

The parallel chamber, accessed as soon as one arrives at the apex of the debris cone from outside, is a little smaller but equally impressive as the previous one. It is accessed through a kind of wide crack formed on its left side by the previously mentioned column and on the right by one end of the chamber. Noteworthy in this place is the great quantity of large blocks that have fallen on the floor, but in this case they are not rocks but pieces of speleothems.

The left side wall is what divides this chamber from the main one, while the right side wall shows us an impressive flowstone produced by an ancient water cascade now long gone; the front wall also has little to envy. Noteworthy is a stalagmite found more or less in the center of the chamber that, starting from a small base, as it grew vertically also grew horizontally, creating one of the most beautiful formations I have seen to this day.

Also, just a little before positioning ourselves at the apex of the debris cone, by climbing we will access a small and charming chamber whose mouth opens to the main chamber, with dimensions of about five meters by three meters wide, and inside which we will not be able to stand upright but where we will enjoy its charm, featuring extremely thin columns, stalagmites, and soda straws.

Fauna

While on the first time we accessed the chasm we did not find any bat specimens, on this second visit we did find a colony of between twenty and twenty-five large bats that I want to think are horseshoe bats since they were double or triple the size of those I had found until then in other cavities.

Many spiders can be observed as well as mosquitoes also of dimensions not commonly found outdoors in Mallorca, not to mention — since it personally surprised me — the occasional blowfly. Logically, any other type of fauna that the cavity may harbor is beyond my understanding and I leave it for specialized persons to describe.

For further reading:

JORDI SERRACOBO, BLANCA AMENGUAL, MARC LÓPEZ ROIG, JOSEP MÁRQUEZ, XAVIER BAYER, CISCO GUASCH, ANTONIA SÁNCHEZ i JOAN ANTONI OLIVER. (2007): Quinze anys d'estudis quiropterológics a les Illes Balears. Endins, 31: 125-139. Palma de Mallorca.

JORDANA, R., ET AL.: Descripción de una nueva especie de ENTOMOBRYA (COLLEMBOLA, ENTOMOBRYIDAE) de una cueva de Mallorca (Islas Baleares, España). Serie Zoologica, 29(2): 8-21. 2005.

ENCINAS, J.A., 1974. Inventario bio-espeleológico de Baleares, año 1973. Com. IV Simp. Bioespeleología. E.C.E., G.E. Pedraforca: 49-62. Barcelona.

GINÉS, A., 1982. Inventario de especies cavernícolas de las Islas Baleares. Endins, 9: 57-75.

name: Avenc de sa Finestreta
starting_point: Puerto de Pollensa, UTM coordinates DATUM ETRS89 508230 4418185
clothing: Coveralls, gloves, and knee pads essential
equipment: Ropes: 1 x 40 m, 1 x 65 m. Slings: 7. Carabiners: 12. Bolts: 5. Some rope protectors.
description: Discovered in 2015 at the base of a cliff near dwarf palms, this 73 m deep sinkhole required extensive widening at various points. It features narrow passages, multiple vertical shafts, and a series of chambers descending to the CO2 Room at maximum depth. A challenging cave requiring both vertical progression skills and the ability to navigate very tight passages, where any rescue would be extremely difficult.
full_description:
Description

Located at the base of a cliff and hidden next to a group of Chamaerops humilis (palmetto or dwarf palm), it was discovered by Pedro Martín in 2015. After arduous clearing and widening work carried out at different points of the cavity by the discoverer in the company of Antonio Tarazaga, that same year the first topographic survey was conducted under the direction of Rafael Minguillón, covering the first 22 m of depth of the sinkhole. Subsequent clearing and widening have allowed it to reach its current depth of 73 m.

As soon as one enters through the small access mouth, one must face the first narrow passage, which once overcome leaves us above a small shaft of approximately two meters in depth. A small window leads us into a short crawlway that will soon allow us to reach the head of the shaft that will place us seven meters lower, where we will set up another anchor point to reach the Gatera Room, at -22 m.

Four meters before reaching the deepest zone of the shaft, a level opens to the left and right. To the east, one can descend to a small obstructed shaft. From this location, one can observe how the exterior light is visible in the overhead zone. This is the inaccessible entrance to the sinkhole, but it can be used to pass some of the equipment we will need into the interior of the cavity. To the west of this level and after a small climb, one can access a beautiful room featuring beautiful curtains, some of them of large dimensions.

In the SE zone of the room, a crawlway of approximately ten meters, the third difficulty in the cavity, absorbs the water that reaches this point of the sinkhole. In the overhead zone of the crawlway, the erosive channel that once allowed the continuity of the water as well as the development of the sinkhole is clearly visible. At the end of the crawlway, a new shaft of 18.5 m in depth opens, placing us at the -41 m level, on a platform of 3 x 4 m — this is the Sala des Fang (Mud Room). To the east of the room, a small sinkhole opens that, after overcoming the Pas des Patiment (Passage of Suffering), will allow us to look out through a small window onto the Pou des Nius d'Oroneta (Swallow Nests Well); two spits located on a column will allow us to install the rope to descend along a stone cascade to the Sala des Paleonivel (Paleolevel Room), which is located 18 m below, and from there to the SE a new shaft exceeding twelve meters will place us in the deepest zone of the cavity, the Sala des CO2 (CO2 Room), where mud abounds in addition to a certain amount of carbon dioxide, and where although the cavity appears to continue through a sinkhole on the west side of the room, the passage becomes impassable.

The Avenc de sa Finestreta is an interesting cavity, since in addition to qualities that allow the practice of recreational caving, both with the use of vertical progression equipment and by testing our ability to overcome very narrow passages — notably the Pas des Patiment, undoubtedly the most difficult, along with the one that gives the sinkhole its name and which is located just one meter from the entrance, whose greatest difficulty comes when exiting the sinkhole. It should be noted that an accident in this cavity would make rescue an extremely laborious and difficult undertaking.

name: Avenc de sa Moneda
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: Multiple descent routes available. Generally: 50 m rope, 2-3 bolt plates, carabiners, slings, 2-4 protectors (varies by route)
description: A spectacular pothole in the Serra de na Burguesa with a massive main chamber exceeding 40x40 m, beautifully decorated with formations, large stalagmitic masses, and impressive columns — including a remarkable 19 m column with plants growing at the top. Natural light from the entrance creates dramatic lighting effects, and multiple descent routes of varying difficulty make it suitable for both sporting and speleological interests.
full_description:
The "Avenc de sa Moneda" is located in the Serra de na Burguesa within the S'Hostalet estate, near the headwaters of a small stream. To approach it, it is necessary to access the uppermost area of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization (Calviá) where we will find space to leave our vehicle in a small plot. Once past the gate of the S'Hostalet estate, we will walk along a track in continuous descent until reaching a crossroads where we will take a westerly direction for 200 meters until reaching the first left curve, at which point we will leave the path and head off-trail to the right. Within a few minutes we will cross a very small stream bed, and once on the other side we need to look for a larger stream bed that runs parallel to our path. Walking along the slope of the stream, we will have no great difficulty finding its enormous entrance. We must not confuse it with the "Avenc de N'Andreu," which is found about 25 meters before but has a small entrance.

If we decide to descend via the route detailed below, we will descend on a gentle slope for about eight or nine meters until reaching the first and only rebelay, which we will pass without difficulty to "land" on a beautiful ledge after 3 meters of descent that can serve as a waiting area for the rest of the group. The vegetation sheltered by the pothole begins to make its appearance at this spot, where the branches of a fig tree are within our reach; plants also grow on the ledge as well as in the vertical shaft. On a 19-meter-high column already visible from the ledge, small plants also grow — a certainly curious phenomenon. All that remains is a final stretch of 16 meters, most of which is a beautiful aerial descent from which one has a lovely perspective of the cavity ("Main Hall"), before we set down on the highest part of a pile of detritus. The hall in which we find ourselves, of considerable dimensions exceeding 40x40 meters, is of extraordinary beauty as it is carpeted with parietal and ceiling formations, not to mention the large stalagmitic masses and great columns that fill much of the hall and make it recognizable among all others. Of note is the great 19-meter-high column near the entrance and next to the detritus falling from outside, which is striking not only for its vertical perfection but also for the plants growing at the top to take advantage of the exterior light. The natural light from outside plays a very important role in this cave, as depending on its intensity the illumination changes, giving the main hall one appearance or another, which at the same time allows for different photographs of the same spot. We can continue descending on the left side of the detritus via a slippery ramp — especially if wet — until reaching the bottom of the hall, where moving around becomes much more comfortable. Of note is how the formations closest to the entrance of natural light are covered in green algae due to humidity and contact with the exterior; one doesn't want to think what appearance all these formations would have if they were removed from exterior light in an active state. The cavity is practically composed of just this great hall, since the small chambers that can be observed in its surroundings, mainly on the SW side, are divided by stalagmitic flowstones that form the partition. On the SW side, on the other side of the stalagmitic barrier that divides the hall, there is a chamber laden with beautiful and abundant formations. In the place where the cavity appears to end, to the right one can continue visiting the cave on two levels: the upper one ends after a left turn, and the lower one will take us to a small shaft ("Shaft Hall") from which, descending through it, we will emerge into the "Main Hall" below the stalagmitic mass. At the northern end of the great hall, at the bottom of a small shaft of approximately 7 meters, one can access without great difficulty. On the west side, behind some formations and rocks, a small ramp will place us at the top of a shaft of almost 20 meters, which one can climb down with great care, though it is best to rig a rope at its top using a natural anchor to facilitate the task. Five meters from the bottom of the shaft there is a small level, and 5 meters further down another of greater development, which we will access through a narrow hole and which marks the deepest point of the cavity. On the first level, multiple bone remains can be seen, probably belonging to some rodent (pending identification), of which no skull remains were found.

A characteristic of this cavity that differentiates it from many others in the Serra de na Burguesa is that the origin of its formation does not appear to come from a fracture in the terrain; nor does the shaft appear to have developed through dissolution, so everything indicates that it collapsed, falling upon the cavity.

The vegetation found inside this pothole is one of its great attractions, and for our part, lacking botanical knowledge, we will not go into detail until we obtain the necessary information.

Rigging I.

At the lowest part of the pothole entrance and next to the standing skeleton of a tree, there are two spits drilled into the rock that can serve to rig the rope anchor. A small descent of approximately 10 meters will place us on a small ledge of about 3 meters where we will make a rebelay by placing the second plate on a new existing spit. Once on the ledge, all that remains is the final descent, first against the wall to do the last meters aerially. Obvious friction points will require the use of protectors.

Material: 50-meter rope, 2 plates, 2 carabiners, 1 sling, 4 protectors.

Rigging II.

Starting from the same point as the previous route, this route goes almost horizontally toward the place where the fig tree grows, walking very carefully along the wall until reaching a rock where a spit is installed for the first rebelay. From this spot we will again head down a gentle ramp to descend to the lower level, where using a sling we will rig on a natural anchor twice in succession — this creates a traverse line. At the vertex of the ledge, a new rebelay on an existing spit will allow us to descend.

Material: 50-meter rope, 3 plates, 5 carabiners, 2 slings, 2 or 3 protectors.

Rigging III.

This is the most spectacular route. On the north face of the pothole, spits are installed where we will rig the anchor, and as soon as we hang on the wall we will use another to install the plate in a single aerial rebelay that, at 41.5 meters, will deposit us at the bottom of the pothole.

Material: 50-meter rope, 2 plates and two carabiners.

At least one other way of descending comes to mind, starting from the highest part of the entrance, on the west side.

In summary, a beautiful pothole suitable for all tastes, as it satisfies both the strictly sporting and the speleological aspects. If the visit to the "Avenc de sa Moneda" does not fill a full day of caving, it could be completed by visiting the "Avenc de N'Andreu," which is found just 25 meters away and which we will probably have stumbled upon. It is not of great interest.

name: Avenc de sa Soca
starting_point: Parking at Nuestra Señora de la Paz and restaurant Mirador de Na Burguesa (Génova). Follow the path past the radio station antennas towards Coll des Pastors. After the first high-voltage tower (on your left), climb a small pass and when the descent ends, leave the path — the pit is 800 meters away, off-trail.
equipment: Ropes: 1x20 m, 1x25 m, 1x70 m, several slings and carabiners for 4-5 fractionations.
description: A 103-meter deep pit in the Serra de Na Burguesa near Calvià, the second deepest known in the area, formed by tectonic fractures rather than water dissolution. The descent involves steep slippery ramps with flowstone floors and narrow passages requiring careful rope work. Formations include stalactites, curtains, stalagmites, columns, and soda straws, with the best decoration below 50 meters. Designated as a site of speleological, recreational, and biological interest.
full_description:
The morphology of the pit we are going to discuss maintains similarity with the characteristics of others in the area, among which is the Avenc de l'Infern (GRACIA 1997). The numerous pits found in the Serra de Na Burguesa owe their formation to fractures in the terrain, which makes them cavities entirely different from those existing in areas such as Escorca or Pollença, where water is the cause of their formation through the process of dissolution.

One can install on a pine tree in the surroundings to begin the vertical descent to the Sala d'Entrada (Entrance Chamber), the only place — with the exception of the deepest part of the pit — where we can maintain an upright position.

The floor of this chamber is covered with stones, earth, blocks, and tree remains, and it is also a place where life appears to be very present. Notable are multiple specimens of the spider Meta minardi, as well as chilopods (centipedes), and at the time of our visit, a specimen unknown to me of a sporocarp (fungus), for further identification. The formations in this chamber or vestibule are mainly covered with earth, and the formations that can be found in the form of wall flowstone are extremely scarce. A few meters from the main entrance, a second opening appears, impassable due to the obstruction of a rock, but it is not inconceivable that in the future there could be two accesses to the pit. The continuation of the cavity is at the southeastern end of the chamber in the form of a narrow crack that allows us to pass through crawling without major difficulty. Perhaps we can say that the cavity is divided into two parts: the entrance chamber and the rest. The first ramps are not too steep, being about 47° (CRESPÍ 2001); the floor is covered with earth and blocks at first, later becoming a slippery place also due to how wet the earth is, so the use of progression equipment can save us from a scare. Indeed, as we descend, we can observe practically the ends of the cavity where some cracks appear that lead nowhere. Formations are scarce as observed so far until reaching approximately -50 m, where on our right a corner seems to break the pattern and shows the first concretions worth mentioning.

On the main descent, the slope increases to 50° (CRESPÍ 2001). The entire floor is covered with pavement flowstone bathed in a thin layer of water that gradually soaks our clothing. Along the way, we will encounter a couple of ledges where we can, if necessary, wait for our companions. The concretion is greater at the level of the second ledge and at lower elevations until reaching the -103 m level, where blocks of stone accumulate on the floor along with the impracticable continuation of the cavity, as a block of stone prevents it. Attempting to continue without clearing the obstruction is impossible, but what is certain is that once stones are thrown, everything suggests they descend quite a few meters.

Clastic processes are clearly visible in the lower part of the cavern, where large slabs have detached from the ceiling and threaten to fall on us, were it not for the fact that they are propped up by other blocks. As for the lithochemical processes, we can find a great variety, including stalactites — some of them eccentric — curtains, stalagmites, columns, soda straws, and wall and pavement flowstone. Solifluction processes also occur in this cavity.

Great care must be taken with loose stones as they can fall on us, so when one person is descending, no one should be below unless they are in good shelter.

In summary, a cavity that exceeds one hundred meters in depth — not many reach that depth in the area; in fact, it is the second of such dimensions known to date — which despite not being rich in formations has enough to give us a good time contemplating and photographing them. At no point does the visit lose interest, as its low ceiling keeps us continuously alert, sometimes having to overcome very narrow sections, and we will certainly not emerge to the outside too clean. If the visit is well utilized, the Avenc de sa Soca can occupy us for a full day, but if that is not the case, less than thirty meters away is the claustrophobic Cova des Corbs (463465 4379473 ED50), and a little further away the Avenc des Cans (463478 4379538 ED50).

The Avenc de sa Soca is considered a site of speleological, recreational, and biological interest.

name: Avenc de sa Sorpresa
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint in Génova. Follow the path toward Coll des Pastors, then head toward Coll des Vent, detouring at a pass on the slope of Puig d'en Bou
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 462832 E, 4380171 N. DATUM WGS84: 462730 E, 4379971 N
equipment: Vertical progression equipment and a rope of about 20 m
description: A small but interesting chasm on Puig d'en Bou that likely functions as a natural sinkhole. The entrance (0.90 x 0.50 m) leads to a 5 m descent into a single small chamber with parietal flowstone, beautiful concretions, and a striking column affected by solifluction. Considered a natural site of interest, it can be combined with a visit to a larger nearby cave.
full_description:
Located on Puig d'en Bou is this small but interesting chasm which, although it will certainly not occupy us for an entire day, we can take advantage of a visit to another more substantial cave to then approach it. In my particular case, I visited it after finishing my visit to the Cova d'en Palem.

Located in a small depression in the terrain, this chasm originated from a fracture of the ground, like many others in the area, and may function as a sinkhole for the location. With dimensions of 0.90 x 0.50 m, the entrance is located right at the edge of a small path that departs from the main trail, and therefore finding it will cause us no difficulty even if we go without a GPS.

A tree somewhat removed from the chasm will allow us to set up the rope before beginning the descent into its interior through a shaft of some narrowness that will deposit us at the bottom after a descent of approximately five meters, to then, once "landed," walk through the only small chamber through which we will travel westward along a slight slope whose floor is covered with everything the water drags in from the outside.

As we head toward the back of the chasm, we can observe a wall flowstone that covers the left side of the chasm, although the other side is by no means lacking in concretions — in fact, that side is much more interesting. The deepest part of the cavity is the most ornate, featuring a beautiful column affected by the solifluction process more than half a meter in the vertical direction, not to mention a precious efflorescence that was found in a corner of the chasm.

In summary, a small chasm that is well worth visiting.

The Avenc de sa Sorpresa is considered a natural site of interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory list of natural underground spaces of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

name: Avenc de Son Nassi
starting_point: Restaurant Can Penaso on the Ma-11 road from Palma to Sóller. Park at the restaurant, walk along the road towards Sóller to km 15.800, then leave the road to enter the s'Alqueria estate (permission required).
equipment: Vertical progression equipment plus one rope of approximately 25 m.
description: A 14-meter deep pit on the slopes of Puig de Son Nassi with two entrances connected by a cracked rock bridge threatening collapse. Inside, a remarkable fig tree has grown from a seed germinating 4-5 meters above the floor, extending its roots down to reach the ground with an unusually large trunk. The cave walls are well-decorated with speleothems, and the pit drains water through narrow openings. Designated as a site of special speleological interest.
full_description:
When on November 24, 2005, while hiking, it occurred to me to climb to the summit of the Puig de Son Nassi accompanied by my wife, we passed by the pit in question, but it never crossed my mind that I would end up descending into its interior to explore it and help with its survey, since at that time I had not yet made the decision to practice speleology. Finally, two years later, accompanied by J. A. Encinas on a rainy day, we decided to venture into its depths without knowing if that pit I had found by chance was really anything more than a simple hole. It was interesting to discover, leaning over one of its two openings, the bottom of the pit, giving us already from above the impression that it had been worth climbing up to it, and even more so when, to our surprise, we found another one just a few meters away.

Since the pit is situated very close to the saddle separating the Puig de s'Alqueria from the Puig de Son Nassi, it is possible that its formation began with a tearing of the latter mountain due to the pressure exerted by the weight of the Puig de s'Alqueria upon it; the water surely did the rest.

Once again the fig tree is omnipresent in a pit, being in this case one of the most beautiful, if not the most beautiful, since the seed germinated just about 4 or 5 meters from the bottom on a rock, forcing the tree to extend its roots over the rock until finding the floor of the pit, while at the same time growing and thickening until reaching a trunk of considerable dimensions never seen by me inside a cavity.

A holm oak, if I remember correctly, will serve us to secure the rope and begin the descent into the interior of the pit, at one point passing our body between the wall and the trunk of the fig tree, so one must be careful to pass the rope through that spot beforehand. Once at the bottom, one can appreciate the dimensions of the cavity, which at this point are approximately seven meters and thirty centimeters long by about three meters wide at its widest part.

Looking toward the exterior, we can also observe how the two entrance openings of this pit are separated by a kind of bridge, with the connecting rock cracked and threatening to break at any moment. We can also see that the walls of the pit, except for the side where the fig tree has grown, are well decorated with concretions, giving the pit a cozy appearance despite, as I said before, being threatened by the possible collapse of the rock that separates the two entrances.

Once the exploration of the pit has begun, one can quickly verify that at one of the two ends and in each of the two corners, the pit has continuity, with water draining through them from the slopes of the Puig de Son Nassi. Through the crack on the left side, we quickly realize that besides being inaccessible to us, if we throw a stone into it, the depth is small, although one can never be sure of that since large blocks of stone have fallen from the outside at this location, probably causing its obstruction. In the other corner on the same side, however, the opening that provides continuity to the pit is of larger dimensions, inviting exploration, since we have also been able to verify that at least five or six meters vertically it extends further. The problem is that after trying, we were unable to access its interior to continue the exploration, as the slimmest among us, after trying insistently, had to give up.

The Avenc de Son Nassi is considered of special speleological interest.

Near this pit is the Avenc des Coll de Son Nassi, which we can visit to complete our stay in the area.

name: Avenc de Son Pou
starting_point: Km 8.450 of the Orient to Alaró road
footwear: Good mountain boots are always recommended
clothing: No special clothing needed to visit the cave via the artificial entrance. For descending from the natural mouth, an approximately 50m free-hanging rappel is required.
route_type: Vertical if accessing from the natural mouth of the cave
approach_time: 2 hours and 30 minutes approximately, walking only
equipment: For descent from the natural entrance (~55m free-hanging), full vertical progression equipment is needed. For the artificial entrance, only flashlights or headlamps are necessary.
description: A massive cave shaped like a hollow pumpkin with a natural opening at the top and an artificial tunnel for easier access. The main chamber is enormous (5,000 sq m) with a 50m high ceiling, though many stalactites have been damaged by vandalism. The cave hosts endemic species including the Henrotius jordai beetle and Roncus neotropicus pseudoscorpion, and is classified under the Red Natura 2000 network.
full_description:
The Avenc de Son Pou, to put it simply, is like a hollow pumpkin, with an opening in the upper central part which is what we could call the natural entrance. However, since visiting the cavity through the natural entrance would require a free-hanging rappel of 55 m to reach the bottom, a tunnel has been drilled through the rock that allows access to the interior of the cave on foot.

The tunnel that brought us into the interior of the cavity was reportedly built to extract guano (bat and bird excrement) from inside the cave, although other people claim that the real intention was to control the water from the spring that was located inside.

Once inside the cavity, the chamber we find ourselves in is of gigantic dimensions (5,000 sq m), although unfortunately I must say that the impact of human activity on the place is all too evident, as there are many decapitated stalactites and even on some walls there remain traces of graffiti made by uncivil people who fail to understand the importance of this cave.

The ceiling of the cave is 50 meters high, so it seems to have no end, while at the same time we discover our own smallness before so much beauty that overwhelms us from all sides. It occurs to me, as I glimpse a tree in the distance outside the cavity, how wonderful it must be to do a free-hanging rappel from the 'small' mouth down to where I am standing.

Now that I am beginning the adventure of exploring cavities on our island of Mallorca, I find myself wondering what awaits me from now on, since already in this first cave all kinds of sensations are welling up in me, all of them wonderful.

The exploration of the cave, which consists of two additional rooms, presents practically no difficulty to visit, since as I said before it is a kind of hollow pumpkin in which, looking from the entrance straight ahead and slightly to the right, there is a kind of passageway that gives access to another much smaller chamber with a fairly high ceiling, on whose walls the effect of water has left its mark with a beautiful flowstone.

Quality

The cavity is home to the endemic troglobites Henrotius jordai (beetle) and Roncus neotropicus (pseudoscorpion). The subterranean habitat has great biological importance in the Balearic Islands, as it acts as a refuge habitat for many species of troglobitic invertebrates. Some of these species are very localized endemisms and some are true relict species from the Tertiary period, which have survived climatic changes in this environment where humidity and temperature remain nearly constant.

The quality information belongs to the Natura 2000 Network.

In summary, it is a simple cave to visit if we do so through the access excavated in the rock, in which the process of stalactite and stalagmite formation has practically stopped, but which is well worth visiting. On this website there is a described excursion in the hiking section that describes how to access the cavity.

name: Avenc de l'Infern
elevation_change: Not relevant; only a small slope near the pit
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 462620 E, 4380700 N. DATUM ETRS89: 462526 E, 4380495 N
equipment: Several short ropes, some carabiners and slings
description: At -132 m, this is the deepest known cave in the Serra de Na Burguesa. Despite requiring vertical progression equipment, it lacks major vertical drops and reaches its lowest point in a rock-filled chamber containing the most beautiful formations and ancient paleolevel marks. The cave is filled with loose rocks throughout, making it one of the most dangerous cavities in the range alongside Avenc des Pastor.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

The description I am going to give of this cavity corresponds to the Vía dets Antics (Route of the Ancients) since it is the only one I have done of the two possible routes.

Once in the Sala d'Entrada (Entrance Hall), we will head to the deepest area of it where two old pitons are installed that can be used (at least as of 2010) to descend through a small hole that gives continuity to the descent.

This cavity, at -132 m, is the deepest known to date in the Serra de Na Burguesa. However, despite the fact that the use of vertical progression equipment is necessary, it lacks major vertical drops, and the deepest zone is reached in a chamber where a great quantity of rocks accumulates. In the deepest zone of the chamber are found the most beautiful formations of the chasm, where ancient paleolevels can also be contemplated.

One of the characteristics of this cavity, and perhaps where its name comes from, is the great quantity of rocks it harbors in its interior, which are a danger to be taken into account. Together with the Avenc des Pastor, it is one of the most dangerous cavities of the Serra de Na Burguesa.

Note: If you wish to visit the chasm via the new route, which does not require a rope, you can do so from the Sala d'Entrada. You can obtain all the information in ENDINS 21, where you will also find the survey.

name: Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization. Drive to the top, park at the end of Calle de la Televisión, continue on Camí des Ri de ses Creus to a V-junction, take the right branch toward Coll del Pastors
best_season: Any, though avoid summer if possible
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 461100 E, 4379715 N. DATUM ETRS89: 461193 E, 4379919 N
equipment: One 40 m rope, 2 hangers for the two bolts, sling for the rebelay on rock bridge
description: A 38 m deep predominantly vertical cave on the south slope of Puig de Na Boira, explored in January 2010. The 4x6 m entrance leads to a 22 m long chamber illuminated by natural light, with red earth and scattered formations. The descent continues through an extremely narrow fracture lined with flowstone, requiring removal of equipment at the tightest point. A dry stone wall near the entrance was built as an ancient shelter, and the cave is rated as a site of speleological interest.
full_description:
Located on the southern slope of Puig de Na Boira, this chasm was discovered by J. V. Pardo. Together with the neighboring cavity "Escletxa dets Ossos," the Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat has a different morphology depending on which area we are talking about, since the most external part of it, and always in my opinion, is different from the fracture zone where most of the chasm develops.

Once J. V. Pardo completed the clearing work, all that remained was to enter its depths to carry out the exploration work, which happened on January 31, 2010, in the company of members of the speleological group VOLTORS (Palma). With the cave explored, its survey still remained pending. For this purpose, on May 5, 2010, we set to work on the survey, dividing the work into two groups so that the deepest and most demanding part of the cavity was handled by R. Minguillón and J. V. Pardo, and the rest of the cavity by yours truly accompanied by L. Morante.

The entrance, mostly hidden by bushes, is about 4 x 6 meters. Through it we will comfortably access the interior of the cavity, composed of a chamber about 22 meters long and a little more than half a dozen meters wide; the maximum height does not exceed 5 meters. The outside light illuminates the interior of the chamber almost entirely, allowing one to appreciate the intense red color of the earth which, accompanied by rocks of different sizes, forms parts of the walls as well as the ceiling. There are not many formations to be found in this area of the chasm, and those that exist are in the most western part of the chamber, formed by small stalactites, the occasional curtain, as well as wall and floor flowstones. Also noteworthy is the abundant wall concretion in the eastern area, the place where the cave continues.

The crack through which we begin the descent does not exceed one meter in width at its widest part. The walls are draped in wall flowstones and it is manageable without vertical progression equipment down to -10 meters, where we will set up the rope using two small stalagmites that are at a little more than half a meter in height. The narrowest part of this already narrow cavity is found right at this point, since to access the small ledge about five meters below, depending on the build of the speleologist, it may be necessary to remove part of the vertical progression equipment to get through this extremely tight passage. A curious anecdote happened to us at this location. Once past the narrow passage and as I was saying before, we will position ourselves on a small ledge of approximately two meters where the ground, covered with damp earth, is slippery and can be dangerous if we are not attached to our rope. The rest of the cavity follows the vertical fracture where, hidden among a chaos of rocks, small stalactites develop as well as curtains, some of them of considerable size. In the deepest part of the cavity, rocks abound as well as earth that in some cases covers them; some bone remains are also found in this place. Wall flowstones are abundant throughout the entire descent since the walls remain covered by a thin film of water.

Inside the cavity and near the entrance, in a corner, the construction of a dry stone wall conditioned this place to serve as shelter and protect from the inclement weather those who once used it. This particular area was possibly used because venturing into the western zone caused some fear due to the possibility of ceiling collapse.

Perhaps the cave may not be enough to occupy a full day, but that should not worry us since in the surroundings we have enough cavities to keep us busy for more than one day, notably the "Cova/avenc de na Picacento," "Sa Escletxa dets Ossos" which is found just over a dozen meters away, "Cova de ses Cabres," "Avenc des Xuclamel" located near the summit of Puig de Na Boira, and why not the "Cova del Ferro" which we pass on the way back.

The Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat is considered a site of speleological interest.

name: Avenc d'en Corbera
starting_point: Jardín de Flores urbanization (Ses Rotgetes de Canet), km 1 of MA-120 road between Esporlas and MA-1130 Palma-Valldemossa road
clothing: Coverall essential due to heavy mud
equipment: Vertical progression equipment, 20m and 35m ropes, 2 protectors, 5 carabiners. Expansion bolts are usually already installed.
description: A popular cave near Esporles, frequently used for caving courses due to its easy access. It begins with an 18m practice shaft, then descends through a second pit with two rigging points and an aerial 10m descent. The Great Hall leads via a crawl to the 75m Sala des Fang (Mud Hall). A 7m rope climb accesses a 30m upper gallery with formations. The deepest point (-41m) is in the Guano Halls, where bat colonies have declined.
full_description:
Near the town of Esporles is this interesting cavity, very frequently visited mainly due to its easy access as well as its technical characteristics, since it begins with a shaft of approximately 18 m that has served on many occasions for practice during the various caving courses that have been held on the island.

Once at the bottom, we will approach the first landing through a considerable slope, and after a downclimb of approximately a couple of meters, we will reach the second landing, from which we will descend to the second shaft of the day, where we will need to set up a couple of rebelays, performing an aerial descent of about ten meters after the second one.

Once in the Great Hall, we will advance to the back of it, looking for the crawl of about five meters through a gentle slope that will allow us to access the second hall called Sala des Fang (Mud Hall), of large dimensions, approximately 75 m. As we move through it, we will have to walk very carefully, watching not to step into any of the trenches found in the floor. On the right side of this hall, between two columns, we can find a tied rope (on the day of our visit, the rope was cut about 3 m from the ground) that will allow us to access the upper gallery after overcoming about seven meters of height. The Upper Gallery, about thirty meters long and of quite uneven width, is mainly concretioned in its final section.

Once the Upper Gallery has been finished and photographed, we must return again to the Sala des Fang to continue our visit. We will head to the back of it to begin ascending a steep slope covered with stalagmites and impressive columns, where the only care we need to take is not to slip since the floor is covered in mud. Once on the other side of the columns, and at the end of the slope, we can access, if we wish, some small chambers after crossing a sea of blocks.

On the way back to the entrance hall, we can visit the Guano Halls, named for the large amount of guano that can still be found in this place, although at present everything seems to indicate that the number of bats has declined — specifically, we could only observe one pair. Climbing first through the cone and then descending steeply to the deepest part of the cavity at -41 m, we will pass through a kind of arch that marks the beginning of the Guano Halls. I began the tour on the left side, climbing through the chaos of blocks. First, we find a small room also on the left side, lacking interest. We continue climbing while multiple concretions begin to appear, notable among them some beautiful parietal flowstones found in the upper part of the cave, which rises to about a meter and a half from the surface. The descent can be made by the same route or by looking for other alternatives.

For further reading:

Daniel Mayoral, Tomeu Mateu. MALLORCA BEAUTY IN THE DARKNESS. Published by Espeleo Mallorca SC 2006.

name: Avenc d'en Miquel
starting_point: Parking area at the top of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: 2 ropes of 30 m each, 1 bolt plate, several slings and protectors
description: A structural cave at the eastern end of the Pla de s'Aljub Gros with a main chamber of 16 x 21 x 3 m at a 35° slope. A shaft leads to an intermediate level and a larger lower level (4.5 x 18 x 12 m) of greater speleological interest. The cave is neither comfortable nor easy to navigate due to large unstable blocks and loose rocks, especially between the upper and lower levels.
full_description:
Description

At the eastern end of the Pla de s'Aljub Gros, this cavity of structural origin is found, formed by a main chamber of 16 x 21 x 3 m with an average slope of 35°. During the exploration and surveying work, a shaft was discovered in the eastern area, which has allowed the dimensions of the cavity to be extended, reaching a depth of 37 m. Ten meters from the start of the shaft, as a result of collapses that have occurred over time, there is a small level (Intermediate Level), and 16 m further down a larger one measuring 4.5 x 18 x 12 m, which we have called the Lower Level, of greater dimensions than the Intermediate and of greater speleological interest.

Although reconstructive processes are not very abundant, they have developed both on the Upper Level and especially on the Lower Level.

It should be noted that the Avenc d'en Miquel is neither a comfortable nor easy cavity to traverse. On the contrary, due to its current morphology in which large blocks and, perhaps worse, stones of all sizes threaten the visitor, especially in the section between the Upper Level and the Lower Level.

Rigging

A spit was installed at the top of the shaft, so that is the place where the required plate must be placed.

Note: The day's outing can be completed with a visit to the Cova Fosca, which is found in the surrounding area.

name: Avenc des Bolets
starting_point: MA-10 road, km 17.4, about 100m from the Manut junction. Follow the Lluc path.
access: Own vehicle or bus to Lluc monastery
equipment: Vertical progression equipment and a 30m rope. No installation hardware needed as the rope can be tied to a tree.
description: A 22-meter completely vertical pit near Lluc monastery, suitable for less experienced cavers. Located just 5 meters off a popular walking path, it is easy to find. The more interesting descent involves a small ledge and a division in the shaft where a rigging point can be created. Recognized as a natural site of interest and catalogued by the Nord de Mallorca group.
full_description:
Very close to the Lluc monastery is this interesting pit (sinkhole), near one of the paths used by visitors to the monastery for walks, making it not at all difficult to find.

The vehicle can be parked very close to the path heading toward Lluc, where there is space for a couple of vehicles just a few meters from where we need to start walking. If the spot were occupied, we would take the road to Manut and Binifaldo (signposted) and park where it wouldn't be in the way, since the road is very narrow while at the same time used by trucks from the Binifaldo bottling plant.

With its approximately 22 meters of completely vertical drop on one of its sides, it is a suitable pit for practice, mainly for people without much experience, as is my case.
