However, it is even more interesting to descend the pit from the side where you have to go down to a small ledge just over a meter in height relative to the exterior, where the rope can be securely fixed to a holm oak near the mouth. If we descend from this side, about four meters from the bottom we will encounter a kind of division of the shaft where we can set up a rebelay by tying the rope around the rock and descend to the deeper side of the shaft, which is the side closest to the wall we descended along. Once at the bottom, the other side of the pit can be seen through a small crack.

The Avenc des Bolets is recognized as a natural site of interest and was catalogued by the Nord de Mallorca group. It is located very close to the Cova de sa Cometa des Mort, which we can visit to complete the day's outing. And if that is still not enough, also not far from here, just behind the Coll de sa Bataia gas station, only 5 minutes away, there is the cave of the same name, which can be accessed without any type of equipment.

name: Avenc des Cabrit o des Xinxoram
starting_point: Coll Baix path, km 4.800 (measured from Bar Bodega del Sol).
equipment: Ropes: 1x30 m, 1x10 m. 1 protector. 2 bolts. Oxygen bottle for the CO2 Chamber.
description: A predominantly vertical cave near the Alcúdia cliffs with multiple chambers at different levels. Notable features include the Flag Chamber with beautiful large curtain formations and wall flowstone, and a CO2 Chamber with diverse speleothems but dangerously high carbon dioxide levels (7.35%). A gas detector and oxygen supply are essential for the deepest section, which has a very narrow entrance suitable only for slim cavers.
full_description:
Description

The cavity is located just about fifty meters from the cliff and close to the trail that runs toward the Cova Tancada des Cap de Menorca. A descent of 5 m places us in a small chamber of X x X x 5 m where sparse formations as well as some roots stand out. At one end of the small chamber, a gallery ramp of approximately 6 m in length positions us on a flowstone, marking the beginning of the descent to the Sala de ses Banderoles (Flag Chamber), which is located 10 m below.

Saleta de s'Entrada (Entrance Chamber)

Of reduced size, 1 x 3 x 5 m, the only things noteworthy are some formations and several cupolas. This is one of the two sinks responsible for the development of the cavity.

Sala de ses Banderoles (Flag Chamber)

With a NE-SW orientation, the chamber reaches approximate dimensions of 5.5 x 11 x 10 m. The reconstructive processes in this chamber are abundant, notable among them beautiful curtains of considerable size as well as a wall flowstone that covers the entire side through which one descends. A new widening in the deepest zone of the chamber allowed us to descend to...

Racó dets Ossos (Bones Corner)

A very beautiful corner in the SW zone of the Flag Chamber, which one must climb to reach, conceals the bone remains of a young goat that presumably fell through the chimney, survived the fall, and ended its days taking shelter in this place, hidden behind beautiful formations. At present, rodents have established their residence there.

Sala des CO2 (CO2 Chamber)

With dimensions of 8.30 x 9 x 8.65, and a NW-SE orientation, this is a chamber where constructive processes abound with a great variety of speleothems. Naturally, the name assigned to it corresponds to the high concentration of CO2 it harbors inside — 7.35% on the day the survey of the chamber was conducted.

Note: To visit this cavity, it is essential to always be accompanied by a gas detector. Accessing the CO2 Chamber is very difficult, since in addition to the challenges presented by the access opening to this chamber (suitable only for slim-build cavers), it will be necessary to carry an oxygen bottle due to the high concentration of CO2.

name: Avenc des Cans
starting_point: Parking near Nuestra Señora de la Paz and Mirador de Na Burguesa restaurant (Génova). Follow the path past the radio antennas toward Coll des Pastors. After the first high-voltage tower (on the left), climb a small hill — the pothole is about 800 m off-trail.
equipment: One rope of about 40 m, 2 protectors
description: Named after dogs that fell to their deaths inside — their remains are scattered in the deepest zones. The nearly circular entrance is hidden by a pine tree and low scrub. A steep descent (60°+ slope) over fractured flowstone leads to a chamber with large collapsed blocks. The deepest section features varied formations including columns, stalactites, stalagmites, and curtain formations.
full_description:
A BIT OF HISTORY

A hunter once told me about the existence of a terrifying pothole from whose great entrance much heat emerged and occasionally a small column of smoke. After he "described" to me the place where it was located, I devoted myself body and soul to finding it, it being the pothole that has cost me the most to find — not because it is excessively difficult but because of the inability some people have to describe a location. Once located, I immediately realized it was the "Avenc des Cans," of whose existence I already had news but had not yet had time to search for. All that remained was to take the coordinates and confirm it, then leave it on the waiting list for when its time would come, which happened on June 5, 2010, the first time.

DESCRIPTION

The nearly circular entrance of this pothole is partly hidden by a small pine tree and the rest of the low scrub vegetation typical of the mountain range where it is located. We can rig from the aforementioned pine tree, without forgetting to immediately place a first protector, and a little further down a second one on the opposite wall on a kind of ledge — so to speak, since it is practically nothing. A deviator can be placed, though finding a gap to tie the sling to is somewhat difficult, mainly because it is so hidden. The descent down the ramp of more than 60° inclination in a SE-NW direction presents no problems; we should only note that we must veer to the right to avoid having to make a rebelay on a rock bridge. Our feet walk over flowstone pavements in which significant fractures have occurred until we reach solid ground. We find ourselves on blocks of rock covered with earth that have been progressively breaking off while also covering the cavity, reaching approximately nine meters of thickness at this point. If we head in a NE direction going uphill, we will encounter a barrier of blocks that have accumulated from collapses. One stands out above all the others for its size and for the fear that it might come loose, but it is wedged in such a way that it does not seem possible. We can scramble over the blocks until reaching the end of the cavity on this side. Returning to the spot where the descent rope is, we have no choice but to descend a dirt ramp of slightly more than 40° inclination until reaching the bottom. From this point, where small ceiling formations can already be seen, the cavity continues skirting the ramp coming from the entrance area, with the particularity that passage becomes more difficult due to the narrowness that forces us to take certain precautions, as we will need to use our hands in some cases, and the water covering part of the flowstones at least creates a risk of slipping. The final section is where the greatest quantity of varied formations is found, from columns of every thickness and size to stalactites, stalagmites, and curtain formations. The return is made by retracing our steps.

FAUNA

The fauna found in the pothole is scarce. Some insects, among them a specimen of beetle, and from the arthropod family, a myriapod was found.

TO CONCLUDE

Like other cavities of the "Serra de na Burguesa," the "Avenc des Cans" is the typical cavity produced by a SE-NW fracture that begins at the entrance with an inclination exceeding 60°. The access ramp exceeds 30 m, and the length from one end of the level to the other exceeds 50 m.

The name of the cavity is related to the dogs (cans) that had the misfortune of falling inside, and whose remains are scattered in the deepest areas.

It is possible that the visit to the pothole will not fill a full caving day, which should not worry us at all since nearby there are other cavities to visit, such as the "Avenc de sa Soca" (463442, 4379473) with -102 m and the "Cova des Corbs" (463465, 4379473), a cave that will test our abilities to navigate claustrophobic places. The DATUM for the coordinates is ED50.

name: Avenc des Cérvol
starting_point: Son Vida urbanization. Drive to the end of Carrer d'Albercuix (ends at a barrier), park on the side, and walk down to the third lamppost. The trail begins behind it, near number 22.
equipment: 45 m rope, 3 slings, and 3 carabiners
description: The deepest cavity in the Serra de Son Camps at -34 m, featuring a steep, slippery descent with sparse formations. The most decorated section lies near the exit behind a narrow passage, with abundant flags, stalactites, stalagmites, columns, and flowstones affected by solifluction. Named after deer bones found at its base, it likely connects to the nearby Avenc des Coll de Son Camps, only about 10 m away.
full_description:
Of all the cavities that to this day are located in the Sierra de Son Camps, the "Avenc des Cérvol" or "Deer Pit" is the one with the greatest depth and development. The entrance to the cavity is oriented SW-NW with dimensions of 3 x 1.40 m. The rope installation can be done on a holm oak in front of the entrance, placing a sling on the large rock that partially blocks the entrance to prevent rope abrasion. The descent along a slippery and steep slope is fairly well defined, running through an area with few formations, with some groups of stalagmites and small stalactites scattered across the ceiling standing out. A little further down we need to install a new rebelay on a natural anchor on the ceiling, and another at the same spot just before a drop of about two meters that brings us to the deepest level of the cavity at -34 m. In this part of the cavity, among a great chaos of rocks that have accumulated over time, recent bones of at least a couple of dogs are clearly visible, as well as, at the right end, possibly in an evident state of deterioration, those of the deer that gives the cavity its name. The visit to the cave can be started from the deepest area while heading toward the exit, having to leave the rope and explore the most interesting area which is to the right, not too far from the exit. This area of the cavity is found behind a narrow passage that we will access after overcoming a couple of meters of elevation change. We find ourselves in the most decorated part of the cave, where a large number of curtains, stalactites, stalagmites, columns, and beautiful floor flowstones abound. Descending a little further we will encounter a stalagmitic barrier affected by the vertical solifluction process; next to it is a column also affected by the same process, in this case additionally with horizontal displacement. It is not unusual to find soda straw stalactites in this area.

The "Avenc des Cérvol" is located very close to the "Avenc des Coll de Son Camps"; in fact, the distance separating them through a chaos of blocks is approximately 10 m, which strongly suggests that it is the same cavity. Miquel Alexandre 2002.

name: Avenc des Cocarroi
starting_point: Km 14.300 of the Ma-10 road
equipment: Vertical progression gear and a 20 m rope. No installation equipment needed as the rope can be fixed to rocks.
description: A modest 11 m deep pothole in the karstic landscape of Escorca, notable for its entrance being completely concealed by ivy covering its walls and floor, giving it an air of mystery. The vertical walls show no formations but have water channels carved into them. The cave may have historically served as a snow pit, evidenced by carefully placed stones covering a drain. Wild goats occasionally fall in, and the trip can be combined with an ascent of nearby Puig de Femenias.
full_description:
The place where this sinkhole is located can be defined as a place where the karstic landscapes, shaped mainly by the dissolution of rocks by water that through filtration form a drainage system where numerous caves and sinkholes develop.

This pothole has the peculiarity that its mouth is covered by ivy that is adhered to its walls in such a way that it is impossible to see the bottom despite its wide mouth. With an approximate depth of a little more than 11 meters, the walls are completely vertical without any concretions, although several channels are carved by the water sliding down its walls.

It is worth noting that the ivy is not only found in the upper part of the pothole but has covered the entire surface of both the walls and the bottom, giving it an air of a certain halo of mystery, although a very pleasant one. The bottom is horizontal with a drain on its eastern side covered with some well-placed stones, enough for me to dare to say that we were not the first to have accessed the pothole, as it may have been used as a snow house (a pit for storing snow) in other times, a trade that has now disappeared.

This type of potholes also inadvertently serve as true traps for the feral goats that roam the Serra de Tramuntana, since, without going any further, the remains of two of them were found at the bottom, and in the nearby pothole another one had also fallen in.

The day can be complemented by ascending Puig de Femenias, which is found on the way back before descending through the pass that allows us to reach the lower level, with the visible summit that we will climb 'rough-style,' that is, without any type of markers signaling the path. We also have a small pothole 169 m away, already on the way back, at coordinates 490460 4412646 DATUM EUROPEAN 1950, which we can also access through its thick mantle of ivy.

name: Avenc des Coll de Son Nassi
name_english: Pit of the Coll de Son Nassi Pass
starting_point: Restaurant Can Penaso on the Ma-11 road from Palma to Sóller. Park at the restaurant, walk along the road towards Sóller to km 15.800, then leave the road to enter the s'Alqueria estate (permission required).
equipment: Vertical progression equipment plus one rope of approximately 25 m.
description: A 15-meter deep pit discovered by chance on the saddle between Puig de s'Alqueria and Puig de Son Nassi, likely formed by tectonic fracturing and water action. It consists of two sections: an initial 6-meter descent requiring no special installation (an oak tree serves as an anchor), followed by a parallel second section of 9 meters in three drops with more speleothem decoration. Designated as being of special speleological interest.
full_description:
It was a lucky day when we found the Avenc des Coll de Son Nassi, since on our way back from exploring and surveying the Avenc de Son Nassi, and also because we are always on the lookout for any sign that catches our attention, we found the pit by chance without expecting it.

Since the pit is located very close to the saddle separating the Puig de s'Alqueria from the Puig de Son Nassi, it is possible that its formation began with a tearing of this mountain due to the pressure exerted by the weight of the Puig de s'Alqueria on that of Son Nassi; the water surely did the rest, both in this pit and in its neighbor, the Avenc de Son Nassi.

With an opening 1.5 m long and 1 m wide, there opens a pit with a depth of -6 m that presents no difficulty for descent, making it unnecessary to bring installation equipment since there is a holm oak next to the entrance that will allow us to secure the rope. From outside the pit, one can observe that slightly more than a meter above the bottom of the pit there is another opening that allows the continuation of the pit, so once we have descended the 6 meters mentioned above, we will have to climb without major difficulty to the base of the opening that will allow us to continue the exploration.

9 meters remain for us to descend in 3 drops to reach the bottom of the pit, which is not located below the first section but rather this second part of the pit runs parallel and is sufficiently decorated with concretions, unlike the first section.

The Avenc des Coll de Son Nassi is considered of special speleological interest.

name: Avenc des Feixos
starting_point: Short access: Recreation area of the Comuna de Bunyola. Long access: Entrance of the Son Torrella estate in Santa Maria (479740 4391989 WGS84).
elevation_change: Short access: not relevant. Long access: 220m
approach_time: Minutes (short access); approximately 4 hours round trip (long access)
equipment: 1 rope of 40m, 2 slings, carabiners
description: A beautiful 35m deep chasm with mostly free-hanging descent, formed through a mix of water dissolution and tectonic fracturing related to a nearby cliff. The interior features smooth walls with sinusoidal morphology and attractive formations. The headwall is rigged from a nearby pine tree with a redirect to center the rope over the opening.
full_description:
Description

A beautiful chasm of only 35 m in depth (almost all of them in free air), the result of a mixed genesis in which, although we can say that initially its origin was formed due to the dissolution of the rock as a consequence of rainwater charged with CO₂ that it collected from the slope, subsequently a possible extensional fracture, related to the cliff located just a few meters from the chasm, aided in its development until reaching its current state, in which smooth walls and sinusoidal morphology can be appreciated inside.

Noteworthy in this chasm are the beautiful formations that decorate it, making it well worth the visit.

Installation

Once the head of the chasm is rigged on a pine tree located near the mouth, it is re-secured, and with a sling a redirect is installed so that the rope hangs vertically in the center of the mouth. This will avoid having to use protectors and will allow us to descend directly.

name: Avenc des Garrover
starting_point: La Vileta Cemetery
access: EMT Bus Line 8 or own vehicle
equipment: Ropes: 1x20m, 1x10m, 2 protectors, 2 slings and 2 carabiners
description: A beautiful collapse cave named after the small carob tree growing at its circular entrance, located just 75m from Avenc des Matoll de sa Figuera. The main Column Hall features impressive columns and decorated walls, while the lower Gour Hall contains notable gour formations. An upper room shows dolomitic breccias and a tepee-shaped low ceiling formed by a longitudinal fracture.
full_description:
Description:

Forgotten in a zone prolific in natural cavities, mines, and quarries, just 75 m from the Avenc des Matoll de sa Figuera, also known as Avenc de ses Figueres, this beautiful collapse cavity is located. With a small circular mouth, inside it grows a young pine tree in addition to the small carob tree that gives the cavity its name. Everything seems to indicate that the mouth was modified, although the reason is unknown — probably mining.

Column Hall (Sala de ses Columnes)

After a 10 m descent, six of them in free air, we will find ourselves in the Column Hall, on a conical structure of debris from which the entire hall can be contemplated in its entirety, measuring 10 x 11 x 6 m. Two of the columns stand out for their dimensions. Both the ceiling and wall areas of the hall are beautifully concretioned, with a beautiful flowstone standing out in the NW zone that hides a small room, the Bat Room (Saleta de ses Ratespinyades), frequently visited by said mammals. To the south of the descent point, in a small corner, an alveolus or corrosion cupola can be observed.

Gour Hall (Sala des Gours)

After crossing the hall toward the 'window' located to the NE and climbing about 3 m, we will position ourselves on a kind of 'balcony,' from which we can access the Gour Hall, either by downclimbing or by setting up an installation, to overcome the 4 m that separate us from it. This is a hall of 10 x 20 x 4 m, in which besides the small group of columns, a set of gours is worth noting. In the southern zone, a small corner hides that is not of great interest (Es Racó).

Upper Room (Saleta d'Amunt)

The Upper Room is accessed from a zone of blocks. From there we must climb a small ramp and from it, after a delicate ascent, we will find ourselves in a low-ceilinged room with a tepee shape. A longitudinal fracture along the ceiling, currently obstructed, may have been the cause of the formation of this room, which is scarce in formations. Dolomitic breccias can be appreciated on the ceiling of the room.

name: Avenc des Mort
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint, next to the monument of Our Lady of Peace (Génova)
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 462539 E, 4379714 N. DATUM ETRS89: 462445 E, 4379509 N
equipment: 2 carabiners, 2 ropes of 25 m, 2 slings (one for a deviation, one for the rebelay)
description: One of the most beautiful caves in the Serra de Na Burguesa despite its modest -30.5 m depth. Features a scenic 12 m aerial descent and richly decorated chambers with eccentric stalactites, numerous curtain formations, and hidden alcoves. In 1978, human remains were found of a person who apparently died of starvation. The visit can be combined with the nearby Cova des Ribellet.
full_description:
Much less well-known than other chasms in this mountain range such as the Avenc de s'Embut or the Avenc de sa Moneda, the Avenc des Mort is without a doubt one of the most beautiful cavities found in the Serra de Na Burguesa, despite not being a cave of large dimensions or even great depth at -30.5 m. Nevertheless, it meets the necessary conditions for spending a good while caving, where we can enjoy a beautiful descent into its depths in which we will perform a 12-meter aerial traverse, as well as being able to scrutinize beautiful hidden corners and spending hours firing our camera.

The installation can be set up on a pine tree near the larger of the two mouths the chasm has, then placing a deviation on a wild olive tree at the head of the ramp that will allow us to access the shaft after installing a first and last rebelay on formations securely. The descent, in a beautiful aerial position, will deposit us first on a stalagmitic mass and shortly after on unstable ground due to the large number of rocks that cover the floor of the cavity.

As soon as we free ourselves from the rope, we can begin to marvel at everything that surrounds us, which is a great deal since the lithochemical process has covered this place with all types of formations.

We can begin the visit by descending along an unstable ramp until reaching the deepest part of the cavity where there are beautifully adorned corners with small gours (dry at the time of my visit) near the place where the bone remains of a goat are found, which sought this corner to bid farewell to this world.

To access the most hidden areas of the cavity, one must climb a stalagmitic mass which poses no great risk if done carefully; the effort will be rewarded by what we will find in this place, from eccentric stalactites to a great concentration of curtains, in addition to allowing us to observe how the natural light entering from the outside illuminates the highest areas of the cavity.

In 1978, the remains of a corpse were found who apparently died of starvation, still preserving remains of their clothing (BARCELÓ, 1998). Said remains were surely removed from the place since I only found animal remains that over time have fallen into the interior.

In summary, a good option for spending a few hours inside, which can be complemented for the day with a visit to the Cova des Ribellet which is very nearby (462655 4379752, DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) or any of the many cavities in the area.

name: Avenc des Pares
starting_point: Sa Coveta Negre recreational area (Caimari)
clothing: Overalls, gloves, and knee pads essential
equipment: Shaft 1: 1 rope of 15 m, 1 large sling, 1 carabiner. Shaft 2: 1 rope of 45 m, 1 sling, 1 carabiner. Shaft 3: 1 rope of 70 m, 2 bolts, 6 carabiners, slings and some rope protectors. Shaft 4: 1 rope of 40 m, 7-8 slings and 7-8 carabiners.
description: A double pothole discovered by Pedro Martín and Antonio Tarazaga of the Grup Espeleològic de Llubí near Avenc des Cans. The cave consists of four interconnected shafts reaching a total depth of 90 m, with the deepest single pitch being 45 m. The second shaft features beautiful formations and a hidden chamber considered the most attractive part of the cave. Excellent conditions for sporting caving, combinable with nearby Avenc des Cans just 60 m away.
full_description:
INTRODUCTION

Pedro Martín, accompanied by Antonio Tarazaga, both speleologists belonging to the Grup Espeleològic de Llubí (GELL), after finishing a visit to the Avenc des Cans, found this uncatalogued cavity in the vicinity, which they immediately accessed to carry out an initial exploration. Subsequently, once it was confirmed that it was not catalogued, they drew up a first sketch, which was sent to the Federació Balear d'Espeleo (FBE), so that the pothole would be incorporated into the FBE's cavity list. Some time later, the discoverers, in the company of other speleologists from the island, updated the sketch with a more detailed topographic survey.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

As we have already mentioned, there are two entrances to this double pothole, with a separation between them of slightly more than 15 m. The one further to the north is the one that allows a practically complete view of the cavity (number 1 on the topography).

SHAFT 1

The visit to the pothole via Shaft 1 (recommended) is done by installing the rope on the holm oak that is right next to the mouth; it is also recommended to install a deviation approximately one meter below, which will allow us to descend the 31 m of depth, thus avoiding any type of abrasion until reaching a small ledge that is located just before reaching the floor of the shaft. During the entire descent we will be able to contemplate the flowstone that covers the entire wall on that side of the shaft. The floor of the bottom, with little slope, is covered with numerous rock fragments, while two sinks located in the NE and SE areas drain the rainwater that this shaft collects, with the one to the NE being the one that allows us to penetrate several meters until it becomes impassable.

SHAFT 2

This is the shallowest of the four shafts that make up this pothole, at only 11 m; it was the first one found, and from it an attempt was made to access Shaft 3 before the entrance to Shaft 1 was found (it is possible to access Shaft 3 from this location, although it is not recommended). Once Shaft 1 was located, the attempt was abandoned due to the difficulties presented by its widening. However, Shaft 2 is beautifully decorated with formations, as well as a semi-hidden chamber to the SE of the gallery right at the beginning of the crawl that connects to Shaft 3. It is a small jewel, possibly the most beautiful corner of the cavity, and should not be missed.

SHAFT 3

Access is recommended from Shaft 1; to do this it is necessary to climb a few meters if we are at the bottom of the chamber and head east. A window of approximately 1.5 x 2 m places us above the head of an impressive shaft 45 m deep from this point, which we will access by first installing a traverse line and then installing the anchor at the two spits placed for this purpose. 15 m below, a rebelay can be made on a natural anchor (dispensable). 29 m further down, the floor of this beautiful shaft is reached. The deepest area of the shaft is found 19 m below.

SHAFT 4

At 29 m deep, this is the shaft that presents the greatest difficulties when moving through it due to its complicated morphology, in which significant constrictions are combined, whose walls are covered with coraloid formations that continuously threaten to tear the clothing of the long-suffering speleologist. This part of the cavity is accessed from the landing of Shaft 1, from where one must approach the east wall and, following a small ledge that ends at a point where one must climb a couple of meters until reaching a new ledge, somewhat exposed, until reaching the point where the rope is installed. Before reaching the bottom of this shaft, several tight spots must be overcome, as well as two small chambers, until reaching another one that contains some flowstone and a small rimstone pool. At the eastern end of the chamber, a small drain absorbs the water while preventing further continuation.

In summary, a beautiful pothole that brings together all the conditions for the sporting practice of speleology, whose visit can be complemented, if one still has the energy, by visiting its neighbor Avenc des Cans, which is only 60 meters away.

name: Avenc Miner de s'Aliga
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint (Génova). Walk to Coll des Pastors crossroads, take the left path then right, descending to a small stream. Follow the ascending trail to the Marble Caves, then descend to Coma de s'Aliga and take the right path toward the old mines. The chasm is about 20 m above the second mine entrance
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 461570 E, 4380210 N. DATUM WGS84: 461468 E, 4380010 N
equipment: Vertical progression equipment, one 40 m rope, plus 4 protectors
description: A natural cave combined with a historic mining complex, with total dimensions of 30 x 40 x 20 m. Unlike the neighboring Cova Minera de sa Punta which was stripped of its ornamentation, this cave retains some original formations including a beautiful column and stalagmites. The mine features two artificial galleries for mineral extraction: an upper gallery at -26 m and a lower gallery extending about 120 m. Access involves a steep ramp with a 10 m drop and significant rockfall danger during rope operations.
full_description:
Located near the Cova Minera de sa Punta and the Cova Minera devora sa Punta, its total dimensions are 30 x 40 x 20 m. Unlike its neighbor Cova Minera de sa Punta, which was stripped of all its ornamentation, in this case the emptying of the cavity left zones untouched that can give us an idea of what it once was. This mining complex consists of a natural cavity and two artificial galleries (Upper Gallery and Lower Gallery) for the extraction of materials.

To access the mining work area, one must descend a steeply inclined ramp that ends in an approximately ten-meter drop. During the descent along the ramp, it is easy for rock falls to occur at any moment due to the movement of the rope. The danger is real, so maximum attention must be paid during entry and exit maneuvers to the cavity.

Although initially the mineral extraction was possibly done through the natural mouth of the chasm, later, as they went deeper, it became necessary to build a first extraction gallery at -26 m (Upper Gallery) and another at -46 m (Lower Gallery), reaching the cut or face. The work was not completed in the chimney connection area.

Natural Cavity

The natural cavity begins in the so-called Saleta de s'Entrada (Entrance Little Hall), which is at a depth of -26 m, and through which we will move until surpassing some blocks and reaching a first step in the Main Hall. To our right, it is still possible to see the access to the Upper Gallery. A ramp allows us to ascend to a flowstone where some concretioning is visible, with a beautiful column and a group of stalagmites standing out, some of them decapitated.

Returning to the initial step mentioned above, we can descend to a lower level in search of the end. To our left is a large volume of carefully stacked rocks, and next to them, the beginning of a small room where the work was concluded and where the connection with the Lower Gallery should have been made.

Returning toward the Saleta de s'Entrada, one can climb to our right to a beautifully concretioned corner.

Upper Gallery

With a length of approximately 45 m, the Upper Gallery is blocked a few meters from the masonry portal that gives access to it. The rest of the gallery can be accessed from the Main Hall.

Lower Gallery

The Lower Gallery is 20 m below the Upper Gallery and its length is approximately 120 m.

Neither the ceiling of the gallery nor the side walls have needed to be shored up or lined, except at very specific points where pine wood props and masonry work have been used. On our last visit, the Lower Gallery was in good condition, and gypsum outcrops could be found.

name: Avenc Tapat
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: 2 ropes (40 m and 25 m), several slings and carabiners
description: A distensional fracture in dolomitic breccias (NE-SW orientation) with a well-concealed small entrance (0.4 x 0.8 m). Despite the narrow fracture format, the walls are entirely covered in flowstone with beautiful curtain formations in overhanging areas and delicate speleothems affected by subsidence. The 56 m depth provides several hours of exploration through tight passages.
full_description:
Description

Located on the firebreak of the track that runs between the Coll des Pastors and the Mirador antennas, this distensional fracture develops in dolomitic breccias with a NE-SW orientation. The small entrance of 0.40 x 0.80 m was well covered, which made its discovery difficult.

After rigging on a pine tree found nearby, a descent of approximately 3.5 m takes us into the fracture proper, where the separation between the two sides of the fracture gives us an idea of what the rest of the visit will be like.

During the descent to the first level, we will be accompanied by some beautiful roots. Once we touch the ground, we will begin a descent, having to make a rebelay with a sling on a natural anchor through which the sling can be threaded (this is a good option if we don't want to place a spit, which would be the most appropriate thing to do). The descent to the next level, although very short (less than 3 m), is difficult. At this point, we must overcome a small escarpment in order to advance a few meters along the fracture or alternatively continue the descent by making another rebelay on a natural anchor. At the top of the "shaft," 24 m further down at -48 m, we reach the northern end of the last accessible level. Its length is 12 m, and at the southern end we can see that the fracture continues but is impassable.

Although this type of cavity does not lend itself to being particularly beautiful, it is not devoid of formations. In fact, its walls are entirely covered by flowstone, and in some cases with beautiful curtain formations in overhanging areas, not to mention certain corners where delicate speleothems grow, some of them affected by subsidence (collapse) processes.

Its 56 m of depth will allow us to spend several entertaining hours traversing a pothole where the narrow separation between the walls will at times put us to the test.

name: Clot des Sero
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: 25 m rope, 3 protectors, 2 slings, 2 carabiners
description: A pumpkin-shaped collapse cavity in the Serra de na Burguesa with an 8-meter diameter opening from a ceiling collapse. Famous for its abundant Asplenium scolopendrium (hart's tongue fern), which gives the cave its name. The naturally lit main chamber features columns and stalagmites, while a smaller sub-chamber contains diverse formations including solifluction-affected columns and coralloid types. Considered a site of special biological interest.
full_description:
The "Clot des Sero" is a cavity and, to put it simply, shaped like an empty pumpkin on the inside with an opening at the top of approximately eight meters in diameter, the result of the collapse of part of the ceiling, and composed of a single chamber, although at one end of it there is a sub-chamber filled with formations of all kinds, among which a column affected by solifluction and some of the coralloid type stand out. It is actually a hollow resulting from the collapse of part of the wall.

One of the characteristics that has made it well-known among the cavities of the "Serra de na Burguesa" is the existence of a plant that grows abundantly in one area of the cavity. It is the fern Asplenium scolopendrium, commonly known on the island as "llengo de Sero" — hence the name of the cave. In Spanish, one of its many names is "Lengua cervina" (Hart's Tongue), and it can be used to stop diarrhea and reduce intestinal inflammation.

The cavity is illuminated by natural light except for the previously mentioned sub-chamber and a few other small corners. In it grow some formations of the column and stalagmite types. In roughly half of the cavity, various collapses of large blocks prevent access; some of those blocks are becoming covered with calcium carbonate. The central part of the chamber is covered with abundant vegetation, with fig trees, brambles, and others growing in addition to the previously mentioned fern, obstructing movement through it. At one end of the chamber, a small balcony or ledge houses formations of reasonable dimensions, especially thick columns.

Installation

There is not a single way to descend into the bowels of the "Clot des Sero"; I will simply describe the way we used. Next to the path, at the lowest part of the opening, there is a juniper near the mouth of the cavity on which we can place a sling around its base, but due to the few guarantees it offers, it is advisable to run the rope across the path to a nearby pine tree. Right at the edge of the pit's mouth, it is necessary to place a protector to avoid very obvious abrasion. A section of wall followed by a short free-hang places us on a small balcony next to the base of a wild olive tree ("ullastre") that grows there. Before setting foot on the aforementioned balcony, we will need to pendulum a little to attach ourselves to the mentioned wild olive tree or to a column next to it. Once secured, all that remains is to place a new sling around the column followed by two new protectors that we will place on two edges that will allow us to descend again, first a section against the wall followed by a short four-meter free-hang. We "land" right at the cleanest spot in the pit, since the ground is covered with ferns, which allows us to move with some comfort in this area, though not so much in the rest.

The humidity we may encounter in the cavity, at least in winter, is great. I would not be surprised if it can reach 100% at some point — that was the sensation of cold we had during our visit despite the fact that at no time were we inactive.

The "Clot del Sero" is considered a site of special biological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

Unless we intend to scrutinize, survey, and photograph the cavity — and even then it will not take more than half a day — we can complete the outing by visiting its neighbor "Avenc d'en Miquel" (461555, 4378705), or why not the "Cova Fosca" (461328, 4378579), which is also not too far away. The coordinates are taken with the DATUM ED50.

name: Cova Bandolera
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint. Follow the path past the antennas toward Coll des Pastors. Turn off at the junction just before the high-voltage power line tower that crosses the path.
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: Discovered in 2008 in the Serra de Na Burguesa near the Avenc de s'Embut. The single chamber measures approximately 25x10 m with a 10 m ceiling, featuring a notable column with active stalagmite growth and attractive flowstone deposits. Care is needed descending as a thin fungal layer makes the lower section slippery. Numerous goat bones inside suggest animals fell through the entrance and could not escape.
full_description:
On June 8, 2008, my wife and I went out once again to continue "combing" the area of the Serra de Na Burguesa near the viewpoint of the same name, and in the vicinity of the Avenc de s'Embut — a place fertile in cavities of all types, especially pits. We entered the path before the Avenc de sa Pols, which descends gently toward the area of the town of Génova. We had passed by this path many times without ever having paid it much attention until this moment, so we entered it while our eyes scanned both sides, knowing that it would not be at all unusual to find some cavity — something quite common in this area. It did not take us long to find the Cova Barbellera, a small cave with a ceiling that does not much exceed one meter in height and uncatalogued until that moment. The entrance of the cave is surrounded by a dry stone wall, which leads us to think that in times past it was possibly used to shelter animals such as sheep or goats. The coordinates of this small cave are: 464318 E, 4379493 N. DATUM EUROPEAN 1950.

Just a little further down, the path suddenly ends, to my surprise — I thought it would emerge near Génova — so we decided to leave the path and head westward toward a depression in order to inspect the edge of it, hoping to find something, which fortunately happened shortly after at coordinates 464196 E, 4379554 N. Luckily for us, the cave was uncatalogued.

The cave is oriented 42° N with an entrance 2 meters wide by slightly more than one meter in height, situated on a small rock slope, giving from the outset the sensation that something interesting might be contained within. Downclimbing without having to use a rope or any other equipment, once we have descended about three meters we will reach a sort of ledge formed next to a beautiful column in which the formation process has stopped except at the right end, where a stalagmite attached to the column is active, thickening the column.

We will continue descending along the right side of the cave next to the aforementioned stalagmite, taking care not to slip, as a thin layer of fungi makes the final section of the cave a dangerous place — though it never gives the impression of being so.

Once positioned at the bottom of the cave, the natural light entering it allows us to visualize the surrounding environment, which is none other than a chamber of about 25 meters in length by 10 in width and a height of 10 meters, with the ceiling at the same level as the cave entrance.

If we decide to begin the visit heading NNW, we will first observe a beautiful flowstone almost directly below the entrance that extends along the entire side, though the most interesting part is to the right of the column and almost beneath the mouth of the cave. To finish visiting this side of the chamber, one must climb a couple of meters to access its last corner, passing over a small well, and in this location some small columns as well as extremely fine soda straws and an occasional small curtain can be appreciated, in addition to a tiny room that is accessed not without some difficulty due to the narrowness.

On the way back, all that remains is to visit the other end of the chamber, which lies in the SSE direction, for which we must navigate around large blocks of stone on the right side belonging to a collapse in the cave, and after advancing a few meters it immediately forces us to stop as the cave has no possible continuation. The ceiling of the chamber begins right above the entrance of the cave and descends vertiginously until reaching the other side of the chamber where it does not exceed one meter in height.

Of note is the great quantity of goat bones found inside the cave right below its entrance, surely due to accidental falls from the exterior. The animals must have endured a long agony before dying, given the impossibility of getting back outside.

Near the Cova Bandolera is the Cova de s'Embut (464241 E, 4379764 N. DATUM EUROPEAN 1950), whose visit will allow us to complete the day's outing.

name: Cova des Bufador de Son Berenguer
starting_point: From Santa Maria del Camí, take the Coanegra path to the Alaró path junction, take the first left to an electric transformer. Park near a beige building about 60m away.
equipment: Only a small rope may be needed at one visible point; may be necessary in winter.
description: A structural cave formed over Jurassic breccias (Lias) featuring large-scale collapses, a magnificent fault mirror, and over 1,000m of passages across twelve large spaces. Discovered in 1950, the cave spans 70m of depth with abundant crystalline formations and varied speleothems in its deepest chambers. Access requires permission from the landowner.
full_description:
Geologically speaking, the Bufador de Son Berenguer is situated over Jurassic breccias (Lias), being a structural cavity in which large-scale collapses have occurred. It is easy to observe at different points in the cavity large, threatening blocks ready to fall at any moment as a result of the aforementioned collapses. Some of them occurred so long ago that the fallen blocks are now completely calcified. It is also worth highlighting a magnificent fault mirror related to the mentioned collapses.

Discovered and explored on April 6, 1950, according to J. Mascaró (CTM., 1968), a team of speleologists from the GES studied it in the summer of 1960. The results were published in Speleon, 13 (Oviedo), in 1962, by J. Montoriol, who describes the clastic processes, the lithochemical intensity, the sedimentological aspects, and the solifluction derived from the floor settlements. He proposes the speleogenesis as an interstratigraphic development induced by waters absorbed from the exterior and offers several cross-sectional profiles of the cavity.

In 2002, J. A. Encinas, M. L. Redondo, and M. Luque surveyed the only topography currently existing of the cavity. In it, a notable ramp covered with endogenous clastic detritus can be seen, precipitated along the seventy meters of elevation from the entrance to the bottom, with large boulders obstructing the flanks and the bottom. They are Triassic limestone rocks, intensely leptoclased in the upper part, alternating with breccia strata and compact marls, as far as is perceptible inside. A cupular phenomenon of subsidence origin, of which we only see barely half of its more than 120 meters of virtual diameter, is arrested by a rectilinear break and by the travertine massif that runs from NW to NE. So that the vault remains largely stable thanks to the proliferation of columns derived from the intense lithochemical apparatus.

The interior route amply exceeds one thousand meters. It forms twelve large spaces between the two detached interstratigraphic plates that overlap in the first upper third. Part of these recesses are formed by the wedging of boulders retained between ceiling and floor, upon detaching voluminous calcareous sections. So that a good part of the journey is rough, though entertaining.

The most remote areas of the cavern possess abundant crystalline filigrees and notable speleothematic variety; as they require being traversed largely by crawling, the risk of breaking the formations became overwhelming during the obligatory planimetric work. More comfortable are the two upper chambers, also with a relatively low ceiling, with a fairly flat floor in the first and the other narrowing toward the east; while the open spaces at the bottom are the largest.

The vestibular or entrance zone, together with the southern extremity, serves as a preamble to the center of the complex. From there, four more or less passable paths depart, practicable without the need for ropes. The first toward the -61 m level, with an alternative path running through the columns that lead directly to the chaotic core of blocks, passing through a narrow crack. A third progression route toward the bottom heads NE above the flowstones, and the fourth requires climbing to the upper part of the right flank, as one enters the underground.

Regarding the formation of the endokarstic system, the determining argument was already documented in the proceedings of the "II National Congress of Speleology" held in 1972: J. A. Encinas, "A speleogenetic constant in the Pollensa area (Mallorca)." The note warned about the presence of plastic marl strata relatable to incipient cavernments, in discrete but illustrative and easily graspable calcareous cavities.

Unlike the general breccia behavior of the Triassic calcareous and dolomitic strata, and that of diaclastic discontinuities in the Burdigalian detrital materials, the vacuities generated at the contact between marly clays and compact rocks follow singular pneumatological patterns. Active in the initial chamber, when subjected to an atmosphere with high CO2 content. But, for this primordial space to be an effective excavatory instrument, the essential requirement is that it has sufficient volumetric entity to harbor dissolving capacity of the upper stratum.

Among the multiple possibilities for creating underground discontinuities, the one generated as a consequence of water mass loss in the marls — by basin adjustment, by water table migration, by vadose supply desiccation, or by sea level drop — entails notable reduction of clay volume. These shrink toward the bottom, causing the detachment of the drier upper parts and progressive subsidence of the plastic material, until reaching the hard rock plane: leaving empty the space corresponding to the volume of water expelled from the clays, or causing detachment of the rocks above, or both phenomena simultaneously. Dissolution thus begins the digestive process of the interior, and as it advances, it captures rainwater runoff that, in turn, moistens part of the already dried clays, drags them toward the drainage outlet, or sediments them on the bed, around the incipient detrital cone. Except when the subsidence undercutting causes the total detachment of the affected area, crushing the marl substrate and leaving clear a subcircular hemispherical contour; of which the speleologist usually only accesses the part above the detrital cone.

And the core of the emptying — whether clastic, massive, or ultimately digested by endokarstic dissolution — does not always manifest in its characteristic spheroid appearance, because frequently it is found in fan shape, or adopting a flattened labyrinthine arrangement, or, as in the case that specifically concerns us, constituting a steep ramp.

It is true that not all these ramps are due to focal subsidence processes, nor are all those effectively related to marls foreign to pre-Tortonian orogenic tensions; because the cave of Es Bufador, like the Cova Nova de Son Lluís (M.:Por./N.01), like the Coves de Gènova (M.:Pal./G.02) or the caves of Campanet (M.:Cpe./C.01), to cite only some highly significant phenomena, shows — as J. Montoriol already pointed out — an undeniable structural component. The same one in which a calcareous stratigraphic series slides over the more modern plastic marl deposits, causing an imbricate drag in which the percolating water action eventually takes its toll, along the axial plane of the contact; until draining beneath the ravine bed.

Note: Currently, the cave can only be accessed by requesting permission from the owner.

name: Cova Calenta
starting_point: From the parking area of Santueri Castle
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Helmet and lighting only.
description: Located beneath the Santueri Castle parking area, this is the largest and most interesting cave in the castle area, with prehistoric human settlements dating from 2500-1700 BC. It has two entrances, extensive formations, and a complex interior with multiple chambers and passages at different levels, though most formation activity has ceased.
full_description:
The Cova Calenta, like the Cova des Bous nearby, was the site of the first human settlements in Felanitx, along with 104 other archaeological sites. Both caves are located on the eastern slope of Santueri Castle. These settlements were established between 2500 and 1700 BC. Another human settlement on the slope was the Cova des Confessionari des Moros, which is located one minute from the Cova des Drac.

In the chamber to the right of the entrance, among some pools with water, prehistoric ceramic remains can still be found, and outside, near the entrance, pieces of Roman tegulae (tiles). The anthropic impact on Balearic caverns. J. A. Encinas S.

This cave is very well hidden beneath the castle parking area. It can be accessed by entering the forest right where the parking retaining wall begins, next to a pine tree, about fifteen meters away, or alternatively by climbing the first group of steps of the castle access stairway and descending into the forest through an opening alongside the slope, then searching for it by walking parallel to the parking area. The cave entrance is very overgrown with vegetation, making it not at all easy to find if we choose the second access option.

Of the four caves in the Santueri Castle area, the Cova Calenta is the largest of all and also, why not say it, the most interesting from my point of view, since although the formation process has stopped in its interior almost entirely, it has far more formations than its neighbors, also gathering greater characteristics for practicing speleology.

This cave has two access mouths, so if we wish, we can organize the visit by entering through one and exiting through the other. If we choose to enter through the left entrance (the larger one), we will immediately find ourselves inside a small chamber about seven meters wide by nine meters long, and right at the back of the chamber there is a small tunnel that gives access to a large chamber where the enormous chaos can be appreciated due to rock falls as well as the irregular floor, having to be careful not to fall into its many holes, which in turn connect with others, sometimes giving the feeling that it is a two-story chamber since the floor is hollow in many sections, mainly due to the large number of blocks piled on top of each other in the area.

Curiously, in this cave the possible routes are marked with fishing lines stretched along the floor and in some cases thicker cords, which we must be careful not to trip over and suffer a fall.

We can make the visit by first exploring the left side, followed by the central area, and finally finishing on the right side, exiting through the small entrance. But before doing so, we will have enjoyed climbing, descending, and entering an endless number of nooks and crannies that will make the visit very enjoyable, always being very careful to watch where we place our feet on the ground.

The cave visit can be complemented by visiting both the Cova des Bous and the Cova des Drac, and even, if possible, by entering the interior of the castle, a place with incredible views of both the coast and the interior.

name: Cova de ca na Picarola
starting_point: Km 3 of the MA-2020, at the municipal boundary of Bunyola and Santa Maria del Camí.
distance: Approximately 500 m from the parking area
equipment: 25 m rope if descending the pit in the Entrance Chamber.
description: A predominantly horizontal cave in Bunyola with multiple interconnected chambers and galleries, explorable with or without vertical equipment depending on the route. Highlights include the Speleothem Chamber with the richest formations, steep ramps including the dangerous Inferno Ramp with rockfall risk, and the Gours Chamber with delicate formations. Remains of at least one Myotragus (extinct Balearic goat-antelope) were found in the Lower Gallery.
full_description:
Description

Sala de s'Entrada (Entrance Chamber)

With dimensions of 14 x 25 x 3.5 m, with a NW-SE orientation, this is a collapse chamber where reconstructive processes are not abundant. There are two points in the chamber that allow communication with the rest of the cave. The first of them (point 2 on the survey) allows us to continue the visit using progression equipment, while point 3 on the survey avoids having to use any type of equipment to explore the cave.

Es Pou (The Well)

From point 2 on the survey, the rope head is installed on a natural anchor to descend a first drop of 10 m and a subsequent one of 4 m, which places us in the southern zone of the Galeria Llarga (Long Gallery).

Sa Saleta (The Small Chamber)

Of reduced dimensions, 6 x 8 x 2 m, it is situated partly beneath the Entrance Chamber. It has an entrance from the exterior, after overcoming two levels formed by dry stone walls. In the part farthest from the entrance, a small crawlway allows access to the Entrance Chamber.

Els Escarpes (The Escarpments)

This is a transit zone between the Pas de Pedro (Pedro's Pass), point 3 on the survey, and the Sala dets Espeleotemes (Speleothem Chamber), point 8 on the survey. An escarpment connecting with the Long Gallery runs along the east side.

Galeria Llarga (Long Gallery)

It occupies the entire east zone of the cavity and can be accessed either via the Well with progression equipment or from the Speleothem Chamber without it. The accumulation of detritus currently separates it from the Lower Gallery.

Galeria Inferior (Lower Gallery)

Measuring 2.5 x 22 x 2 m, it accumulates large rock blocks, among which remains of at least one Myotragus were found. At its center it contains an inaccessible sink pit of 3.5 m. Its ceiling is formed by rocks that, due to their size, became wedged together, forming the floor of the Long Gallery.

Sala dets Espeleotemes (Speleothem Chamber)

This zone of the cavity, measuring 9 x 20 x 2 m, is the one that harbors the most formations as well as extensional fractures with horizontal displacement. It is accessed from the Entrance Chamber from points 2 and 3 on the survey. If we want to avoid using progression equipment, we must access from point 3 on the survey. The visit to the cave can be continued either to the NW, accessing Sa Rampa (The Ramp), or to the SE, via a ramp that will bring us closer to point 6 on the survey.

Rampa de l'Ensurt (Fright Ramp)

After overcoming some tight spots, one reaches the lower zone of Sa Rampa, 16 x 28 x 2 m at the highest zone. The slope exceeds a 40% incline; despite this, we can move without having to use progression equipment. The left zone in the order of progression is the most decorated with concretions.

Rampa de l'Infern (Hell Ramp)

A dangerous place, this ramp has a considerable drop. Once the deepest zone of the ramp is reached, a fracture prevents further progress without using progression equipment. It is better to retrace our steps, as stones fall from the upper zone at this location, threatening imminent collapse. These are the dimensions of the ramp: 3.5 x 6 m.

Saleta des Gours (Gours Chamber)

With its 12 x 7.5 x 1 m, this is one of the places in the cavity where formations are most abundant, along with some gours that give it its name. Movement through this spot is best avoided, as irreparable damage can be caused to the formations.

Es Pedregal (The Rocky Ground)

This small corner of 4 x 7 m is formed by a ramp covered with stones from an upper level.

name: Cova de Cala Falcó
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma-4014 road from Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, a path leads approximately 1.5 km to a half-open metal gate, then follow the trail to Cala Varques and coast along to Cala Falcó
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Helmet and lighting only. Neoprene to cross the lake (not essential).
description: Located on the beach of Cala Falcó, this karstic cave has easy access and minimal technical difficulty. It features a large entrance arch, a beautiful crystal-clear lake with reflected stalactites, and a well-decorated rear chamber with active formations. The cave also contains calcium carbonate films floating on a small lake, similar to those found in Cova des Pont.
full_description:
Located in an area surrounded by other caves of greater importance, the Cova de Cala Falcó finds itself somewhat forgotten, to such an extent that it is difficult to find information about it despite having easy access as it is located on the beach itself and having practically no technical difficulties.

The entrance to the cavity is situated on the northeast side of the cove and is formed first by an arch 10 meters wide by 2.80 meters high and 7 meters deep, which narrows until reaching 2.8 by 1.30 meters at the interior part of the entrance.

Once inside, we find a large chamber that immediately begins to gain height while the ceiling seems to reach the exterior surface. If we advance along the right side of the cavity, we will soon realize that it has no continuity, but if we head to the opposite side, once past a small ridge, a descent begins that soon brings us to the shores of the larger lake, where a beautiful column on the shore stands out, preventing us from continuing the visit. Despite not being very large, the lake is nevertheless of great beauty with its clean and crystal-clear waters, even though some pieces of floating wood are found inside while stalactites are reflected in it. It is also possible to appreciate some stalagmites inside, which demonstrates that this part of the cavity was not always flooded. A paper by Dr. Joan J. Fornós explains the oscillation curve of sea level during the last 300,000 years, Web http://www.universia.es/contniveluno/noticias/uib.jsp

Crossing the lake presents no problem since only at the beginning and at the end does it have any depth; the rest we can do on foot (we must cross the lake along its left side).

Once on the other side of the lake, we find a chamber of considerable dimensions, devoid of formations in almost all of it, with the floor covered in large blocks fallen from the ceiling. The chamber connects to another of more or less the same dimensions, and this one is quite well decorated with concretions, being active. In a small lake on the most eastern side of the cave, small sheets of calcium carbonate can be observed floating on the surface, similar to the Carbonate Lake of the Cova des Pont (Manacor).

In the deepest part of the cave, I found some efflorescences when I was about to visit another small lake, possibly produced by the droppings of some bat specimen. The appearance was that of a tuft of fine silvery hairs, or why not, fine fiber optic threads growing vertically with a size of approximately 10 cm. It is the second time I have found this type of efflorescence inside a cave; the first was in the Cova del Pirata (Manacor).

Noteworthy is the maidenhair fern plant growing at the entrance to the cavern, something not very common on the island but which I have been able to observe some specimens of in caves on the island.

The karstic Cova de Cala Falcó is a cavity created by marine erosion and is classified as a natural site of special speleological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventorial List of Natural Subterranean Sites of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

If the visit to this cavity does not fill the day, one can make a visit to the Cova des Coloms I at 525963 E, 4372750 N (accessed by water from the cove).

name: Cova de cala Varques A, C
name_english: Cave of Cala Varques A, C
starting_point: At km 9 of the Ma-4014 road connecting Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, there is currently a parking area.
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting if not accessing the flooded areas.
description: Part of the Cala Varques cave system, consisting of zones A and C. Zone A was formerly occupied by hippies and features a long low-ceilinged gallery connecting to the flooded area. Zone C is the smallest terrestrial section of the system, with a single 16x18 m chamber containing large rock blocks. Both zones connect to the rest of the cave system through underwater passages.
full_description:
Zone A

Coordinates: 525388 4372304 DATUM WGS84

For a long time the entrance area was occupied by hippies, but this no longer occurs at present. A long gallery with a low ceiling connects to the flooded zone.

Zone C

Coordinates: 525458 4372190 DATUM WGS84

The terrestrial part of the cave is the smallest of the three that make up the system, with a single chamber measuring 16 x 18 m in which some large stone blocks accumulate. It connects with the rest of the caves in the system through the flooded zone.

name: Cova de Cala Varques B
starting_point: At km 9 of the Ma-4014 road connecting Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, there is currently a parking area.
equipment: If not practicing speleo-diving, a neoprene wetsuit for the aquatic area.
description: The largest of the four caves along the beach line at Cala Varques, while the other three (A, C, D) form a connected system. The entrance is semi-hidden among lentisk bushes. Inside, a gentle descent leads to a beautiful lake decorated with ceiling and phreatic speleothems.
full_description:
It is the largest cave of the four cavities found along the beach line, although the other three form a cave system designated ACD.

The access entrance to the cave is semi-hidden among lentisk bushes. Once inside, a gentle descent leads to a beautiful lake beautifully decorated with ceiling and phreatic speleothems.

name: Cova de Cala Varques D
starting_point: Ma-414 km 9.200 approximately. There is a parking area at that point to leave the vehicle.
clothing: Wetsuit if you intend to enter the lakes.
equipment: For land exploration, only a preferably tear-resistant coverall is needed.
description: Cova de Cala Varques D is part of the interconnected Cala Varques cave system (zones A, C, D), linked via underwater passages. Access is from the small beach at Cala Varques, though winter storms may block the entrance with sand. The cave features a vestibule opening to the exterior, an initial water contact area, and a lake in the southern section.
full_description:
Cova de Cala Varques "D" is part of the Cala Varques cave system comprising zones A, C, and D. These cavities are connected to each other subaquatically. It is accessed right from the small beach at Cala Varques, when winter has not filled the entrance with sand due to storms.

Vestibule accessed from the exterior.

First contact with the water.

Access to the lake located in the south of the cavity.

name: Cova de Camp Duai
coordinates_utm: Contact the Balearic Speleology Federation (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: 1 rope of 25 m for the entrance, 1 rope of 20 m for the shaft between upper and intermediate levels, 1 sling of at least 2 m for a small jump past Mud Alley, 1 protector, 2 bolts, carabiners
description: A multi-level cave in Sant Llorenç reaching 78 m deep, historically used for tobacco smuggling. The upper level features alveolar chambers with formations and roots, while the CO2 Chamber contains an impressive corrosion pit. The intermediate level is the most spectacular, comparable only to Cova des Pas de Vallgornera, with pristine white speleothems including thousands of helictites, tree-like stalagmites covered in botryoidal formations, and translucent efflorescent crystals.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

After overcoming a 22 m descent, the last 15 m in free air, we 'land' in this alveolar-type hall, not lacking in formations nor in the multitude of roots that cover part of its walls and ceiling. In appearance, a classic hall like so many others on the island, which however shows no signs of what this cave conceals. The surface is covered with clasts fallen from the exterior, as well as bones from different mammal species. Near the rope there is a dry-stone construction (a type of bench), which was part of a pulley system used to lower contraband tobacco that was stored in the cave.

CO2 HALL

This hall, in addition to beautiful formations, houses the Carbide Well (Pou des Carburo), with dimensions of approximately one meter in diameter and a depth of ten, presenting significant levels of corrosion clearly visible on its walls, as well as on the calcium carbonate flowstone above the mouth, reaching a height exceeding two and a half meters. Not far away, just a few meters, there is another interesting vent, although due to its inaccessibility its dimensions are unknown. Outside the cave, just 25 m from the entrance at a bearing of 25° N, there is a small well currently filled with stones inside, and it is unknown whether it was ever directly connected to the cave.

THE NEW WELLS (ES NOUS POUS)

This is a structural hall of considerable dimensions, in which a gigantic subsidence process collapsed it, revealing a beautiful tepee that runs longitudinally from one end to the other. This event also created a beautiful fault mirror in the NE zone. A crack of significant size, as well as some chimneys in the central part of the hall, absorb the rainwater from the exterior, charged with a significant concentration of CO₂ capable of corroding the calcite flowstone over which the water flows. Formations are not lacking in this hall; in fact, they abound copiously both in quantity and variety everywhere, with great activity appreciable in the hall, especially in the central zone.

THE SUFFERING (ES PATIMENT)

After crossing the Mud Alley (Carreró des Fang) and descending a small jump, one reaches a beautiful ceiling corrosion cupola, which precedes a kind of window that opens onto one of the most beautiful areas of the cave. The abundance and variety of speleothems in this place is immense; the helictites can be counted in the thousands, of all sizes, thicknesses, and shapes, pointing in all directions, even developing vertically from the floor. Notable in this hall is a most beautiful column largely covered in eccentric formations and acicular crystals, some of which have dagger-like morphology.

INTERMEDIATE LEVEL

Once the level has been changed (letter 'F' on the survey), in progressive descent, we approach the part that makes this cavity unique. This is the level with the greatest extent, measuring 36 x 75 m. Only the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera can compete with everything that has developed here over time. Pure extreme whiteness in the forms generated by the electromagnetic energy that orders the axial sliding of the crystals, translucent efflorescences over the lumpy carbonate coating. In the depths and various recesses of the cavern — a sort of enormous geode — located between the -35 meter mark and the seventy-something meters, as far as the speleologist can transit in depth, across the length, width, and height of the steep, narrow or scarped slope. The speleothems in this place are impressive, not only for their immaculate whiteness but also, on occasion, for their originality — such is the case of the singular stalagmite of arboreal structure, large in size and completely covered in botryoidal formations.

name: Cova de can Bordils
name_english: Cave of Can Bordils (also Cave of the Hiding Places)
starting_point: Ma-4023 road km 2.400 between Porto Cristo and Cala Millor. After passing the second right turn toward Cala Millor, at 177 m you'll find the gate to the property. Ring the bell for the owner to let you in.
best_season: Any, although swimming season is recommended
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting.
description: Located on private farmland just 70 m from the house, this cave is accessed via steps carved into the ground beneath a fig tree. The cave descends to approximately sea level and contains a gallery with a small lake near the entrance, a main chamber with ceiling formations, and a crystal-clear saltwater lake. The cave has three lakes of varying sizes. It is one of 30 ZEC-designated caves on Mallorca, requiring official permission to visit.
full_description:
It is surprising the relationship that fig trees have with the entrances of cavities on the coast of Manacor. Once again, upon making the first contact with the Cova de can Bordils or dets Amagatalls, the first thing I could see as soon as I approached is that a beautiful fig tree protrudes from the interior of the cave, just like some others in the area, such as its neighbors the Cova de sa Figuera and the Gruta de l'Amo den Martí, which are very close by. Perhaps one day this matter will need to be analyzed, because although one always tends to think — and it may indeed be true — that bird droppings can contain seeds that subsequently germinate, why is it always fig tree seeds that end up germinating and not others?

The Cova de can Bordils or dets Amagatalls is located right on the Can Bordils estate, about seventy meters from the house, so it is necessary to ask permission to visit from Sr. Pedro, who will kindly allow it (at least during our visit he did not put up the slightest objection and everything was made easy for us).

To access the cave, we descend via stairs carved into the ground that are usually quite dirty, both from branches pruned from the fig tree itself growing next to the stairs, and from the garbage that the property owners throw into the interior of the cave, not to mention dead animals — sheep, goats, chickens, etc. In fact, in this first part of the cave there is a large quantity of bones that, depending on the person, can make quite an impression.

Navigating around the garbage, we continue descending progressively until we reach the floor of the cave, which must be at approximately zero meters altitude, where on our left we can observe a gallery and at its entrance a small lake that seems to block the way but can be circumvented. On the first visit that Mallorca Verde made to the cavity, we could not visit this entire gallery due to lack of time, as the owners had to leave, and we left it pending for another day. So we continued straight ahead through a wide corridor that places us in a sort of esplanade in a chamber with some formations, mainly on certain areas of the ceiling of the cavity as well as on the left side. On our right and several meters below us lies a lake of crystal-clear saltwater.

Once outside the cave and talking with the property owner, he told us that once you cross to the other side of the first lake we found going down on our left, the gallery continues until reaching another one where it ends. So the cavity has three lakes of different dimensions, with the last one remaining for us to explore on the next visit. Once the lake that had been pending from the previous visit has been visited, this Web page remains pending an update of the cavity.

NOTE: The Cova de Can Bordils is one of the 30 ZEC cavities that currently exist on the island of Mallorca. For this reason, access is prohibited without the corresponding permit from the Ministry of the Environment.

name: Cova de Can Milló
starting_point: From Santa María del Camí, take Camí/Carrer de Coanegra to Carrer Ritch Miller and park there. Vehicles cannot go further, but equipment can be dropped off closer to the cave.
equipment: Ropes: 1x50m, 1x15m, 1x25m. Slings and carabiners. CO2 detector recommended.
description: A predominantly vertical cave reaching -74m depth with two descent options from the entrance chamber via vertical shafts up to 30m deep. Features beautifully decorated chambers with delicate speleothems and gours, culminating in the Mud Chamber (Sala del Barro) with variable CO2 levels. Classified as a Special Conservation Zone (ZEC) under Red Natura 2000, requiring official permission to visit.
full_description:
Description

Once in the entrance chamber, to continue deeper into the cavity we have two options, both through vertical shafts. If we choose the shaft situated to the east, we access it through a low-ceiling gallery that places us at the top of it. First, we descend a small ramp of approximately half a dozen meters, then another slope of a dozen meters, and from there descend a thirty-meter vertical drop.

If we choose the other option, after a three-meter vertical drop we reach a beautifully decorated chamber, then reach the top of a fifteen-meter vertical shaft from where we advance until reaching the same level as the first option. We find ourselves in a horizontal zone. It is recommended that if the group is large, they reach this point using both options and exit using the opposite one from the descent when visiting the rest of the cavity.

We continue visiting the cavity along the only possible route, first descending a ramp where it is also recommended to install a rope (I don't remember the measurements; with a 20 m rope I think there will be plenty), until reaching another chamber where we must climb a couple of meters to continue. Soon we find on our right a beautiful gour beautifully decorated with formations, and a little further ahead a crawlway to the left as an alternative to another slightly longer but less demanding passage. Choose whichever you prefer, or take one on the way in and the other on the way out, thus having used both. Further ahead, after overcoming a small obstacle, we find a beautiful group of delicate speleothems that we should not approach too closely to avoid damaging. A little further on, we reach the top of the last shaft of approximately fifteen meters (I don't remember exactly how many), which deposits us in the Sala del Barro (Mud Chamber), the sinkhole and final level of the cave. In this chamber, in addition to delighting in the speleothems covered with a fine layer of mud that makes it different from the rest of the cavity, we will also notice the presence of CO2 in variable quantity depending mainly on the season when we visit. It is advisable to carry a detector.

The Cova de Can Millo is catalogued as a Special Conservation Zone (ZEC). These types of cavities are areas of great environmental interest for the conservation of diversity, which have been designated by the member states of the European Union to be integrated into the Natura 2000 Network. Officially, to visit this type of cavity, the corresponding permit from the Ministry of the Environment is required.

Be responsible and don't forget to leave the cave as you would like to find it on a future visit.

name: Cova de can Sion
starting_point: Cami Vell de Campanet, 3.4 km from MA-2200 road km 47.230, on the Palma-Alcudia to Pollensa road
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed, but a rope is helpful for the lower part of the cave. Some unexplored areas may require vertical equipment.
description: Behind its small entrance lies a cave of impressive dimensions and beauty comparable to any other on the island, with abundant concretions, numerous large water-filled gours, and densely packed columns. The cave has two difficulty levels ranging from horizontal to vertical zones. It houses a large bat colony and contains ceramic remains indicating historical human use for water collection. Classified as a ZEC site, access requires permission from the Environmental Ministry.
full_description:
The small mouth hides a cave of great dimensions and a beauty comparable to any other on the island. As soon as we find ourselves inside, we can move in any direction since the cave will always surprise us. On my first visit to this cave, accompanied by my wife, I dedicated myself to visiting the zone on the left side, a completely concretioned place where, especially at the beginning, we will have to use our hands to overcome some of its ramps. We will immediately begin to marvel at its innumerable rimstone pools, some of great dimensions and almost all of them full of water — notably a semi-aerial one shaped like a shell. The columns are packed together one next to another, leaving in some places a small space between them that will serve us to continue the visit.

As I said at the beginning, we can visit the cave in multiple ways.
