One that I propose could be to start by descending in a straight line from the entrance and visiting the entire right side, which in principle is not very large, and then move to the left side where there is a great room in which large blocks accumulate on the floor.

In short, each person should do it however they wish, since there is plenty of cave to visit. Next to a large flowstone there is a corner through which one continues through a crawlway, descending to a lower level where it is necessary to use a rope to avoid problems on the return, as the water makes it a rather slippery zone. Also on the other side of the cave there is some tricky spot where, depending on each person's ability, a rope may be necessary.

A large colony of bats resides inside the cave, so we must be careful to treat both them and the cave with the utmost respect, trying to avoid startling them, mainly by not shining light directly at them. To give an idea of the quantity of bats that inhabit this place, I can say that I have been able to verify that some piles of guano exceed one meter in height.

This cave, like others, is classified with two difficulty levels, going from a horizontal zone to a vertical one where one must be careful not to proceed if it is not within our capability (before taking a step, make sure). In any case, the dimensions of the cavity are so great that we can get lost for hours enjoying its formations as well as taking photographs.

In a fairly deep zone of the cave, I found quite a few ceramic remains, apparently not of great antiquity, which indicates that, as in other important cavities on the island, humans ventured into the depths of this cave with the intention of obtaining water, mainly during the summer season.

NOTE: Cova Can Sion is one of the 30 ZEC (Special Conservation Zone) cavities that currently exist on the island of Mallorca. For this reason, access is prohibited without the corresponding permit from the Ministry of the Environment.

name: Cova de Canet
starting_point: Vial de ses Rotges intersection with Vial V, if accessing through the natural entrance
equipment: Rope and some protectors
description: A cave of great speleological, geological, and archaeological interest, entirely formed in conglomerates -- possibly unique on the island. Remains of Myotragus Antiquus were found here, and carbon dating revealed human presence dating to 7,220 BC, extending Mallorca's known human settlement timeline by 2,500 years. Features include corrosion domes, carved steps, and flowstones. Note: classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC) requiring official authorization to visit.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova de Canet is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities catalogued as LIC. At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these places can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Department of Environment and Territory, so before making any visit, the required permit must be obtained to enter.

Description

A cavity of great interest, to which must be added, beyond the strictly speleological, the geological and archaeological importance. It is no coincidence that right below the entrance shaft, in a Holocene deposit, remains of Myotragus Antiquus were found, among others (PONS et al., 1979). An excavation carried out in the cavity revealed a stratigraphy of 6 meters in depth, which yielded two levels with charcoal (Fig. 2). One of them, situated at 3 meters depth, was dated by C14 to 7,220 +570/-500 B.C. (KOPPER, 1984). These results extended the antiquity of the arrival of the first human settlers in Mallorca by approximately 2,500 years (PONS et al., 1979).

One of the characteristics of the cave is that it is formed entirely in conglomerates, which perhaps makes it unique on the island. Personally, I do not know of another. Except for the areas covered in calcium carbonate, ceilings and walls are decorated with this type of material, giving it a colorfulness of great beauty.

In addition to the classic speleothems that can be found in any karstic cavity, in the Cova de Canet we can find interesting corrosion cupolas of all sizes, as well as what appear to be nozzles similar to those found in the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera (Llucmajor) or in the Cova de sa Balma des Quartó, in Cala Romántica.

The morphology of the cave is rectilinear and it is prepared for us to move through it without any problem. Access can be made either through the natural shaft or through the artificial gallery from the Canet estate itself, with the corresponding permission from the owners. Whichever access we use, we will arrive at the same point — a large-volume hall, the biggest in the cave. From this point we will begin the ascent via a carved staircase until reaching a basically horizontal zone, and then continue climbing, always surrounded by beautiful formations with flowstones on both sides. Noteworthy from this point on throughout the entire route is the modeling work done by the water, polishing the conglomerates.

A gentle descent takes us to a completely flat area where a hole can be seen to our left, through which a lower level is accessed — a heavily concretioned gallery in which we will have to abandon the vertical position to move through it.

Returning, we will continue through a practically flat area at first, then progressively ascending gently. Along the way we will begin to encounter the excavation pits made by archaeologists, a large gour currently dry, and if we look up we can marvel at the beautiful cupolas found throughout the ceiling area. The cave begins to narrow until reaching a zone where it closes, not too far from the surface, and where roots make their appearance.

name: Cova de Cornavaques
starting_point: At km 1.1 of the Cala Sant Vicenç road, take the track on the left to the house. Park and walk about 200 m to the torrent, follow it, then leave and walk along the right slope to the cave. Access may be marked with cairns.
equipment: Ropes: 1 x 10 m, 1 x 20 m, 1 x 45 m. Bolts: 3. Slings.
description: This predominantly horizontal cave features a series of impressive chambers including the Entrance Room, the Pool Room with numerous gours, the Giant's Room, and the spectacular Columns Room with 15 m ceilings. The Columns Room contains unique calcite-encrusted tree roots hanging from the ceiling and abundant formations including a beautiful disc behind a group of columns and stalagmites.
full_description:
Description

Once the entrance is located, a small downclimb takes us into the cavity. We will advance through the cave until we reach a sort of balcony from which we will again downclimb until we reach the Entrance Room. Right at the end of said room and on the right side, a cascade conceals the continuation of the cave.

At this point, one of the cavers must climb the aforementioned cascade to install a rope that allows the rest to climb up to the window. The same rope can serve us to descend on the other side of the window to reach the Sala dels Gorgs (Pool Room), which is accessed via a 5 m rappel. We advance through the room, marveling at the quantity of rimstone pools we encounter along the way, until we reach a ramp that brings us closer to the Sala des Gegant (Giant's Room). We advance through it until we reach a sinkhole and enter the Sala de las Columnas (Columns Room).

This is a spectacular room with a height approaching 15 m where all types of formations abound, notably a beautiful disc behind a group of columns and stalagmites. Advancing over rimstone pools and past the columns, we will find roots that from the ceiling plunge down to nearly touch the floor. There is nothing like it in any known cavity on the island; the most curious thing is that these roots are covered in calcite. This is a place where fingers go trigger-happy taking photographs, as it is of great beauty. From this spot, heading to the left, we will head toward a ramp that we must ascend to reach the funnel that will allow us to descend through it to the lower rooms. The approach to the funnel is made via some bolts located in the upper zone of the mentioned ramp next to a bolt with a ring (that is how it was several years ago). We will install on the bolts the rope that will allow us to approach the funnel and install again on another bolt that is located right behind us at the top of the funnel's neck, which will allow us to set up a deviation to prevent rope rub. Once through the neck of the funnel, we will appear on the ceiling of a room about 18 m from the floor. Care must be taken when reaching the bottom, as the rope falls right onto a beautiful rimstone pool that is usually always full of water, which we will avoid by pendulum swinging.

I am not going to describe the rest of the cave since it is straightforward, and I will let you discover it for yourselves.

name: Cova de la Base
starting_point: MA-2210 road
access: From Palma, TIB bus line 340 Palma - Puerto de Pollença. Schedule: from 06:30
equipment: 1 x 35 m rope and a short one for the entrance, 1 sling, 2 rope protectors
description: Developed in Lower Jurassic limestones, this cave has been visited since the Balearic Bronze Age as evidenced by ceramic remains used for water collection. A singularly beautiful cave with abundant formations, it features numerous helictites and calcite crystallizations covering ceiling areas in the lower-level galleries. A second smaller entrance near the main one has connected to form a single unit.
full_description:
DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Geologically speaking, it develops over Lower Jurassic limestones (Lias), with breccias visible inside the cavity at some points in the upper-level galleries.

It has possibly received visits since the Balearic Bronze Age, as attested by ceramic remains from that era, along with more contemporary ones, which were used for obtaining water from inside the cave.

A singular cavity of great beauty. It has nothing to envy of other well-known cavities existing in the municipality of Pollença. All types of formations abound in it, with a large number of helictites standing out, as well as beautiful calcite crystallizations that cover some overhead zones in the lower-level galleries.

Next to the main entrance, it houses another one of reduced dimensions that was the cause of the development of a small shaft that is nowadays connected to the main cavity, forming a single unit.

name: Cova de Mina Petit
starting_point: Mina Petit estate
approach_time: 30 minutes from the Mina Petit estate
equipment: A small rope is sufficient, plus helmet and lighting
description: A cave set in a location of incredible beauty in Pollensa, with a shaft-like entrance of about 5x4 m oriented SW. The interior contains abundant formations, beautiful gours, and a remarkable play of natural light. Animal bones and ceramic fragments from pastoral water collection have been found inside. Classified as being of historic-artistic, geological, and recreational interest.
full_description:
An interesting cave occupies our attention, one that is also set in a place of incredible beauty.

From the access path to the cave, and once at its height, the cave can be glimpsed at a distance of approximately 100 meters, so we only need to approach the ravine that separates us from it and then descend to the stream, cross it, and thus reach the mouth of this cave, which has the appearance of a shaft, a couple of minutes later. The entrance dimensions are approximately 5x4 m, and the cave's direction is 225° SW.

I must say that as soon as we look over the aforementioned ravine, we can immediately observe that to our right and at a lower height, another cave mouth is clearly visible, which could make us doubt which of the two is the one we are looking for. The truth is that it is the same cave, with this mouth serving as a drain and being of no interest to us, though I must say it does have archaeological and paleontological interest.

Continuing with the description of the entrance, as I said, the mouth has all the appearance of a shaft, but it will not be necessary to use vertical progression equipment to access its interior. There are two places where we can comfortably descend to a sort of platform from which, by only one route, we can continue descending, tying a small rope to a rock if we do not feel entirely confident.

Once situated at the bottom of the cave, a beautiful plant hanging from one of the cave walls is worth noting, which is possibly maidenhair fern and well deserves to be photographed.

If we head south, there is not too much to see, but it is well worth a look, and then we can head north, crossing a small and slippery passage while to our left concretions already appear in considerable abundance. Straight ahead, we begin to ascend through a sort of narrow corridor that soon comes to an end, while to our right a group of formations extends toward the highest part of the ceiling.

Returning to the starting point and heading 296° WNW, a sort of doorway invites us to enter an interesting room, sufficiently decorated with formations, in which some beautiful gours stand out, although at the time of my visit the largest ones were empty. The ceiling height of the room at its highest point can reach 9 m.

A lower level exists beneath our feet, which can be accessed from several points, although in my humble opinion it lacks great interest.

One of the things that most draws attention in this cave is the play of light produced by the entry of outside light, which makes this place special and invites one to enjoy it.

Bones of different types of animals can be found scattered throughout the cave, suggesting that they may have fallen into it accidentally and subsequently died, unable to escape. There are also ceramic remains belonging to vessels that possibly served to collect water from the cave by local shepherds.

The Cova de Mina Petit is classified as being of historic-artistic interest, as well as geological and recreational interest. J. A. Encinas S. Relación inventarial de subterráneos naturales de las Illes Baleares. 2006. Ediciones JAES.

name: Cova de Na Barxa, de sa Bassa, o de n'Aladern
name_english: Na Barxa Cave (also: Sa Bassa Cave / N'Aladern Cave)
starting_point: Font de sa Cala. Walk from the beach, coast toward Cala Ratjada, passing Caló de n'Aladern to Punta na Baixa where the cave is located.
clothing: Shorts or a wetsuit for entering the lake
best_season: Swimming season, to also enjoy a swim
equipment: Helmet, lighting, an 8-9 m rope, and water boots
description: A coastal cave featuring a marine-influenced underground lake that must be waded or swum through to explore further chambers. The decorated interior includes a beautiful gour, a 4-meter column, and flowstones. The cave is home to a bat colony and has yielded prehistoric aquatic fauna, including numerous new crustacean species. Designated a site of special biological interest.
full_description:
With the description of the Cova de Na Barxa, we come into contact with water for the first time. What I mean is that until now many of the cavities covered by Mallorca Verde contained lakes inside them — in some cases, and as I recall at this moment, up to five as is the case with the Cova des Pont — but I had never described any cavity in which we had to wade into any of them, either to cross them or to continue the visit of the cave from the water. This is the first time we do so, and for this we have chosen one in which, if we enter in summer, it is not even necessary to wear a wetsuit.

Once we find ourselves at the entrance of the cavity, of considerable dimensions — 2.5 meters high and a little more than 1 meter wide — upon looking inside we can see that to go in we have to descend by downclimbing about 3 meters, holding onto the rock which is quite slippery (at least that is how it was the day I accessed it), so it is advisable to bring a rope in case it is needed.

Once inside, it is possible that the seawater covers the gallery a little more than a hand's width in its entirety, or that the water from the marine incursion only reaches the height of the entrance through which we have accessed. In any case, it is better to wear appropriate footwear for the water, such as simple rubber boots or mountain boots that may already be a little old. Once on dry land, we can observe how the water depth increases in the direction of the sea, although the light that could come from the outside is not visible, while if we head in the opposite direction it decreases. To continue visiting the cavity, we have to begin climbing without any danger while ducking since the passage is low in height, to access the chamber, which we can do from two different spots that are next to each other. If we do it through the one next to the right wall in the direction of travel, this one is a bit lower and at the same time a bit narrow, so perhaps — and especially if one is not very slim — the most advisable thing is to climb a little higher and then descend to the entry point of the aforementioned passage, and from that place wade into the water of the lake that is on this side of the chamber and that prevents us from continuing forward unless it is through it.

Once in the water, one must head toward the left side in the direction of travel, so we will have to hold onto the rocks with our hands in order to access a place where the depth of the lake is less, although it is not stable and can change from one time to another. By shining the light on the lake water, we can observe, thanks to its crystalline clarity, the path to follow to cross to the other side and continue the visit.

Once at the other end of the lake, we must ascend a small ramp to the highest part of the cave, which is a kind of 'plateau' from where one can have a perspective of the entire cave except for the entrance gallery. If we head to the right, going down a little, we will reach the most decorated part of the cave, where a beautiful gour stands out next to a flowstone that is undoubtedly the one that supplies it with water. Also noteworthy near the gour is an interesting column of a little more than four meters, and just behind the flowstone there is a kind of ledge that allows us to contemplate the lake and the chamber we are in from another perspective.

The dimensions of this chamber are approximately forty meters wide by the same length, where we include the beautiful lake that so embellishes this cavity. The ceiling height at the highest part I do not believe exceeds more than ten meters.

This cave serves as a refuge for a community of bats, so great care must be taken not to disturb them. A certain amount of guano (natural fertilizer created from the droppings of certain types of birds and bats) can be found in various places. Guano has been used as organic fertilizer on many occasions throughout history due to its properties.

In the lake of this cave, as well as in that of others in this coastal area — such as the Drac cave in Porto Cristo and some others — prehistoric life has been found. Starting in the eighties, and especially in the nineties, the situation regarding knowledge of troglobitic fauna in the Balearic Islands underwent a 180-degree turn. The lead researcher of the project, Damià Jaume, has described over the last ten years up to 43 new species of crustaceans, including 15 new genera and two new families for this type of fauna, especially those inhabiting the aforementioned anchialine caves. Of these new species, 18 were found in caves surveyed in the Balearic Islands.

The Cova de Na Barxa is considered a Natural Site of special biological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

name: Cova de na Mitjana
starting_point: Cross through Font de sa Cala on the main street. Past the cove, continue straight at the junction along Morras street until a left curve with a columned entrance where you can park.
best_season: Any season except when the sea is rough
equipment: 10 m rope, bolt, sling
description: An exceptionally beautiful small cave in Capdepera featuring a saltwater lake fed through cracks from the nearby sea, creating a miniature beach with fine sand and gentle waves. Densely decorated with varied speleothems including a distinctive flower-shaped stalagmite-stalactite formation. Active gours filled with water year-round and phreatic ceiling formations add to its appeal. Despite past candle-wax contamination, it remains one of the most remarkable caves on the island.
full_description:
S.O.S. Cova de Na Mitjana

While the removal of the ladder has reduced the number of visits to the cave and therefore the chances of continuing to deteriorate it at the rate it had been in the past have diminished, the damage it suffered in the past can still be seen in it. The contamination caused by the effect of the wax from candles that were lit, either to avoid getting lost inside or simply to illuminate the cave, still remains clearly visible. Let us hope that this problem is soon resolved and that this jewel of our speleology shines as in its original state.

Description.

Once the entrance has been located, a small crawlway places us at the head of the five-meter pit, where we will install the rope using both the column next to it and one of the two expansion bolts that currently exist above our heads. Once on the floor of the cave, we can observe a large group of phreatic ceiling formations that adorn the gallery preceding the arrival at the chamber that houses the lake. Few caves on our island possess in such a small area a place of such beauty as this chamber. The level of saltwater that penetrates from the sea to this place through cracks, forming the lake, has created a tiny beach of fine sand where small waves break gently, these in turn being responsible for having carpeted the lake bottom in this area with delicate ripples, and which allows us to hear them very softly, creating an idyllic atmosphere difficult to find anywhere else.

Noteworthy in this area of the cave is a beautiful stalagmite and above it a stalactite formed by a group of clustered curtain formations, giving it the shape of a flower, which makes this set of formations immediately recognizable by anyone who has visited the cave. Another characteristic of this cave are the gours it harbors, some of them of great beauty, nearly all of them active and filled with water throughout the year. The cave has a second connection with the exterior, although this one is not passable.

Practically the entire cavity is filled with multiple and varied speleothems that will allow us to enjoy some hours inside, where possibly the person describing this cave has never felt sensations equal to those I felt the first time I visited it in any other cave on the island, with the exception of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera (Llucmajor), despite its small dimensions. That is why its conservation is so important for the enjoyment of all those who come after us.

name: Cova de s'Hostalet
starting_point: C/ Santa Lavinia, Costa d'en Blanes housing development
equipment: Approximately 15 m rope, one protector, one sling for the single natural anchor point, and two carabiners
description: A small but beautiful cave discovered in December 2010 near the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization, close to the Serra de s'Hostalet trail. It consists of two chambers connected by a narrow fracture requiring rope technique to navigate. The lower chamber is richly decorated with ceiling and floor formations including beautiful curtains of various sizes, stalagmites, stalactites, and eccentric formations. Animal bones scattered in the cave suggest they were thrown in through the narrow entrance. Considered of speleological and paleontological interest.
full_description:
On December 27, 2010, this small and beautiful cavern was discovered by MALLORCA VERDE. On January 2, 2011, in the company of R. Minguillón, we headed out in the rain to carry out its exploration, unaware of what the discovery would have in store for us.

Curiously, this small cavity is located very close to one of the paths that allows entry from the "Costa d'en Blanes" housing development into the "Serra de Na Burguesa," specifically the one that runs along the "Serra de s'Hostalet."

DESCRIPTION

Once at the entrance of 1.20 x 1.60 m, one can see through it that the floor is at a shallow depth. To descend, it is recommended to install the rope on a young pine tree near the entrance and place a protector on the very edge, as otherwise the rope may suffer. Once we have "landed" in the "Entrance Hall" with dimensions of 6 x 4 m, the first impression is not too promising, despite the fact that during our visit we found specimens of a type of fungus unknown to us growing near the outside light, which "obliged" us to take out the cameras and start shooting before we accidentally destroyed them. The development of the cave runs through the S/N fracture, requiring a rebelay by placing a sling around a stone that, due to its size, blocks part of the fracture, which we had to partially clear to be able to progress. It is recommended to place the descender on the long daisy chain and a carabiner on it to eliminate the braking system so that it helps us overcome this narrow passage. Once the descent is completed, we find ourselves in the "Bottom or Final Hall" of 15.5 x 7.5 m.

The clastic processes that have developed in this cavity are clearly visible, and one can appreciate how some of the ceiling formations hang diagonally instead of vertically as would be natural. The southernmost zone of the hall partially functions as a sinkhole, gradually eroding the floor, which in the future will allow the cave to continue developing. The northern zone of the hall is heavily concretioned both on the ceiling and floor, with beautiful curtains, as well as stalagmites, barriers of the same name, and stalactites — some of them eccentric. Among the curtains, some are very large for a cavern of these dimensions, and others smaller with a serrated shape.

Bones belonging to different species can be found scattered throughout the hall, some of large size, which strongly suggests they were thrown inside since the animal they belonged to could hardly have passed through the narrow breach, which in some cases does not exceed twenty centimeters in height.

TO CONCLUDE

In short, a very interesting small cavity well worth visiting, since despite its size it has sufficient characteristics — including the fact that the visitor must possess knowledge of certain vertical progression techniques to access its depths.

As it is a cavern of small dimensions, it obviously does not fill a full day of speleology, so we will have no choice but to visit some other cavity in the area. Very close to the path and at its end is the "Avenc de sa Botilla," 461483, 4379083, 347 m, DATUM ED50. And not far from it are other cavities such as "Cova/Avenc de na Picacento" and "Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat."

Cova de s'Hostalet can be considered of speleological and paleontological interest.

name: Cova de s'Ònix
equipment: Only helmet and lighting.
description: A cave similar to other cavities in the Porto Cristo area, from which onyx was commercially extracted, leading to modifications of its interior with mining infrastructure. The cave features a beautiful lake occupying part of the lower section, adorned with abundant marine phreatic speleothems. Currently closed to the public due to danger of collapse.
full_description:
The Cova de s'Ònix is a cave with characteristics similar to other cavities in the area, from which onyx was extracted for commercialization. As a result, its interior was modified by constructing the necessary infrastructure for that purpose.

View of the beautiful lake that occupies part of the lower zone of the cavity.

Another image of the lake, in which a great quantity of marine phreatic speleothems can be appreciated.

Another beautiful corner of the cave.

name: Cova de sa Balma des Quartó
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: Divided into two distinct zones — an outer overhang (balma) and an inner cave — this cavity is set in Upper Miocene limestone. The main 'Skull Chamber' is a large collapse room about 70 meters long with a steep east-west slope, featuring beautiful calcite-covered blocks, soda straw stalactites, and delicate helictites. It is considered of speleological, archaeological, anthropological, and paleontological interest.
full_description:
DESCRIPTION

Located in the Upper Miocene limestones, this interesting cave can be divided into two differentiated zones: the more external or overhang (balma) and the internal or cave, since they are possibly two different cavities, the first being the result of erosion, while the internal one can be classified as a collapse cavity, at least the "Skull Chamber" or main chamber.

THE OVERHANG

The name "Balma des Quartó" by which it was known until now, despite having multiple meanings, possibly owes its name to the compartments into which it is divided. With the new name, we have only tried to incorporate the internal zone of the cavity into the name by which it was known.

It is a medium-sized overhang, approximately 24 x 16 x 7 m, in permanent retreat — the large quantity of blocks piled on the ground testifies to this, helping to conceal it along with the vegetation. At present, a large rock threatens to fall near the entrance to the cave. In the southernmost area of the overhang is the access to the cave through a small hole that, covered with stones, concealed it from curious visitors who might visit the area. A little further ahead, a compartment partly constructed with dry stone wall conceals a small room about four meters long by a little over two at the highest point. From it, a window connects it to the cave entrance. In a corner in the northeastern area of the overhang, there is an artificial well with a depth exceeding two meters, part of the possibly illicit — or not — archaeological excavations that have taken place in this cavern.

THE CAVE

Access to it is through the small opening cleared by R. Martínez, presenting no problem whatsoever. Once inside, the floor becomes completely horizontal and dusty, with a low ceiling that does not allow us to stand upright until several meters further on when we access the "Skull Chamber."

Entrance Zone

As soon as we access the interior of the cave, we find ourselves in a dusty place with a very low ceiling covered with a large number of roots. We advance between columns, leaving to our right a stalagmitic massif that, if we pass to the opposite side, will lead us to a small gallery which in turn ends in a small room of impossible continuity on a lower level next to the entrance.

Skull Chamber

This is a collapse chamber of large dimensions where much of the blocks fallen from the ceiling are covered by calcite flowstones. The chamber, of great beauty, will not leave us indifferent from the start. The length of the chamber is around seventy meters, and it descends through a steep slope from east to west, although we can do so heading southwest until reaching the deepest zone. We will immediately begin to get an idea of what the cave has in store for us. The ceiling of the chamber at some point is separated from the exterior by only one meter. To the east, a beautiful cascade stands out, concealing a small chamber within. Large stone blocks fallen from the ceiling pile up next to each other, some of which have been placed forming a wall of a few meters in length. We can continue advancing toward the deepest zone to the west without major problems, as we will even find a ramp of stones made artificially by human hands to facilitate our work. We will leave some surprises along the way that I will describe later. Already situated at the bottom and on a completely horizontal floor covered with sediments — this place functions as a sinkhole — we can contemplate a stalagmitic massif to the east, columns, and a group of soda straw stalactites that are close to reaching the floor, along with other types of speleothems that also abound in this place at -12.5 m from the surface and 10.5 m from the water table.

We can retrace our steps to the beginning of the chamber, from there heading east ascending, leaving a beautiful stalagmitic massif above a small chamber to our right. Once we reach the highest zone, in front of us we can observe a group of beautiful rimstone pools (gours), possibly without water. If we continue south between the stalagmitic massif now to the west and after passing through a low passage, we will access another group of rimstone pools and subsequently position ourselves on the crest of the collapse. Following it downward will take us to one of the most beautiful areas of the cavity, rich in all types of formations, with the group of soda straw stalactites standing out and where delicate helictites develop on some columns. The return can be made through a gallery that runs to the east along the edge that closes the chamber, leaving a small room to the left concealed by abundant formations. A little further on, a narrow passage will test our ability before subsequently accessing the exit zone.

The "Cova de sa Balma des Quartó" can be considered of speleological, archaeological, anthropological, and paleontological interest.

name: Cova de sa Bassa
starting_point: Park at the exit of the Monnàber tunnel, opposite the military reservoir
equipment: 15m rope for the Lower Chamber and vertical progression equipment
description: A cave with over 100 meters of development, oriented NW-SE, entered through a cliff face draped in large ivy. The main gallery descends at up to 40% grade with hand-carved footholds of possible prehistoric origin. The Galeria de Sa Bassa contains a 2x14m pool decorated with botryoidal speleothems. A Lower Chamber accessed through a concretioned funnel contains a gour and an obstructed sinkhole where prehistoric ceramic fragments were found.
full_description:
Description

We are before a cavity with a development exceeding one hundred meters, with a NW-SE orientation. Access is through an entrance where a large ivy stands out, adhered to the rock and climbing up the cliff wall, covering a large part of the "facade" in green.

Galeria de sa Ceràmica

Once inside the cavity, the descent begins through a gallery measuring 12 x 40 x 3.5 m, the result of the water's work modeling it over time. In the area near the entrance, progress is easy, but as we advance, the slope increases, reaching 40%, which can be overcome thanks to conditioning work carried out on it, consisting of excavating small hollows in the flowstone where one can place a foot. It is unknown whether this anthropic conditioning belongs to Balearic prehistory or is later; the fact is that the objective was to obtain the water stored in the small reservoir for much of the year. If there is any doubt during the descent, it is better to use the vertical progression equipment.

Galeria de Sa Bassa

This is a gallery resulting from the dissolution of conglomerate materials, measuring 4 x 34 x 2.5 m, with a NW-SE orientation that houses a bassa (pool) of 2 x 14 m that gives the cave its name. The maximum level the water reaches is 0.60 m, and the mark (paleo level) left by the water on its walls can be clearly seen, along with beautiful botryoidal speleothems that occupy the entire floodable zone. On the ceiling and walls of the gallery, the result of the water's modeling work over time can be observed, with cupolas and breccias decorating the walls being clearly visible. If one has visited the Cova de Canet (Esporles), this place will remind us of that cave. A funnel at the end of the horizontal zone connects the gallery with the Lower Chamber.

Sala Inferior (Lower Chamber)

Measuring only 3 x 5 x 3 m, it is accessed through a beautifully concretioned funnel-shaped opening. Inside, in addition to beautiful ceiling speleothems, it houses a gour, a small flowstone to the SW, and at the bottom a drain (sinkhole) partially obstructed by a rock, through which another smaller-diameter one can be seen 3 m below. Prehistoric ceramic remains were washed down to this place by water.

name: Cova de sa Campana
starting_point: Sa Calobra road MA-2141, km 9.5. Park at a small clearing on the right side where the trail begins, marked with a red paint arrow.
coordinates_utm: 483860 E, 4410785 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 483765 E, 4410580 N (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: 35m rope for camp hall access (natural anchor), 50m rope for Rampa dels Tres (2 spits), 30m rope for shaft near the great gour (2 spits and natural anchor with natural rigging point)
description: At -317m, this was Mallorca's deepest cave until surpassed by Avenc de Puig Caragoler in 2008. Its most impressive feature is the massive scale of its chambers, especially the Sala des Gegants with 10,000 m² of floor area, 320,000 m³ volume, and 30m ceilings. An overnight camp inside is recommended to fully explore its numerous halls, formations, gours, and flowstones. Classified as a site of biological and speleological interest.
full_description:
Located in the Serra de Tramuntana, in the municipality of Escorca, in a rugged and wild place, sits the Cova de sa Campana, which at -317 m had the honor of being the deepest cavity on the island of Mallorca until it was surpassed in 2008 by the Avenc de Puig Caragoler by just one meter, making that pit from that day on the deepest known. The cave entrance is located in a place known as Ses Mamelles, beneath the karren field of Es Castellolt.

What can most impress us about the Cova de sa Campana is undoubtedly the dimensions of its chambers. There is no other known cavity on the island with chambers of that size, except for the "Sala que no Té Nom" of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera. Notable among them is the "Sala des Gegants" or "Sala de Gràcia," which with its 10,000 square meters, 320,000 cubic meters, and a ceiling height in some points of 30 meters has the honor of being the largest chamber in the cavity.

To make the most of the day, it is recommended to spend the night inside the cave, right in the entrance hall also known as the "Sala del Campament," and thus begin the visit first thing in the morning. The humidity in the cavity is enormous, so if we decide to spend the night in the cave, it is essential to bring a good sleeping bag and a mat to insulate us from the ground — I insist, a good sleeping bag in addition to the mat is essential, otherwise we will regret it.

The approach to the cave, of approximately 40 minutes, will be done walking across a karren field through which we will have to traverse loaded with all the equipment, so be careful where we place our feet, as if we lose balance with the load we could suffer an accident that will surely ruin the visit.

The entrance, approximately two meters high, is easy to find since the path in that area is quite well defined and surrounded by reeds, and we will have no difficulty putting on our equipment. As soon as we enter the cave, we will encounter a metal barrier in a state of considerable deterioration that once served to prevent access. A few meters of downclimbing and we will soon spot the columns that will serve us to set up the first rigging. The cave continues descending several more meters before turning left and allowing us, on a natural anchor, to place the only rebelay for the descent to the "Sala del Campament," which in its first section is quite well lit by light from outside. Now without a rope, we continue descending — be careful of slips, as the floor is usually slippery — until reaching a completely flat surface where we will set up camp. This hall already gives us an idea of what we will find in the rest of the cave, as some of the speleothems found here are very large. Noteworthy among other speleothems in this place are two discs: one small one on the floor behind a gour on the left side, and another much larger one on the right, suspended on the wall right where the cave continues. Already in this hall, a group of large eccentric formations can be seen.

The second hall we will encounter is the aforementioned "Sala de Gràcia," which leaves no one indifferent. A companion told me he had never felt so small before in any other cavity, and it is true — everything that surrounds us is of colossal dimensions, both the stalagmites and some parietal flowstones, as well as a great number of blocks among which we will move with great care to continue the visit. Special mention goes to the great number of gours found in the upper part of the hall, clustered one next to another, making this corner one of the most beautiful in the cave.

If we want to photograph this hall, we should bring along people to help illuminate it, something very difficult since its dimensions seem to swallow the light from the flashes, with the consequent frustration upon verifying the lack of light after the exposure.

We will continue on our way, descending while heading toward the left end of the hall, passing near a gigantic flowstone until reaching a corner where we can see two spits on the wall that will serve to install the second rope, to which we will attach a deviation on a natural anchor formed by a decapitated stalagmite. We descend to the bottom, about 6 or 7 meters, where we will install a rebelay on a natural anchor, and from this point begin the descent down the "Rampa dels Tres" until reaching a kind of landing where we will do without the rope to continue descending through a sort of channel in which steps have been carved, though always using all five senses. Once at the bottom, if we look back we will see that we are still in the "Sala de Gràcia."

On the left side and under an arch, we will access the so-called "Sala Bonica," which serves as the antechamber to the long and arduous crawl that, in continuous descent, ends at a place where a pointed stone creates the greatest difficulty of the crawl. We only need to look for a cairn that will allow us to place our foot on it and overcome this obstacle before entering the head of the "Sala del Fang," covered in large blocks and mud, so the descent will be done carefully, sliding if necessary by resting our backsides on the ground. Once at the bottom of the hall, we can note the white formations of the place that contrast with the dark color of the mud that coats it. A curiosity of some of these formations is the displacement that exists between some stalactites and the stalagmite below them — it remains to be determined which of the two types of speleothems has shifted. There are also numerous gours in this place as well as beautiful flowstones.

The next hall we will access, on the left side, is the so-called "Sala des Gurs," from which the misnamed "shafts" depart — which are nothing other than the end of the cavity that would lead to the deepest part of the cave, which we will not address at this time. In the deepest part of the hall there is an impressive stalagmitic flowstone surrounded by multiple small gours, and further to the left is the largest and deepest gour in the cavity, fed by water sliding down a beautiful flowstone. Leaving the large gour to the right and a few meters on, before reaching the end of the hall, there is a hidden shaft of about 25 or 30 meters that can be accessed using the two existing spits first, and then a stalagmite inside the shaft itself on which we will hang a sling that will take us to a first rebelay and subsequently to the bottom. Near this shaft there is again another disc of large dimensions, somewhat camouflaged due to its verticality.

At this point ends the misnamed "tourist" zone of the cavity, giving way to the more technical zone that we will not address in this first approach to the cave.

The Cova de sa Campana is considered a site of biological interest as well as specifically speleological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory listing of natural subterranean features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

For further reading:

Daniel Mayoral, Tomeu Mateu. MALLORCA BEAUTY IN THE DARKNESS. Published by Espeleo Mallorca SC 2006.

Page under construction.

name: Cova Figuera-Carrotja d'en Servera
starting_point: Ma-4023 road between Porto Cristo and Cala Millor
access: From Palma: TIB Bus Line 412 or own vehicle
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Helmet and lighting only.
description: Classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC), requiring authorization. The entrance hall is the largest room at 2,500 m² with massive stalagmitic formations and columns. The cave extends through multiple chambers connected by crawlways and tight passages, including the aptly named 'Suffering Crawl' and several galleries with pools connecting to the marine water table. Features include retraction polygons, abrasion cupolas, and a paleosol layer.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova des Diners is classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as LIC.

At present (see the date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Conselleria de Medio Ambiente (Department of the Environment). Therefore, before making any visit, the mandatory permit must be requested to be able to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Entrance Chamber (Sala de s'Entrada)

This is undoubtedly the largest room in the cavity at 2,500 m². Over time it has undergone major anthropic modifications with the intention of making it more habitable, although the exact uses it may have served are not known with certainty. Everything indicates that the deepest zone of the cave may have been used as a livestock shelter. There are several stone troughs distributed throughout this area of the chamber, placed in strategic locations to collect water from dripping.

This chamber houses the largest formations in the cave in the form of stalagmitic massifs and columns. In the northern zone of the chamber, subsidence processes are clearly visible in many of its formations.

Low Chamber (Sala Baixa)

The subsidence that occurred at some point in the northeast of the cave gave rise to the formation of this small chamber of 20 x 28 m, with a very low ceiling where one cannot stand upright in almost any part of it. Scarce in formations, it is instead abundant in multiple roots. Also noteworthy in the chamber are the existence of several surface fractures, with separations ranging from a few centimeters to nearly two meters in width and similar depth.

Suffering Crawlway (Gatera des Sofriment)

Located in the southern zone of the Entrance Chamber, traversing it lives up to its name. Although it has beautiful but scarce formations, accessing some of the places where they are found can put the suffering speleologist to the test.

The Trench (Sa Trinxera)

With a length of 5 m, its height does not reach one meter, and what it almost never lacks along its course is enough water to give us a welcome of mud.

Column Chamber (Sala de sa Columna)

Once the trench is crossed, we access this chamber of 33 x 20 x 2.5 m. There are not too many speleothems in the chamber, although near the entrance there is a beautiful corner formed by a pair of columns and a lovely ceiling decorated with beautiful soda straw stalactites. Another isolated column at the end of the chamber has contributed its name. Noteworthy is the clay surface of a floor that is always damp and a solitary column in the farthest part of the chamber.

Paleosol Chamber (Sala des Paleosòl)

After crawling through a truly low-ceilinged passage from the Column Chamber, we access this chamber of 11 x 45 x 2 m. One traverses it on a gentle descending slope across very slippery clay ground until finally reaching horizontality. In the northern zone of the chamber, a small isolated column surrounded by delicate stalactites can be seen, this being one of the few places in the chamber with speleothems. Also noteworthy is a band of calcite crystallizations that runs from north to south along the eastern side of the chamber, which was exposed after the collapse of an ancient floor.

The Cupolas (Ses Cupules)

This area of the cavity can be located on the topographic map with the number 9 and has a surface area of approximately 260 m². It can be accessed from the letter "F" on the topographic map or more directly from number 14 in the Entrance Chamber, although this route is more arduous, having to overcome the narrowness of the opening and the low height of this gallery throughout the entire route. From here, three branches depart in different directions that will be detailed later. Noteworthy in this place are the wells, two large pools, and multiple cupolas, some of large dimensions that in at least one case communicate with each other.

Connection Gallery (Galeria de sa Conexió)

This is a crawlway that allows faster access to or exit from the cave, not without certain difficulties. Its dimensions are: 7 x 31 x 1.5 m at the highest point, although in practically the entire gallery the average height rarely exceeds 0.5 m. The most noteworthy feature of this crawlway are the two groups of retraction polygons separated by cracks in an advanced state of consolidation.

Gallery of the Two Branches (Galeria de ses dues Ramificacions)

Starting from the Cupolas, with a main NW-SE direction, this gallery of 31 m in length has two branches that emerge just before the end with NE-SW and N-S directions.

There are not too many things to note in this gallery, except for a well connecting to the water table, some cupolas, and a passage that does not exceed 30 cm in height. Also visible in some areas is a strip of "Terra Rossa" at ceiling level covered with a thin black film on the underside with an apparently erosive appearance.

Deep Well Gallery (Galeria des Pou Profund)

Starting from the westernmost pool, it takes a NW-SE direction for 28 m. As soon as we enter it, we find a well in which a water depth of approximately -3 m can be seen. Little else to note about this gallery, except for a couple of cupolas above the well and a couple more at the end.

Final Gallery (Galeria Final)

With its 8 x 50 x 1.5 m and NE-SW orientation, it reaches the zone farthest from the cave's exterior, housing a well of -2.5 m that connects to the marine water table, as well as some ascending abrasion cupolas of large dimensions. Reaching the final zone of this gallery requires overcoming significant narrowings, especially in the last section.

name: Cova de sa Germaneria
starting_point: Ma-1032 road km 8 (Coll des Moli des Vent), GR-221 variant towards Calvià
equipment: 15 m rope and vertical progression equipment
description: A large karstic cave system accessed through a circular natural entrance formed by collapse. It comprises multiple interconnected chambers richly decorated with stalactites, stalagmites, columns, flowstones, and numerous rimstone pools (gours), including the Speleothems Hall with its forest-like array of dark manganese-oxide-coated formations. A 15 m rope and vertical progression equipment are needed to descend into the Labyrinth Hall via a beautiful flowstone cascade.
full_description:
Description

The main orientation of the cavity is NE-SE and access is through a natural entrance with a circular shape. It is the result of a collapse (a few meters further south there is another older and smaller entrance that is not used today); beyond it there is a portal flanked by two thick stalagmites that leads to the Entrance Hall.

Entrance Hall

With a NW-SE orientation and dimensions of 25 x 21 x 4 m, this is a hall resulting from the successive collapses that have occurred over time. The ceiling presents a tepee morphology that reminds us of the Blocks Hall in the Cova de ses Meravelles, Bunyola. Also noteworthy are the corrosion channels that extend horizontally along the northern wall near the entrance. The origin of this morphology should possibly be sought in a lacustrine or restricted fluvial context.

In the hall there are a great number of formations, some of them very large. In the NW zone there is a beautiful corner formed by two rimstone pools (gours) that receive water through a lovely flowstone that functions as a waterfall and collects water directly from the outside through a kind of "chimney." Other rimstone pools can be found in various places in the hall, notably the one in the NE zone next to the escarpment connecting this hall with the Labyrinth Hall, where one can appreciate the anthropic conditioning work intended to allow it to hold a greater quantity of water. Near the rimstone pool one can also see some columns affected by subsidence processes. In the SE zone, just above the escarpment leading down to the Speleothems Hall, one can see a "chimney" currently blocked, which surely served as an important water filtration point in past times. Nearby, due to the corrosion of the rock turning it to powder, a beautiful cupola has formed.

Speleothems Hall

An easy 3 m downclimb on the eastern side of the escarpment separating it from the Entrance Hall places us in the most extensively developed hall of the cave, with dimensions of 24 x 33 x 6 m and a N-S orientation. It is a horizontal hall, also of the collapse type, in which lithochemical seepage has been abundant, harboring large formations, most of them covered by a dark layer, possibly caused by manganese oxide precipitated on their surface, giving the sensation of being in a stone forest where the dark-colored "trees" are pressed closely together.

Once in the hall, we can head to our left toward a large block from which, if we climb on top of it, we can contemplate one of the curiosities of this cave: bulbous-shaped speleothems suspended from the ceiling, vestiges of ancient paleo-levels. This type of speleothem can also be seen in the Cova de Ses Baldufes (Palma).

Descending a small ramp, rimstone pools of various sizes appear in our path. Again, a stalagmitic massif of considerable size affected by subsidence has tilted a large speleothem.

The cave becomes horizontal and is divided by several columnar massifs into three large compartments whose floors are carpeted with rimstone pools, one of them reaching 4 m in width by 7 m in length.

As we advance, the hall gradually narrows until it practically ends in a point, connecting on the eastern side with Es Racó (The Corner).

Es Racó

To the SE of the deepest zone of the Speleothems Hall lies this gallery, semi-hidden behind a stalagmitic massif, which connects to the north with the Lightbulbs Gallery. Separating it from the latter are a couple of rimstone pools, one of them of large dimensions coming from the Speleothems Hall. One of the notable things about the gallery is the large number of stalactite fragments scattered across the floor along its length.

Lightbulbs Gallery

In the central zone of the Speleothems Hall, on its western side, behind a stalagmitic barrier, one accesses through a small window this gallery with approximate dimensions of 4 x 20 m, noteworthy for the half dozen rimstone pools it contains. Just on the other side of the window, already inside the gallery, we find ourselves inside a rimstone pool of considerable dimensions in which one can observe epiaquatic speleothems of bulbous morphology, the result of the coating of the original stalactite, with characteristics similar to the so-called "lightbulbs" found in the Cova des Ases (Felanitx) and especially in the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera (Llucmajor), although their state of conservation is not the same. The remains of fires in the form of charcoal and smoke impregnated on the edges of the rimstone pools and walls are possibly due to visits this place received in the past with the intention of collecting the water stored in the rimstone pools.

Traversing the entire gallery requires us to get through a rather narrow passage, as well as having to climb to exit it at its northern end, rejoining the Speleothems Hall near the escarpment separating it from the Entrance Hall.

Labyrinth Hall

Returning to the Entrance Hall, the continuation of the cave is found in the northern zone, where through a window we can observe the hall, needing to install a rope of about twelve meters on a formation in order to descend along a beautiful flowstone. Although there are hints of steps carved into the flowstone, the use of vertical progression equipment is recommended to avoid a fright or something worse.

It is a horizontal hall of 12 x 17 x 3.5 m, completely ornamented mainly by around fifty columns, where the floor is full of stalactite fragments. We could divide the hall into two clearly differentiated zones through an imaginary cut from west to east: the southern zone is occupied by a large flowstone with an inclination ranging from 40° to 60°, while the completely horizontal northern zone is occupied by a large number of columns.

Everything seems to indicate that at some point this place functioned as a sinkhole and possibly still does through a small orifice located in the NW zone of the hall.

The hall's name comes from graffiti found on a column with that name.

Roots Gallery

This is a gallery approximately 16 m long that marks the end of the cave on the western side. It has two directions: the first NE-SW and the second NW-SE. The entire eastern zone of the gallery is composed of a flowstone on whose upper part reconstructive processes are abundant.

The Hidden Rimstone Pools

This area connects with the Roots Gallery; in reality it is the continuation on its eastern side. It is a small corner that houses half a dozen beautiful rimstone pools, some of them active, surrounded by columns that make passage difficult mainly due to their low height.

name: Cova de sa Guitarreta
starting_point: Km 32 of the MA-6014 road, follow the path to the entrance of Possessió de Sa Guitarreta
equipment: 10m rope for a small drop in the SE zone of Sala dets Ratspenats (drop under 3m). Entry shaft can be easily downclimbed.
description: A LIC-protected cave accessed through a sinkhole entrance, featuring a 16.5m chimney with visible horizontal strata and a large bat chamber (Sala dets Ratspenats, 18x19x9m). Reaches -58m depth with a phreatic lake of saline water at a constant 26°C. Extremely challenging with very tight passages and temperatures reaching 30°C.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova de sa Guitarreta is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities catalogued as LIC. Currently (see date on the cave's information sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Ministry of the Environment and Territory, so before making any visit, the required permit must be obtained to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

During our exploratory work of the cavity, we observed two things that we consider important to address in this description of the cave. On one hand, as already mentioned in the introduction, it is clear that access to the interior of the cavity is currently through the entrance of a shaft that functions as a sinkhole, capturing rainwater from the ephemeral doline in which it is located, until it is lost in the depths of the earth. On the other hand, the cave opens through a normal fracture in an approximately NNE direction.

ACCESS

A shaft mouth three meters in diameter allows access to the interior of the cave through a ramp with a steep slope at the beginning, which later levels out to an average inclination of 30 degrees, until reaching the place where an old gate was installed with the purpose of allowing the bats that use the cave as a roosting and breeding site to pass through easily. The gate was removed for not meeting the necessary requirements for its intended purpose.

From this point, we continue descending without difficulty while passing through a zone where clasts abound, some of considerable size. Some steps carved in the rock facilitate passage until we finally reach the base of the "chimney," located just before accessing the Sala dets Ratpenats (Bat Chamber).

THE CHIMNEY (LA XEMENEIA)

This shaft or chimney has a height of 16.5 m, ending in a dome. To ascend to the highest zone of the shaft, in addition to the vertical progression equipment, the necessary materials are required, as nothing is installed. During our exploratory work, we used self-tapping screws, which were removed once finished, leaving zero impact on the cave. It is truly a singular place, as on its walls the horizontal strata become visible, giving the appearance of large wheels stacked one on top of the other.

THE UPPER GALLERY (SA GALERIA DE DALT)

It is located in the upper zone of the Chimney, with a length that does not reach a dozen meters and a cross-shaped morphology. It currently receives rainwater from the exterior, as is clearly visible on its surface, where a multitude of stones of different sizes accumulate, which during winter the water drags and precipitates down the chimney, posing a danger to anyone who might be directly below.

BAT CHAMBER (SALA DETS RATSPENATS)

Once we enter the main chamber of the cavity, measuring 18 x 19 x 9 m, we are impressed on one hand by the sonar pulses emitted by the bats as they fly over our heads, and on the other by the beauty of this collapse chamber where a large quantity of blocks accumulate in the eastern zone from north to south, as a result of past collapses that could recur in the eastern zone at any moment. The guano, which is abundant mainly in the central zone of the chamber, supports a cave-dwelling fauna that lives in it, and together with the strong smell of urine, makes staying in the chamber uncomfortable if prolonged.

There are two options to continue visiting the rest of the cave. The first is through a small crawlway located in the SW zone of the chamber, which ends in a drop of just over two meters. Once descended, to the left one can observe where the cave collects rainwater. To continue visiting the cave, we must go straight ahead, accessing an upper level where the exploration can continue. The second option starts from the NW zone of the Sala dets Ratpenats, from the top of the blocks (it is clearly visible). It is an opening in which, once entered, we immediately turn to the left a couple of meters and then descend again to the right down a very steep ramp. Once descended, if we turn right about four meters, we will converge with the previously described passage, letter "J" on the topography.

SUFFERING ZONE (ZONA DES PATIMENT)

It becomes highly complicated to continue describing the rest of the cavity from this point, as it involves advancing wherever the cave allows, trying not to take any wrong access while attempting to keep descending as we advance in order to reach the deepest zone of the cavity. The most advisable approach would perhaps be to use the topography with a compass. Along the way, we will encounter some downclimbs of considerable slope that are overcome without great difficulty because the distance between the floor and the ceiling is sometimes only centimeters. In this zone of the cave, the temperature reaches 30 degrees Celsius, dropping to 26 or 27 degrees near the phreatic lake.

THE PHREATIC LAKE (EL LLAC FREÀTIC)

A small orifice of approximately 50 cm in diameter at the -58 m level of the cave allows the cave diver to submerge, not without difficulty, into the phreatic underwater zone, whose passable dimensions are approximately half a dozen meters wide by just over a meter deep and ten meters in length. At both ends as well as in the deepest zone, the cave continues, but it is not passable.

Notable is the thermal anomaly of the brackish waters at this location, with an apparently stable temperature of approximately 26 degrees Celsius, which keeps that zone of the cave at about 27 degrees Celsius, as opposed to the central zone where it reaches 30 degrees Celsius.

Note: This is an extremely demanding cave. Besides the difficulty in orientation, one must be prepared to move through extremely narrow passages and endure temperatures that in some zones reach 30 degrees Celsius, all accompanied by high humidity.

name: Cova de sa Lluna Plena
starting_point: Cala Morlanda
best_season: Avoid summer due to potentially high carbon dioxide concentration inside
equipment: No equipment needed except helmet
description: Hidden among dense vegetation, this modestly-sized cave presents some difficulty in progression due to its low ceiling height. Although the entrance and the passage to the interior lake have been artificially widened, movement inside remains challenging. The cave is well-concretioned with abundant calcite deposits covering walls and ceilings.
full_description:
Hidden among the dense vegetation cover, this modestly-sized cavity is located which, nevertheless, presents certain difficulties in progression throughout its development.

Although both the access mouth and the passage toward the interior lake have been subject to artificial widening, circulation through its interior presents certain difficulties due to its low height.

The cavity is well concretioned, with abundant calcite deposits covering walls and ceilings.

Preparing the entrance of the cave, in order to be able to access the interior without too many difficulties.

This photograph and the one below give us a clue about the condition of the terrestrial zone of the cavity.

name: Cova de sa Piqueta
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma-4014 road connecting Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, a path leads approximately 1.5 km to a half-open metal gate with a chain. After crossing it, a trail leads first to Cala Varques, then along the coast to Cala Falcó, from where a track approaches the cave.
equipment: In addition to basic equipment, bring a 15-20 m rope to access the lake area.
description: A cave at the bottom of a 26x36 m sinkhole with a 12x3 m arched entrance, part of a connected system with Cova del Pont and Cova del Pirata. The interior is chaotic with large fallen rock blocks and few formations, though the far end hides beautiful small speleothems including a geode with reddish crystallizations, bluish transparent crystals, and serrated curtain formations. A small lake connects to the Pont cave system underwater.
full_description:
We are before a cave that until not long ago was treated as a single unit, until a work published in the journal ENDINS No. 29 of 2006 by Francesc GRÀCIA, Bernat CLAMOR, Joan J. FORNÓS, Damià JAUME, and Mateu FEBRER revealed the connectivity that exists between the Cova de sa Piqueta and the Cova del Pont and the Cova del Pirata. Although the three caves are part of the same system, we are going to treat them individually, as we already did with their two companions. The only cave that escapes being part of this system is the Cova des Xots, which is located near the Cova del Pont at one end of the doline, coordinates 525850 E, 4373360 N, DATUM EUROPEAN 1950.

The Cova de sa Piqueta is located at the bottom of a doline measuring 26 x 36 m, with an arch-shaped entrance of 12 x 3 m (M. Trias, 1977). Access to the interior of the cave is currently done after passing through dense vegetation that hides it from the eyes of people who may walk nearby, and then downclimbing a ramp on which large stones abound, being careful not to step on them to avoid any of them striking a companion below.

The first impression the cave gives us is of a fairly chaotic cave due to the large blocks of stone fallen from the ceiling and devoid of beauty — that was at least the first impression I received when I entered the cave. Later, as I will recount further on, everything changed, although I must note that we are before a cavity that, unlike many of its neighbors, does not abound in concretions.

If we head in a NE direction, we can descend to the small lake that connects the cave with the Cova del Pont. To do so, one must at least use a support rope, as the slippery condition of the floor can send us plunging into the void. The lake would lack interest were it not for the fact that through it the connection with the Pont zone of the system is made underwater.

At the opposite end of the cave from the entrance, which we can easily access from the left side, is the most beautiful part of the cave — although one must search for this beauty carefully, as the speleothems we will find are very small and we can walk right past them without seeing them. The concretioning in question is found in a narrow zone strongly affected by the clastic process, where there is even a high probability of collapse, since during the long time I spent photographing the different speleothems, small stones continuously fell, seeming to warn of an imminent collapse. One of the most interesting things we found was a geode (a rocky cavity, normally closed, in which minerals dissolved in groundwater have crystallized, whose crystals are large due to the low pressure at which they formed) that contained in its interior interesting crystallizations of a reddish color, undoubtedly due to the color of the local soil. Very close to the aforementioned geode, in a crack, interesting crystallizations of a bluish, almost transparent color hung. Curtain formations abound in this place, some with serrated shapes and others covered with tiny crystallizations.

In summary, we are before a cave whose interior is piled with large quantities of stone blocks that hinder our movement through it, partly resembling some parts of the Pont zone of this system. At first glance, it is less interesting to visit, as demonstrated by the dense vegetation that hides its entrance, but I nevertheless recommend it.

name: Cova de sa Punta
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint (Génova)
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Helmet and lighting only.
description: A cave in the Na Burguesa range that was historically used for gypsum extraction, resulting in the loss of most calcite formations. It consists of a large main chamber accessible by hand-climbing, with two short wings at the rear. A traversable crack on the right side still preserves some formations, including small columns broken by ground subsidence. Located in an area with a high concentration of caves and mines.
full_description:
From the day when, searching for the Coves des Mármol, I decided to descend cross-country from the Mirador de Alzamora, this area of the Serra de Na Burguesa has never stopped surprising me, since although a great number of caves and avencs (pits) are known in the range, it never crossed my mind that I would find such a large concentration in so little terrain. In this place there are truly all kinds of caves — some of them were used to extract gypsum, possibly by the Arabs, and later after the Spanish Civil War, when the international blockade caused many resources to become scarce, another mine was installed in the Cova des Mármol to extract said material, destroying it and splitting it in two. Currently none of these mines are in operation, mines that besides being useful for obtaining gypsum and marble also "served" to destroy the natural caves.

In the satellite photo one can observe the concentration of cavities in this place.

The Cova de sa Punta was also used to extract gypsum (the mineral calcium sulfate dihydrate), and this is why most of the calcareous concretions have disappeared, with only some remaining at the entrance, mainly on the right side.

Contrary to the Coma Miner de s'Aliga, where to extract the gypsum from the cavity a gallery was built underneath it to remove the gypsum rock, the extractions from this cavity were done through the entrance via a ramp built for that purpose, subsequently letting it slide down the mountainside to the bottom of the valley, from where it was brought to the kiln.

The cave consists of a large chamber which we can access using our hands to descend to the bottom, and then move through it easily as it is completely horizontal. Once we reach the back of the cave, two wings open up, one on each side, of short length.

Just as we enter the cavity, on the right side, a traversable crack allows us to walk at an angle through it, where we can appreciate some concretions and observe how some small columns have been broken in half due to ground subsidence.

On my third visit to the cave, accompanied by J. Encinas and his wife Lourdes, both experienced experts, we took the opportunity to explore it, name it, and survey it so that it would be registered.

Once the visit to the cavity is finished, about 15 m to the right of the Cova de sa Punta there is another small cavity considered of archaeological interest that we can visit. And if we have time, in the photo one can see the location of the rest of the cavities in case we are interested in locating them.

name: Cova de sa Reineta o des Ranxo des Coll
name_english: Reineta Cave (or Ranxo des Coll Cave)
starting_point: Cas Garrigues (Bunyola)
best_season: Any, although in winter the approach can be shorter
equipment: 25m rope if accessing the deeper areas
description: A subvertical cave near Cas Garrigues featuring a notable downward slope, abundant fallen blocks, and subsidence features. The cave contains a wide variety of speleothems throughout the middle and upper zones, allowing comfortable exploration and photography. A rope is required to reach the deepest areas due to the considerable incline.
full_description:
INTRODUCTION

The most advisable approach to this cavity can be made from the Cas Garrigues recreation area. The only problem is that for several months, vehicle traffic is prohibited to approach this place, and from there it is practically a stroll to reach the cave. Therefore, a second option is to make the approach via Es Cocons, which will take us approximately two hours.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

A sinkhole near the path, easy to locate, which can be easily accessed without the need for vertical progression equipment. Once upright, it is easy to traverse a large part of this subvertical cave with a notable slope in the areas farthest from the exterior. Abundant in this cavity is the presence of blocks as well as multiple detachments caused by subsidence processes.

The cave harbors a large quantity of varied speleothems, with which one can spend a good while taking photographs, being able to move through the cave with relative comfort through the middle and upper zones. If one wishes to access the deepest zone, the use of vertical progression equipment is necessary, since as I mentioned earlier, the slope is considerable.

For more information:

https://drive.google.com/file/d/1u0AjqGiel2qtKYfdIiWJI-4o0VOoX86v/view

http://ccmallorca.net/informes.htm

name: Cova de sa Sorpresa
coordinates_utm: Available at the federation (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950 / DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A somewhat overlooked cave near the repeater towers road in Calvià with N/S orientation. It contains multiple chambers connected by narrow crawlways and slopes, culminating in a beautiful final hall with formations spanning wall to wall. Notable features include the Myotragus Room, which contained preserved remains of the extinct Balearic goat, and the Pou Room with abundant concretions. The cave is richly decorated with gours, columns, flags, soda straw stalactites, and eccentric formations.
full_description:
Description

Next to the repeater towers road and near "Escletxa de sa Fusta," with a N/S orientation, lies this cavity, perhaps somewhat overlooked, possibly due to an incorrect assessment of it. The entrance opens onto a flat surface of the terrain.

Entrance Hall

To access the hall, one must first sit on the edge, then place the feet on a block that has fallen from the cave, and from there complete the descent. The hall, approximately 8 x 5 m, is not of great interest; sparse in formations, it penetrates into the depths of the earth to a depth of seven meters, although it possibly continues beyond the stones that block the passage.

Main Hall

The continuation of the cave runs in a NW direction through a kind of narrow crawlway whose surface is formed by a pavement flowstone approximately seven meters long, on which small stalagmitic formations are fixed that will allow us to hold onto them with our hands while crawling, and at the same time serve as support for our feet.

To continue deeper into the cave, we descend along a ramp with an average inclination of 30°. In the first part, over a floor covered with earth and loose stones, it is recommended to go one at a time to prevent any stones from being dislodged by a companion's passage; we can take shelter behind a massif where we can also regain a standing position. Before that, we will have left a small hidden chamber to our right which we will visit on the way back. On the left side, nothing worth noting — in some areas the ceiling and floor meet, making it impassable. A little further down, we reach a much more horizontal zone where ancient ceiling formations stand out. To our right (SW), a passage opens to another hall that I will describe on the return. In a descending direction, a window opens the way to the final hall.

Final Hall

A window opens before us indicating the way to follow. Carefully, so as not to break any of the formations — especially a very thin stalagmite of approximately one meter — and with the same care, we avoid damaging some small rimstone pools that stand in our way. We find ourselves in the most beautiful zone of the cave, where a barrier of formations crosses the hall from one side to the other. Once past the barrier on the right side, we continue descending until reaching a small pool at -15 m that marks the end of the cave on this side, where moving through the eastern end becomes difficult given the proximity between the floor and ceiling, as well as the great quantity of formations in the form of columns of all thicknesses, and above all the thousands of small stalactites. On the way back toward the exit, we will visit the two halls we have yet to see and which had to be cleared.

Well Hall

We access it once past the window, entering through a narrow shaft down which we downclimb for 5 m, then head NW until reaching the bottom of the hall, with dimensions of 13 x 4.5 m at its widest point. A very narrow 5.5 m shaft reaches the deepest zone of the cavity. The hall is oriented SE/NW with a low ceiling, abundantly concretioned; some small columns are affected by a solifluction process.

Myotragus Hall

As with the "Well Hall," to access the "Myotragus Hall" the entrance had to be cleared with considerable difficulty due to the lack of space to use the geologist's pick, which was the tool employed. It is a hall of 13 x 10 m that in turn contains a small nook in its upper zone. It is a very interesting hall since it contained inside it remains of a Myotragus, whose head was reasonably well preserved despite being incomplete. A very fine soda straw stalactite immediately draws attention due to its size of approximately 2 m, displaced horizontally with respect to the stalagmite that it once helped to form. Another thick stalagmite develops in what was once a small pond, currently dry due to the inactivity of the hall.
