Stalactites of great age with curious shapes hang from the ceiling alongside younger ones. In the SW zone of the hall, abundant blocks of stones cemented together separate it from the "Well Hall."

Speleothems.

Although certain zones of the cavity are devoid of ornamentation, in others they abound both in quantity and variety: one can find everything from rimstone pools, to columns of varied sizes and thicknesses, very fine stalagmites, curtains, and helictites, some of them eccentric formations of great beauty. Solifluction processes are present in some of the formations.

name: Cova de sa Terra
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: 15 m rope
description: Located near Cova dets Albons, this N/S oriented cave follows the typical Sierra morphology of fracture ramps with subsequent widening. The Sala de ses Espeleotemes features wall and floor flowstones with various stalagmites, while Sa Rampa descends 14 m at a 45% gradient. Ceramic remains suggest it was historically used as a water source. The cave hosts bats and is considered a site of special speleological interest.
full_description:
At the base of a small escarpment near the Cova dets Albons, the mouth of this cave opens, hidden behind vegetation that conceals a small sinkhole. The orientation of the cavity is N/S, following the same direction as its neighbor Cova de sa Sorpresa. Its morphology does not differ from many others in the sierra, one of its main characteristics being fractures in ramp form with a subsequent widening, only to then end in a closure with no possibility of continuation.

Sala de sa Entrada (Entrance Room)

Just after crossing the entrance, we access a small room on a ramp of 8 x 9 x 2.5 m whose deepest area is occupied by a large number of stones that prevent its continuation. The room lacks interest, although in another time it surely had some as a place where people who made their living in the area would come for water, as attested by some ceramic remains found in the room.

Sala de ses Espeleotemes (Speleothem Room)

To the east, a narrow yet short gallery allows us to access the Speleothem Room. To do so, we must pay the utmost attention, since at the exit of the aforementioned gallery, one must make a small jump until placing our feet on a small stalagmite that positions us in the room — a loss of balance during the jump would surely cause us to fall down a steep slope until reaching the deepest area of the cave. The dimensions of the room are 7 x 12 x 2.5 m. It is a practically horizontal room with a certain amount of concretion, where the parietal flowstones in the NW area stand out, as well as the floor flowstones that have cemented the stones on the room's floor and upon which stalagmites of different sizes and thicknesses have developed. Some small rimstone pools can also be found, though they are empty since the cave's activity is nonexistent. Ceiling speleothems are also quite abundant, though not of great beauty.

Sa Rampa (The Ramp)

With dimensions of 16 x 8 x 3 m and a ramp of approximately 45% gradient, it descends for 14 m over a cemented floor where deposited stones are found that can tumble at the slightest touch of the rope. Once at the bottom, the surface is covered by materials that have accumulated over time, blocking the way. However, along the western side, one can still continue descending with great difficulty about 3 more meters until reaching a small inaccessible shaft.

The Cova de sa Terra is the place chosen by some bat specimens to roost, so they should not be disturbed.

The Cova de sa Terra can be considered a natural site of special speleological interest.

name: Cova de sa Tortuga
starting_point: Cala Varques beach
equipment: Not necessary
description: Located near Cala Barcas, this cave is concealed by vegetation and requires navigating a tight entrance followed by a challenging traverse. Inside, a lake with crystal-clear waters is partially covered by a collapsed ceiling platform. The cave features notable speleothems showing subsidence-related displacement, abundant phreatic formations, and delicate soda-straw stalactites extending into the lake waters.
full_description:
Near the small cove of Cala Barcas, this cavity is found hidden among the vegetation. Access to the interior of the cavity is through an entrance of reduced dimensions, while the rest of the traverse will make us "suffer" for a few minutes until we reach the ramp of dirt and loose stones that will deposit us near the southern zone of the lake.

As we continue, leaving the lake water to our left, we can appreciate to our right a large group of formations in which subsidence processes that occurred in the cave can be observed, causing them to shift both vertically and horizontally. These processes may have occurred due to the dissolution of existing materials when being "attacked" by the brackish waters of the lake.

To continue the visit, one must climb to the right to reach a platform that is nothing other than the collapsed ceiling resulting from a collapse that occupied part of the lake. On said platform, in addition to stepping on the collapsed zone, there is an interesting variety of speleothems.

The lake holds crystal-clear waters, as well as some corners in the more remote areas where phreatic speleothems are abundant, not to mention the delicate speleothems popularly known as "macaroni" (soda straws), which on some occasions extend into the interior of the lake waters.

name: Cova de Sant Martí
starting_point: Located 100 m from the MA-3470 road between km 2 and 3. Access is signposted and you can park beside the entrance.
footwear: Appropriate footwear for entering a cave
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting. Neoprene suit if entering the water.
description: Currently closed to the public. A natural cave linked to early Christian worship in Alcúdia, featuring two medieval Gothic chapels dedicated to Sant Martí and Sant Jordi built in the 13th-14th centuries after the Catalan conquest. Beyond the chapel area, the cave extends into passages with underground lakes inhabited by two protected bat species sensitive to noise and light.
full_description:
CURRENTLY CLOSED TO ACCESS

The Cova de Sant Martí is linked to the earliest Christian worship in this part of the island, according to historians.

The natural cave communicates with the outside through a staircase carved into the rock. In the large chamber, which is accessed by descending the staircase from the outside, there are two chapels formed by a semicircular arch with Gothic-style cross-ribbed vaulting from the medieval period, representing respectively Sant Martí and Sant Jordi, and they were built after the Catalan conquest during the 13th and 14th centuries.

In the main chamber, 15 meters below the exterior and next to the access stairs to the cave, there is a small metal barrier approximately eighty centimeters high on the taller side, oriented at approximately 320°, which gives access to the most interesting part of the cave where two species of protected bats live that are sensitive to noise and light.

Once past the barrier and inside the main chamber, which is approximately 15 meters long in the shape of a cross, at the center of which there is a mound that we will have to pass on one of the sides. The cavity is very humid and slippery, where immediately upon entering one can see a lake to the right that surrounds the entire right side of the cavern and which at the far end extends approximately 10 meters further. The cave extends deepest on the left side, where we will access immediately upon entering the cave and, reaching the previously mentioned mound, passing it on the left side with care not to fall into the water unless we are wearing a neoprene suit. Immediately, once on the other side, one can see that the cave actually continues after overcoming a small slope. The remaining stretch to explore is not at all comfortable, as we will have to advance sometimes crouching and other times even dragging our body along the ground. On our left, in the direction we are advancing, one can quickly see the water of the lake, which is the continuation of the one we skirted before, and which we can descend into through a small pit and follow to the end of the cavity.

The underwater part of the cave has been explored, as in one of the lakes there is a guide rope that goes into its depths, although I do not know the result of the exploration.

On the way back to the chamber where the two chapels are located, one can also access another lake of approximately 15 meters in length, which we will access by descending through a sort of crawlway, thus concluding the visit.

name: Cova de ses Arrels
starting_point: Porto Cristo
access: Own vehicle or AUMASA bus company, Tel.: (+34) 971.55.07.30
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: Discovered during road construction near Porto Cristo in January 2011, this cave features beautiful formations, two small lakes, and abundant tree roots that inspired its name. The main chamber measures approximately 30x27 meters with impressive white columns, stalactites, and stalagmites. It is considered a site of biological, speleological, archaeological, and paleontological interest.
full_description:
NEWS ABOUT THE DISCOVERY

On January 22, 2011, the newspaper "ULTIMA HORA" published the news that during the Porto Cristo bypass road construction works, a cave near the "Coves dels Hams" had been brought to light. Given such news, and before anyone thought of committing some monstrosity with it — on Sunday, February 27, two media outlets once again reported on the appearance of the cave, mentioning the discovery inside the cavity of Myotragus bones and pre-Talayotic pottery.

CONSEQUENCE OF THE DISCOVERY

Members of the FBE, led by their president Guen Mulet, went to verify the finding to see what exactly it consisted of, and once they confirmed that it had a certain significance, they immediately communicated it to the Department of the Environment (Medi Ambient), which authorized the FBE itself to carry out the terrestrial and underwater topographic survey as well as a study of the cave, all accompanied by the archaeologist and geologist from the construction company.

FIRST CONTACT WITH THE CAVE

On January 29, unaware of what is described in the previous paragraph, V. Pardo and I decided to make a first visit to make contact with the finding, not knowing whether we would find ourselves alone in the cave or would coincide with other groups who had also taken an interest. Indeed, a cave of considerable size was found at the indicated location, so we set to work with its exploration as well as carrying out initial photographic work. A week later, specifically on February 12, this time accompanied by R. Minguillón, T. Mateu, and F. Gutiérrez, we went to the site with the idea of scrutinizing any nook that we might have missed the previous week and to begin the topographic work of the cavity. On February 26, the same members as the previous occasion set out to finish the exploration work as well as the photographic work, which we completed after a seven-hour working day.

DESCRIPTION

The entrance to the cavity is less than four meters wide by approximately one meter high, and once we enter it, the height decreases further before we can recover our upright position in the "Entrance Chamber" or "Root Chamber," measuring about 20 x 10 meters, from whose ceiling hangs a great quantity of beautiful roots. Someone, after seeing a photo of the chamber, commented to me how interesting it is to see how roots develop exploring the space in the absence of supports and obstacles. The chamber is heavily decorated with different types of formations that are partly hidden by the large quantity of roots mentioned above. At the northeastern end of the chamber, a small low gallery that does not exceed ten meters descends on a gentle slope.

Once the visit to the "Root Chamber" is finished, we can continue the visit descending the steep earth ramp, though free of danger, until reaching the small lake (photo 4), measuring 10 x 5 meters with a maximum depth of 1.5 meters and 27 meters from the entrance, which separates us from the continuation of the cave, although once crossed, the cavity only continues on a gentle ascent for another five meters. Only noteworthy are the efflorescences found during our visit. The surroundings of the lake are covered with various lithochemical formations, among which stands out a "vegetal stalagmite" — in reality it is a stalagmite that the roots embrace, covering it as they grow seeking the moisture they cannot find inside the earth; some more specimens can be found in the cave.

To continue the visit, we can begin the ascent up the ramp toward the exit while scrutinizing from side to side. A sort of crawlway at the southeastern end of the cave gives us false hopes of continuity since it is impossible to pass through its narrowness. About nine meters from the exit to the southeast, a low passage opens the way for us to continue the exploration. The first thing we encounter is a sort of mini chamber where continuity opens through a narrow crawlway of considerable slope that deposits us on the ground almost facing another crawlway that for now we will ignore since we will use it on the return to exit through it.

We are at the beginning of the cave's largest chamber, with dimensions of approximately 30 meters in length by almost 27 at its widest point. The direction of the chamber is southeast and it begins once we pass through a sort of doorway formed by both stalagmitic barriers. A gentle slope heading southwest covers almost the entire chamber, forming floor flowstones in some places, and the rest of the surface is covered with dusty earth and stones of different sizes. In another place in the chamber and near the beginning of the descent to the small lake, stone blocks accumulate next to each other, the result of a ceiling collapse. The northeastern side, which is closest to the outer surface, is practically covered with stalagmites and columns, making it a difficult place to traverse, although it does not prevent access through one end to the cave's other small entrance, inaccessible to humans but not to some rodent that for a time used it to bring into the interior an immense quantity of snails on which it fed. The entire chamber is beautifully decorated with gorgeous columns of intense white color; of the same color are the interesting stalactites that hang from the ceiling in whimsical shapes; stalagmites of all sizes also adorn the cave floor, among which stand out some small ones that are covered with roots and which I usually call, perhaps erroneously, "vegetal stalagmites."

A small jump takes us into a small gallery of approximately 3.5 meters wide with low ceilings that in at least one place do not reach 40 centimeters. Once past the first jump, it is easy to appreciate certain corners adorned with elegant speleothems. The end of the gallery is marked by a small lake that barely exceeds one meter in width by a little more than half a dozen in length. In both this small lake and the larger one, a fine layer of calcite covered the water's surface at the time of our visits.

On the return to the exterior, once the main chamber is left behind, instead of climbing through the steep ascending narrow passage, we can do so through the extremely narrow crawlway that will deposit us right above the impassable passage near the lake. This area, at least in winter, tends to be wet and slippery, so care must be taken not to slip. The rest is ascending toward the exit.

Given the proximity to the neighboring tourist cave of "Hams," it would not be surprising if they are part of the same system, and although they are not connected by land, neither are they through either of the two lakes. Only the small one harbored hopes since on the last visit of February 26, 2011, we found a guide line tied to a stalagmite left by cave divers. Finally, the FBE informed us that the connection with the "Hams" caves through the lake was not possible.

STATE OF CONSERVATION

The state of conservation of the cave is acceptable, although it certainly seems to make clear that the cavity had been visited prior to its exposure by the bypass road works. It is impossible that in such a short period of time since the publication of the news on January 22 in the newspaper "ULTIMA HORA" and our first visit on the 29th of the same month, it would show so many signs of having been visited.

FAUNA

In a quick glance through the cave, the fauna located inside consisted of different species of arachnids and orthopterans; in the latter case, these were some young crickets.

SPELEOGENESIS

This is not the field in which I can move best since I lack training in this area. I will only say that the "Cova de ses Arrels" appears to be in an advanced evolutionary state in which lithochemical processes are abundant throughout, mainly in the descent area and near the main lake, also near the small mouth of the cave. Ceiling collapses can be appreciated in the southeastern area of the cavity with a group of blocks of considerable size.

TO CONCLUDE

In summary, an interesting cavity of considerable dimensions, full of formations, with two small lakes, several narrow passages that will give us the opportunity to get dirty from top to bottom, and above all noteworthy is the great quantity of roots of all types and sizes that invade the entire cavern and which suggested to us the name for the cave.

The "Cova de ses Arrels" can be considered a site of biological, speleological, archaeological, and paleontological interest.

The day's outing can be completed by making a visit to the "Cova de sa Figuera" which is located nearby, although you will need to drive to get there.

name: Cova de ses Meravelles
starting_point: Coma-sema estate (Orient)
clothing: Coveralls recommended if accessing all corners
distance: 1 km from parking next to Coma-sema house
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 481363 E, 4400340 N. DATUM ETRS89: 481260 E, 4400140 N
equipment: Only helmet and lighting
description: A 500 m long cave once developed for tourist visits, featuring remnants of stone stairways enabling an easy circular tour. It contains numerous chambers including the Sala des Blocs (an ancient necropolis), elaborate speleothems, and the spectacular Enchanted Castle area with pure white active stalagmites. The deepest point is -35 m in the Clay Chamber, and the cave is on private property requiring landowner permission to visit.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

We find ourselves before a cavity with a development of about 500 m, which was once fitted out for visits, so although those works are no longer in the same state of preservation as then, they do allow for an easy visit with minimal precaution (the cave is very slippery after a rainy day). Once the Boca des Forn (Oven's Mouth) is crossed, the design of the conditioning works allows for a circular visit of the cave.

To access the interior of the cavity, one must descend via some old stone stairs until reaching a gate which, once crossed and after descending eleven more steps, places us on a kind of landing from which we can access the Sala des Mort (Hall of the Dead) to our right.

Sala des Mort (Hall of the Dead)

Small hall of 13 x 13 x 8 m located in a bend of the entrance stairway. It is accessed through a crawlway of slightly less than 0.40 m in height that had to be cleared at some point. Once inside, we find a horizontal hall somewhat decorated with speleothems though with little activity.

Noteworthy in this hall is the existence of two "chimneys" (vertical pits) on the northern side, reasonably decorated with concretions. Their verticality and slippery surface make their exploration impossible.

This hall, like the Sala des Blocs (Hall of Blocks), served as a necropolis for the ancient inhabitants of the mountain range.

Sala des Blocs (Hall of Blocks)

This is a structural hall, approximately 26 x 32 x 7 m, in which significant collapses have occurred, so its main characteristic is the presence of a chaos of blocks, more or less planar and of large dimensions.

As mentioned previously, the ceiling features the presence of rocky strata of great continuity, with tepee morphologies.

The ornamentation of this cave is restricted to the southern zone, where there is a ramped area with abundant columns of small dimensions, through which one can access the upper part of the Sala de s'Argila (Clay Hall); and to the western side of the hall, where large wall flowstones have developed.

A large part of this hall was used as a burial ground as attested by a great quantity of ceramic, bone, and charcoal remains that cover the floor between the blocks. Currently these remains appear disturbed and mixed. The existence of a sieve and a trowel as well as a dustpan hidden in a crack on the eastern side of the hall, behind some stone blocks, seem to confirm archaeological work of which we have no information.

In the same area, east of the hall, one can appreciate a beautiful column that formed due to water seepage through what was once a "chimney."

Saleta des Mal Pas (Little Hall of the Bad Step)

Its access is found on the north wall of the Sala des Blocs. A window above a flowstone places us above a crawlway of 5 m in length. At its end is this hall of approximately 2 x 5 m, occupied in its central area by an old flowstone.

Sala de Sa Rampa (Hall of the Ramp)

The connection between the Sala de Sa Rampa and the Sala des Blocs is made through a small gallery in the southern zone, which has been artificially enlarged (the use of drill holes can be observed), called Boca des Forn.

This is a hall of 16 x 15 x 4 m which, as its name indicates, is characterized by a more or less uniform ramp of approximately 30° slope, along which the path runs at the highest part. It is bounded at its upper part and on the northern side by flowstones called "Els Orgues" (The Organs), while on its southern side there is a curtain of columns separating it from the rest of the cave. A stairway carved into the ramp descends eastward to the deepest zones of the cavity (Sala de s'Argila, Sala de Sa Troneta, and Sala des Ribell).

As previously noted, the undulating ceiling with striations corresponds to a spectacular fault mirror, draped with numerous isolated stalactites that have been decapitated.

The thickness of the flowstone covering the ramp is not very great and in some places the red clays that constitute the substrate are exposed.

Sala EST

Located above the Sala de Sa Rampa, its dimensions are 14 x 22 x 3 m.

This is a stepped ramp hall, bounded to the north by wall flowstones and separated from the Sala de Sa Rampa by a barrier of columns and flowstones. It is on one of these northern flowstones where a graffiti appears indicating the group that first surveyed the cavity and the year it was done: EST 69, and one of the eastern flowstones with a bell-shaped morphology and blackish color is called "Sa Carretilla" (The Wheelbarrow).

Inside, notable features include the presence of gours and the existence of a small, heavily concretioned hall at the southwest end. It is in this hall where one can observe a stalagmitic formation known as "Sa Ma des Gegant" (The Giant's Hand) and the presence of a small chimney.

Additionally, the abundant fault mirrors that can be observed on the ceiling of the lower part of the hall are worthy of mention.

Central zone of the cavity

From the EST Hall or ascending from the Sala des Ribell, we will arrive at the central part of the Cova de Ses Meravelles. This is a flat area where only the presence of two large stalagmites stands out. If we come from the EST Hall, we will have climbed a small ramp on whose right side we can observe the column known as "Es Pop" (The Octopus), which presents a whitish color that stands out against the rest.

From this area, ascending a small ramp to the northwest we will reach the Racó d'en Miquel; if we ascend another steeper ramp to the southwest, we will reach Sa Ximeneia (The Chimney); or if we prefer, climbing some very steep stairs we will reach Es Castell Encantat (The Enchanted Castle).

Racó d'en Miquel (Miguel's Corner)

To access its interior, one must cross a very narrow gallery of 5 m in length, once the top of the small ramp to the northwest of the central zone of the cavity has been reached. At that point, the gallery entrance, there are some deep gours.

This is a small room of only 2 x 2 m corresponding to an ancient gour, where a great quantity of phreatic speleothems of great purity stand out. The name comes from the graffiti still preserved in this place.

Sa Xemeneia (The Chimney)

This is a spectacular pit of large dimensions, with no apparent connection to the outside, although it must be very close to the surface.

The diameter of the chimney's mouth is about 4.0 m, narrowing as it ascends. Its interior is covered with flowstones that hang from its mouth and indicate that there has been a significant water input through it. For all of this, it can be stated that it has almost certainly played a very important role in the development of the cave.

Nearby, a "disc" is located on the ceiling — a secondary speleothem that forms when water exits the rock under some pressure while simultaneously precipitating calcite particles on each side.

Saleta des Fang (Little Hall of Mud)

Just half a dozen meters from the chimney, toward the north, there is a small room accessed through a narrow passage carved in the rock that overcomes a subvertical flowstone.

This flat-ceilinged room is hidden just below a large flowstone. The room can only be accessed by crawling and at no point can one stand upright.

This is a room of 5 x 12 x 1.5 m, completely flat, decorated by several columns and wall flowstones, and whose floor is completely covered in mud, from which its name derives.

Sala des Gours (Hall of Gours)

Ascending from the Sa Ximeneia area toward the west, we will reach this hall, located in the highest part of the cavity. The dimensions of this hall are 21 x 27 x 6 m. The hall can be divided into two zones: the southwestern half corresponds to a ramp of gentle slope that ends in a fairly open and flat area carpeted with mini-gours; the northeastern half corresponds to a large-scale flowstone crowned by abundant gours of small dimensions but deep, and bounded at its northeastern end by columns.

The cavity ends at its western end where a vertical flowstone exists.

Sala de Ses Arrels (Hall of Roots)

In the northwest area of the Sala des Gours, behind the final flowstone, is this small hall of 4 x 9 x 2.5 m, accessed through a low-ceilinged entrance. The presence of roots, from which it takes its name, is notable.

To the right of its entrance stands out a large curtain whose interior is draped with numerous eccentric stalactites — perhaps the most beautiful part of the entire cave.

Es Castell Encantat (The Enchanted Castle)

Descending from the Sala des Gours, if we stay close to the southwest wall, we can reach the Es Pulpit (The Pulpit) area — a small "balcony" formed by stalagmites.

Stairs will allow us to descend along the southwest side and we will arrive at the upper area of the Castell Encantat, where the first thing that catches our attention will be the existence of three active stalagmites of pure white, as if they were ghosts standing out against the black of the rest of the cavity.

Descending a bit more we will arrive at a flat area where we can either continue descending to the southeast toward the Sala de l'Infern (Hall of Hell), or descend via vertical stairs to the central zone of the cave from where we can begin our exit from the cavity.

This area stands out for the abundance of columnar formations and flowstones, being the most concretioned zone of the entire cavity. Being located in a high area of the cavity, it appears as a fortress, a castle situated on top of a promontory — hence its name.

Sala de l'Infern (Hall of Hell)

This hall can only be reached from the Es Castell Encantat area, via descending vertical stairs, so once visited we must retrace our steps.

This is a hall of 11 x 22 m located in the southern zone of the cave, above the Sala des Ribell (Basin Hall). The latter is observable from a kind of balcony in the lower part of the hall. Like Es Castell Encantat, it stands out for the great abundance of column and flowstone type speleothems.

Sala des Ribell (Basin Hall)

From the central zone of the cavity, descending eastward along a ramp of undulating morphology, steeper than the one in the Sala de Sa Rampa, we will arrive at this hall.

The approximate dimensions of this hall are 20 x 22 x 6 m. A ribell (ceramic basin) is placed in a small depression in the center of the hall. Said basin is always filled with crystalline water from which cave visitors drink, as evidenced by the existence of some cups hidden in a nearby crack. In its vicinity, a beautiful nest of pisoliths can be observed.

Additionally, notable features include the existence of several isolated stalagmites of approximately 1.0 m in height around the basin and a small sinkhole filled with clays at the southwest end.

Sala de Sa Troneta (Sinkhole Hall)

Crossing the curtain of columns heading north, we will arrive at the lower part of the Sala de Sa Rampa and descending some steps we will reach the hall.

This hall functions as the sinkhole of the cave, collecting all the water that enters it. The dimensions are 6 x 12 x 2.5 m. The southern area houses a great quantity of small, clean formations that the mud flooding the hall has not managed to reach.

Saleta Amagada (Hidden Little Hall)

This small hall of approximately 2.5 x 5 x 2 m is found at the back of the Sala de Sa Troneta. Prior to the clearing work performed, it was hidden behind the mud. This is a beautiful hall that houses beautiful formations in an extraordinary state of preservation. One should not enter its interior so as not to dirty it, having to be content with contemplating it from the cleared "window."

Sala de S'Argila (Clay Hall)

It is found after the Sala de Sa Troneta; before reaching it, we will leave to our right a small 2.5 m deep well that possibly also functions as a sinkhole (troneta) and marks the deepest point of the cavity at -35 m.

The dimensions of the hall are 10 x 21 x 3 m, the surface being completely flat since in this part of the cave significant thicknesses of red decalcification clays have accumulated. The only notable features are the existence of a large gour with a flat bottom in the eastern part of the hall and the tepee morphology of the ceiling.

Once the visit is complete, one need only climb the stairs located on the north side of the Sala de Sa Rampa ramp and cross the Boca des Forn to our right.

Note: The Cova de ses Meravelles is on private property, so it is necessary to contact the landowner in order to visit it.

name: Cova de ses Mosques Grosses
starting_point: Na Burguesa restaurant (Génova)
equipment: 20 m rope for safety
description: A structurally-originated cave on the SE slope of Puig Gros de Bendinat with a small entrance and low ceiling requiring crawling. It features two main chambers: an entry hall with abundant lithochemical formations and corrosion cupolas, and a larger hall with a beautiful flowstone descending to -17.5 m depth. The cave continues beyond the current obstruction but is not yet passable.
full_description:
Description.

On the SE slope of Puig Gros de Bendinat (486 m), just below the path of the same name and at the base of a small escarpment, lies this cavity of structural origin, with a small entrance and low ceiling.

Entrance Hall

From the very first moment we enter the cavity, we must crawl along a ramp with a gradient close to 25% in the W-E direction. With dimensions of 6 x 6 x 1.5 m, lithochemical processes abound in the hall, while in the ceiling zone a couple of corrosion cupolas can be appreciated, perhaps the result of thermal aggressions within the cavity; one of the cupolas is filled with delicate speleothems.

The ceiling of the hall at some points does not reach half a meter in height. Seven meters further down, we find ourselves at what appears to be the end of the cavity, but it is nothing other than an obstruction due to the wedging of large stone blocks, where clearing work had to be carried out to continue the exploration.

Flowstone Hall

To enter this hall, one must descend from the clearing zone through unstable materials, making this a somewhat dangerous area due to rockfall from stones that have accumulated, dragged in from outside the cave. It is recommended to install a rope on the exterior of the cavity to help overcome this passage. A kind of channel on the west indicates the descent, while the cave opens up to the east, and a beautiful flowstone descends from the south to reach -17.5 m, this being the deepest zone of the hall. In reality it is a new obstruction that prevents further progress, although we have been able to verify that the cave continues descending for quite a few more meters. The easternmost surface of the hall is carpeted with calcium carbonate from the aforementioned flowstone; in this area a considerable number of speleothems abound.

Visiting this cavity does not require a full day, so it can be complemented by visiting some of the caves found in the vicinity.

name: Cova de Son Vila
starting_point: Leave the Ma-2200 road (connecting the Palma-Sa Pobla motorway with Pollensa) at Km 44.7
equipment: Helmet and lighting only, unless descending into the cave through the shaft, which requires approximately 20 m of rope
description: A cave in Pollensa visited since prehistoric times for water collection, as evidenced by pottery found inside. Features a shield-shaped entrance and a main chamber reaching 8 m depth, with well-decorated formations on the left side. Natural light entering from multiple openings creates a special atmosphere. Classified as a site of historic-archaeological and socio-economic interest.
full_description:
Like many other caves on the island of Mallorca, the Cova de Son Vila was visited since prehistoric times by people to collect water from its interior, as evidenced by the pottery found inside the cave. Today the interior of the cave remains dry, which is likely because the stream of water that used to descend the hillside and enter the cave through various openings has changed its course.

A path that starts from one of the streets of the housing development allows us to access the cave, ending right at its entrance. This path personally confused me for a long time, as I initially thought the construction of the path was due to an attempt at mining exploitation of the cave, but once I scrutinized its interior, everything indicates that no work has ever been carried out inside the cave, so perhaps it was intended to add an extra attraction to the housing development.

The cave entrance is oriented 245° N in the shape of a shield, being approximately one meter wide while the height does not exceed one meter and twenty centimeters. About seven meters above the main entrance, there is another smaller opening in the form of a shaft measuring approximately thirty by seventy centimeters, which seven or eight meters below connects with the cave in a small room.

Access to the cave is made by descending carefully to a sort of artificially constructed stone landing (photo 1), which is approximately one meter fifty below, where on the left side a fall would plunge us to the bottom of the great chamber that comprises this cave, which is eight meters deep.

Just behind a flowstone formation is the access path through a narrow corridor, on whose left side, in the direction of our travel, interesting concretions can be observed.

A small room opens to our right before reaching the bottom of the main chamber, which we will access through a small ramp. Once inside and if we look up at the ceiling, we can see how outside light filters through the shaft entrance, dimly illuminating the place.

Returning from the visit to the small room, we will descend to the bottom of the cavity, from where practically the entire chamber can be observed. Noteworthy is the natural light entering from outside through both the main entrance and some other small openings, giving the place a certain quality that will make us feel we are in a special place. As we advance through the chamber, we will immediately notice that the entire left side is quite well decorated with formations, which is not the case with the right side.

Another small recess, accessed by crossing a narrow crawlway, is found at the end of the cave and serves as a dormitory for some bat specimens.

The Cova de Son Vila is classified as a site of historic-archaeological interest, as well as socio-economic interest. J. A. Encinas S. Relación inventarial de subterráneos naturales de las Illes Baleares. 2006. Ediciones JAES.

name: Cova de l'Afortunat
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: Located near Cova des Ponent and connected via an impassable narrow conduit through which human bone remains were deposited in an undetermined era, adding archaeological interest. The lower level features collapse morphology with blocks and little decoration, while the upper level hosts abundant and varied speleothems with evidence of former standing water.
full_description:
It is located just a few meters from the entrance of the Cova des Ponent, with which it maintains a connection through a narrow impassable conduit. This opening was used in an undetermined era to dispose of human bone remains, a circumstance that adds evident archaeological interest to the cavity.

From a morphological point of view, the lower level presents a typology clearly associated with collapse processes, with a predominance of blocks and scarce presence of concretions. In contrast, the upper floor harbors a notable development of speleothems, with abundant and varied formations.

Access to the cave.

Upper level where abundant and varied speleothems are found.

The upper level shows signs of having held water in some small pool.

name: Cova del Boc
starting_point: Kilometer 4.2 of the Colonya Valley road
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Helmet and lighting only.
description: A beautifully decorated baroque cave in Pollensa with a very small entrance barely one meter high. Once inside, the cave opens up to reveal abundant and varied speleothems including numerous curtains and handsome columns. The cave descends continuously, starting with low-ceiling passages requiring crawling before opening to allow upright walking.
full_description:
The entrance to the Cova del Boc is very small, not exceeding one meter in height and approximately one meter seventy in width, so it is difficult to see until we are right at the entrance. However, just a few meters past the entrance, a baroque cave loaded with decorations immediately opens before us, dazzling us with its beauty.

From the very entrance, the cave begins to delve into the depths of the earth in continuous descent, requiring us to move on all fours or in any other manner, since in this first part the cave has very little headroom. However, as our descent continues, the cave becomes larger so that we can walk upright while all manner of formations, each more beautiful than the last, present themselves before our eyes on all sides.

It is not that the cave at this point is a chamber where everything is visible before our eyes, since especially on its left side, winding passages appear that we can explore while also navigating the various obstacles that present themselves. Although the cave floor in some areas is not too wet, one must nevertheless be careful of slipping.

As I said at the beginning, the Cova del Boc is heavily loaded with decorations, having a great variety among which numerous curtains stand out, in addition to some beautiful columns.

It is impossible for me to explain the entirety of the cave since due to lack of time I could not reach its end, having to turn back at a point where the cave once again began to reduce in size, and after crawling for a stretch through it, I finally turned around, postponing a new visit.

name: Cova del Dimoni
starting_point: Porto Cristo, Sa Punta Rassa street
access: Any bus line going to Porto Cristo
equipment: Helmet and lighting, neoprene wetsuit for navigating the main hall, and a small rope in case the ones usually installed have been removed.
description: An EU-protected cave (LIC) beneath the cliffs near Porto Cristo that is both a terrestrial and marine cave, popular with divers. The main hall measures 41x21 m with 8+ m ceiling height and is mostly flooded by a brackish lake fed through an underwater siphon. A magnificent large column dominates the hall, slightly separated from the ceiling possibly due to horizontal displacement. The underwater entrance at -8 m depth connects to a chamber with terraces and sub-chambers. Dangerous to enter when seas are rough.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova del Dimoni is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI. At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Near the town of Porto Cristo beneath the cliffs lies this interesting cavity that has the peculiarity of being both a terrestrial and a marine cave. Famous among diving enthusiasts, the Cova del Dimoni is regularly visited by groups belonging to companies on the island that are dedicated to this activity.

The terrestrial entrance is easy to find as it is located almost at sea level in the cliff itself, at one end of a mini bay that is possibly an ancient cave that collapsed upon itself. The altitude of the entrance is approximately two meters by about three meters wide, with an orientation of 110°.

Once we enter the cavity, we immediately see that the cave continues through a narrow hole that we climb through to access a small vestibule about six meters long, slightly less wide, and never exceeding two meters in height. The concretions in this place give us an idea of what we are going to find in the cave, as some beautiful columns adorn the left side of the chamber, and the ceiling is covered with small stalactites, with some parietal flowstones also present. This is the area most affected by the soot produced by the massive use of candles that some thoughtless individuals use without any control when visiting the cave. I know that what I write will serve no purpose, but I hope it can prevent some people from bringing candles into the place, and if they do use them, to use the type that come encapsulated in small aluminum containers which, if removed after use, will cause insignificant damage — although the best option is the use of electric light.

To continue the visit, we must go along the left side of a small column from which we will descend approximately one meter to position ourselves on a sort of balcony from which we will continue the descent through a ramp, aided by the ropes that are usually installed. The floor is quite slippery, so one simply needs to be aware of it; otherwise there is nothing to fear.

Once the descent is finished, we will find ourselves at one end of a large chamber measuring approximately forty-one meters long by twenty-one meters wide and just over eight meters high from the water surface, flooded for the most part by a lake of brackish water since the sea enters through a siphon. The depth of the lake ranges from a few centimeters to six meters in the siphon area, although in most of the lake the depth is around two meters. The terrestrial visit of the chamber can be done using hand-line ropes installed in the cave, so we only need to make sure that said ropes are in good condition. Once past this zone, rich in parietal flowstones, we arrive at a place where, although the floor is quite irregular, one can walk without great difficulty — though watching where we step. What stands out most in this place is the great "column" that presides over the entire chamber. I have put it in quotes because it is currently separated from the ceiling by less than a meter, perhaps due to a solifluction process with horizontal displacement. Not far from the column, we will also find two small gours and some other mini pools, as the terrain descends until it meets the water level of the lake. If we want to visit the other terrestrial zones of the chamber, we will have to cross the lake, which I recommend because at the back of the chamber there is a small corner worth visiting, and another at the far left end of the chamber as we advance, hidden behind a wall that is nothing other than the ceiling level partially reaching the water level.

Basically, the cave consists of the entrance vestibule and the main chamber flooded by the lake for the most part, all adorned by a great column that presides over the main chamber, enhancing its beauty. The concretions of the Cova del Dimoni are reasonable, with pavement flowstones as well as parietal and ceiling flowstones, plus multiple columns, some of great beauty — the aforementioned one standing out above the rest — and some small gours in a couple of places in the cave.

As I noted at the beginning, the Cova del Dimoni has two entrances, one terrestrial and one marine. The marine entrance is at about eight meters depth, and communication between the two parts is through the siphon at the bottom of the lake in the great chamber, through which a beautiful turquoise-colored light penetrates.

From the website www.albatros-diving.com we have gathered the following information about the underwater part of this cave: it states that the underwater entrance at -8 meters depth is of sufficient width for several divers to enter simultaneously; we access a chamber of about 20 meters wide by 20 meters long, dotted with terraces at different levels and lateral sub-chambers, one of which hides the chimney that connects it to the lake of the terrestrial cave.

It is dangerous to enter the interior of this cave when the sea is rough, as the strong waves can make exiting difficult. Additionally, although we have not verified this, it is possible that the force of the waves pushes water through the siphon with great violence, which could make staying inside significantly dangerous.

name: Cova dets Ases
starting_point: Upon entering Porto Colom from the Felanitx road, find the roundabout with a ship's anchor. Follow Calle s'Algar towards the lighthouse until the road turns sharply right and the sea appears, with a small green area for parking.
best_season: Any season, though summer is recommended for swimming at s'Algar
access: Bus service to Porto Colom depending on origin
equipment: Neoprene required for swimming in the lake
description: A 497m-long cave connecting to the sea through two siphons. Features a large entrance chamber, a decorated room with columns and flowstones containing animal bones, a crystal-clear lake, and narrow passages leading to a labyrinth area. New galleries were discovered in 2014 and are now protected by a locked gate.
full_description:
Mallorca, despite its small size, possesses important and varied caves of high interest for underwater explorations. The Cova des Ases connects to the sea after passing through two siphons; the first, eleven meters long, connects to a 117 m chamber with a lake at its SE end. The lake, after 20 m of flooded gallery, connects to the open sea. The total length of the cavity is 497 m. In a straight line toward the sea from the entrance, the length is approximately 326 m (measured with SigPac).

The entrance to the cave is hidden among bushes. The visit begins once through the entrance, descending a considerable slope over ground loaded with rocks of different sizes, which however poses no danger as it is completely dry.

The dimensions of this first chamber are considerable but devoid of formations. If we look carefully, just before reaching the lowest part, a door opens to the left, and once we peer through it, a sort of corridor can be seen a few meters below that appears to continue, but which for now has remained pending exploration.

The large entrance chamber connects to a very small one where the cave seems to end, but if we look to the left side, some formations can be seen a few meters up, and possibly some paint marks in the shape of arrows indicating the continuation of this cave. We therefore have to climb up a ramp that is a bit slippery at first but is not at all dangerous. We continue climbing with a tendency to the left, and shortly after, we notice a strong light entering from outside, which might make us think we are exiting the cave, but this is not the case — it is a circular opening of more than one meter in diameter in the cave ceiling where a small fig tree can be seen. The coordinates of this opening are 523570 E, 4365007 N UTM DATUM EUROPEAN 1950, located 55 meters from the main entrance in the direction of the sea, slightly displaced to the right. It is possible to exit through this opening, though not without some risk.

Below us, six or seven meters down, a decorated chamber with some columns of considerable beauty can be seen. Once inside it, we can take some time to photograph it. In this chamber, we can appreciate that on the right side, straight ahead, and on the left, it is decorated with beautiful flowstones as well as some interesting columns. It is also possible to find a large quantity of bones in the area, undoubtedly due to animals that have accidentally fallen through the aforementioned opening and, being unable to leave the cave, have died inside it, or some of them simply died from the fall. I don't know what this chamber is called, if it has a name, but if it does, I wouldn't be surprised if it were the "Chamber of Bones."

To continue the visit, we must descend a steep ramp — not dangerous — seeking the deepest part until reaching a sort of arch that allows us to continue descending until we arrive at a chamber of approximately 20 meters in length, where there is a narrow lake of about 15 meters in length with crystal-clear waters along the left edge of the cave wall, which can be descended to without great difficulty though with some caution, as the terrain is slippery.

Once we return from the lake, the cave continues straight ahead in the direction we were going, beginning a gentle slope to finally have to use our hands to climb until we reach firm ground and continue advancing through narrow crawlways until reaching a sort of labyrinth where ropes (guides) appear that have been abandoned, possibly by people who previously explored the cave for its cartography.

In 2014, new galleries were found, to which the first and second photos of this work belong. We have also incorporated the topography surveyed by J. A. Encinas. To access this area, the corresponding permission must be requested, as a gate with its corresponding padlock protects it from unauthorized visits.

name: Cova dels Ossos / s'Ullerol dets Ossos
starting_point: Road Ma-4042 km 6.350, junction with the Capdepera road and Camí de s'Heretat. Enter the forest toward the telephone line post; the cavity is to the left of it.
equipment: Only helmet and lighting
description: A small but charming previously uncatalogued cave discovered by Gaspar Herráez. Despite its compact size, it features soda straws, small flags, cow-udder formations, and flowstones. Notable for its fauna, including possibly Europe's smallest bat (Pipistrellus pipistrellus) and abundant animal bones that gave the cave its name. Considered of historical-archaeological and speleological interest.
full_description:
Despite its small dimensions, the Cova dels Ossos (Bones), so 'baptized' by me, is a charming cave that was uncatalogued until a week ago when Gaspar Herráez found it by chance, as often happens. Once he communicated the discovery to me, we made the first superficial exploration on November 17, 2007, leaving a more in-depth visit pending.

The entrance through which we will access its interior is of small dimensions, triangular in shape, approximately one meter high by about 65 or 70 centimeters at the widest part, and is oriented at 330° N.

Right after crossing the entrance, we will begin to descend down a small ramp of approximately 5 meters in length by a couple of meters at the widest side, but where we will move almost crawling along the ground due to the low ceiling. Once in the chamber where the small entrance gallery leads, we will immediately notice that it is of small dimensions — no longer than the entrance gallery, by about 4.5 m wide and about 3 m high, but only in one spot, since in the rest of the chamber we will always move crouching, unable to stand upright except at the indicated point. A tight crawlway opens before us to our right in the direction of the cave entrance, steeply sloped, with a length of approximately another 5 m, which in turn leads to another small chamber of a length equal to that of the crawlway and a maximum height of 1.90 m, ending the cave at this point.

Returning to the chamber to continue our visit, two possible options open before us: the first is through a crawlway on the right side, not suitable for certain body sizes, and the second is a kind of crack on the left side that, in a more or less comfortable way, will place us in another chamber of about 10 meters in length that also allows us to stand up while contemplating the formations found at the beginning of it — soda straws, small flags, cow-udder formations, as well as some flowstones can be seen in the place, all of small dimensions in keeping with the cavity we are in. At the back of the chamber, two new crawlways appear before us, one on each side, that descend for a few meters into the depths of the earth while blocking our passage, bringing the visit to a close.

I must highlight the fauna found in this small cavity which, despite its dimensions, is well worth noting. On my first visit, I was able to find a specimen of bat of very small dimensions never before seen by me, and once 'identified,' everything seems to indicate it was a Pipistrellus pipistrellus, which is the smallest bat that can be found in Europe. To give an idea of its dimensions, the forearm measures between 28 and 33.5 mm, where the hairs are blackish at the base and chestnut-colored at the tips, with short and small ears. The diet of this species consists mainly of flying insects and mosquitoes. I should also note an abundant number of black, shiny spiders — both the cephalothorax and the limbs — with the rest of the body being a dull brownish color. I also found a specimen of Scutigera coleoptrata, which is nothing more than a species of centipede but with somewhat fewer legs.

We did not have much trouble naming the cave, as there is a large quantity of bones from different animals in its interior, mainly in the entrance chamber as well as in the first described crawlway, which simplified the task.

The Cova dels Ossos is considered of historical-archaeological as well as speleological interest.

To learn more:

Jordi SERRA-COBO, Blanca AMENGUAL, Marc LÓPEZ-ROIG, Josep MÁRQUEZ, Xavier BAYER, Cisco GUASCH, Antonia SÁNCHEZ, Joan Antoni OLIVER. FIFTEEN YEARS OF CHIROPTEROLOGICAL STUDIES IN THE BALEARIC ISLANDS (1993-2007). Endins 31, 125-140. Palma de Mallorca.

name: Cova d'en Bessó
starting_point: Calle de Leonardo Da Vinci at the level of Passeig Diego Velazquez
clothing: Wetsuit recommended if planning to cross the lake
equipment: For land exploration, only a wetsuit is needed depending on the time of year of the visit
description: A cave classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC), requiring authorization to visit. It opens via a large collapse pit about 25 meters in diameter, descending to a lake at -10m depth. The cave features extensive galleries with fossils, speleothems, and archaeological remains including Bronze Age pottery and human bones, as well as a large bat colony.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova d'en Bessó is classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as LIC. At present (see the date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Conselleria de Medio Ambiente (Department of the Environment). Therefore, before making any visit, the mandatory permit must be requested to be able to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE NEW GALLERIES - 2011

GALLERY OF THE FIG TREE PERFUME

A small hole, cleared by hand next to the wall of the doline, gives access to a crawlway with an inclination close to 45 degrees. The entrance, one meter by about fifty centimeters wide, means that access to the interior of the cavity through the narrow crawlway does not pose any danger along the two and a half meters it spans. The floor is the bedrock that closes the cavity; a small drop of a little more than one meter will place us on a narrow ledge, provided the descent is made on the right side, since if we deviate a little to the left there is a risk of falling to the bottom, three meters below. For this reason, it is recommended to install a rope to help, at least for the first person making the descent. The mentioned ledge seems to continue, but only extends a few meters. The descent of the next three meters presents no difficulty, provided care is taken in what one is doing. For the description of this gallery, the following four areas will be distinguished:

Vestibule or Fossil Chamber. The dimensions of this small chamber are 12 x 4 m and it is formed by the collapse of large blocks wedged on top of each other, with the white color of the material they are made of contrasting with the intense red of the earth. The floor is likewise covered with blocks and earth that make it walkable.

At the eastern end of the small chamber there is a dry stone wall, which indicates that we are not the first people to visit this place and that, possibly intentionally, the access was sealed. Fragments of different types of pottery scattered on the ground led us to report to the relevant authorities through the FBE so that they could be studied.

On different walls of the chamber, some fossils are visible. The shells of some unidentified bivalves, echinoderms, and some specimens of oysters can be observed. In other parts of the cavity, outside this gallery, we have also been able to observe various fossils, such as pectinids and a considerable number of Clypeaster, a type of echinoderm that appeared about 40 million years ago, although it had its greatest expansion in the Miocene, 10 million years ago. At present, some species still live in warm seas.

ROOT RAMP

Once we leave the Fossil Chamber, we begin a gentle descent, first across a floor carpeted with stones. Once again we find a small wall on the far left that looks like a small retaining wall; a little further down, on the ground, there are small roots covered in calcite and also some speleothems in the shape of a fried egg, the beginning of a stalagmite's birth. The drips in this place are intense, especially in winter, and we find nothing similar in the rest of the gallery.

SEDIMENT CHAMBER

It is located in the deepest zone of the gallery at -12 m, with dimensions of 20 x 6 m. It is completely flat and mostly covered by the sediments that the water drags into it, forming retraction polygons (Fig. 2) made up of narrow and shallow cracks; at the eastern end, a very narrow and inaccessible place appears to function as a sinkhole. In this chamber is found the greatest concentration of formations, with two large stalagmitic massifs standing out in the middle of the chamber, separated from each other by a few centimeters, but which do not prevent crossing to the other side, with the right side being more comfortable. From the ceiling hang beautiful and very fine soda straw stalactites (macaroni); on the western wall, if we look carefully we will discover beautiful speleothems, among which are some helictites.

FINAL CHAMBER

The Final Chamber (Fig. 3) is the extension of the Sediment Chamber. To access it we must climb over a height of a little more than a meter and a half; only in one corner can we recover our upright position. The floor is full of blocks fallen from the ceiling, formations are few and the continuity of the cave becomes impossible. Noteworthy are the two engravings found on the wall, made with a pointed object at an unknown time and which appear represented in the topographic survey. One of the engravings has the shape of a rocket and the other, of similar size, is difficult to interpret.

DESCRIPTION OF THE REST OF THE CAVE - 2007

Once again fig trees are found at the entrance of a cave, something that has been happening to me during my latest visits. The Cova d'en Bessó is located just a few meters from the street, however it is difficult to see as it is surrounded by very dense vegetation and cannot be seen until one is right on top of it.

The Cova d'en Bessó opens to the exterior through a pit resulting from a collapse, about twenty-five meters in diameter and at some point on its walls about seven meters deep; however, one can easily access its interior by walking on just one side of it, which is the one next to Leonardo Da Vinci street.

Once inside the "pit," we will cross it on the left side and walk from left to right parallel to the wall until we reach the actual entrance of the cave, which is of considerable dimensions. As soon as we enter the interior, a relatively comfortable descent begins through a large chamber of considerable dimensions heading toward a lake that is located in the lower part of the cavity, at a distance from the entrance of about forty-five meters with a width of approximately another forty meters; the depth is about minus ten meters down to the water level of the lake.

Once at the lake, the path is cut off by it, so without a wetsuit we cannot advance and thus our visit to the cave would end. But if we have one, we can continue the visit advancing through a gallery either swimming or walking along a row of considerably sized stones that allows us to reach the other end of the water, never exceeding one meter and forty centimeters in depth — though if we leave this path, the lake has greater depth.

As soon as we find ourselves on dry land, we can observe how on the right side there are some concretions, with a beautiful flowstone with multiple small stalactites adorning the ceiling standing out, as well as the occasional stalagmite. Also from there one can swim out to the exterior since the lake is the same one through which we entered.

To continue the visit we can proceed along the right side in our direction of travel through a sort of crawlway — well, it is an opening much larger than a crawlway — and once on the other side we can begin exploring all the nooks and crannies, among which we will find a small lake where a guide rope can be seen entering the depths of the water, which was surely left by F. Gracias who was mapping the cavity in 2002 aided by B. Clamor, M.A. Dot, and P. Watkinson.

To continue the visit, we will advance while always seeking the most comfortable areas to do so, since the ceiling is very low relative to the floor in this first part of this second chamber while continuing to ascend, such that it seems at some point we are going to reach the surface at any moment. In the highest part of the cave we can already stand up and therefore move around without any problem while enjoying the concretions of the place, which are more abundant in this part of the cave. It is possible that before reaching the highest part of the cave we may have encountered some other guide line that can be followed or not, since orientation presents no great difficulties.

During our visit it is possible to observe that there are large amounts of guano since even today this enclosure is frequented by bats of large dimensions compared to those found by me in other caves on the island, and we should try not to disturb them too much.

On the shore of the first lake found and on its interior part, there are remains of pottery, mainly from vessels, some of them corresponding to the Bronze Age and of large dimensions, and others from more modern periods, as well as some human bone, which demonstrates that this cave has been visited for a long time — if not as a permanent residence, at least temporarily.

name: Cova d'en Domingo
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint in Génova. Follow the path towards Coll des Pastors. At the pass, take the right path for about 77 m until it widens at a cistern, then take the first right path (careful — vegetation increasingly hides it).
description: A natural cave in the Coma Fosca ravine historically modified for human use as a shelter. It consists of a single chamber with a notably flat floor, decorated with stalactites, flowstones, and a beautiful group of columns reminiscent of the Coves Petites de Capdepera. The cave functions as a sinkhole during heavy rains, and is considered a Natural Site of Interest.
full_description:
The cave in question is located inside the Sa Coma Fosca torrent, to the left of the path. Before reaching it, if approaching from the Coll des Pastor, we will leave to our right the avenc of the same name, which is protected with a metal mesh to prevent hunting dogs from falling into it.

The path that runs through the interior of Sa Coma Fosca toward the Barranco des Sec has been abandoned by the landowners along with other paths on the same estate, in order to prevent the constant passage of cyclists through certain parts of the property. Each passing day it becomes harder to travel along it as the vegetation progressively covers it, and if it were not for the force of the water — since the path runs along the torrent — it would no longer exist.

Finding the cave is not difficult at all since it is right next to the path and the entrance dimensions of 1.70 x 1 m make it impossible to miss. The cave's orientation is 315° NW. As soon as we approach the entrance, we immediately notice that this natural cave has been adapted over time by humans, as the entrance has been modified to allow it to be closed with a door that no longer exists.

What stands out is the flat floor of much of the cave's single chamber, since most caves in the area begin descending immediately. Logically, the flatness of the floor is partly due to human hands, as they presumably used it as a shelter during work periods in the area — evidence of this being the ceramic remains found inside the cave.

I could not verify whether columns once existed in the center of the chamber that were torn out to leave more free space, although at the back of the chamber a fairly thick column lies on the ground. As I was saying, the cave consists of a beautiful chamber with the occasional compartment. Except for the left side of the cave which is not concretioned, the rest of the cave is: starting with a stalactite next to the right side of the cave which is possibly a decapitated column, a flowstone covers all the rest of the cave except for the previously mentioned part. A group of beautiful columns at the back of the chamber stands out, which at some point reminded me of the Coves Petites of Capdepera.

At the back and to the right of the chamber, one must descend a little more than two meters using only hands to reach a place that seems to function as the cave's sinkhole, where a pile of sediments dragged there by the force of water can be found. It should be remembered that the path by which one accesses the cave runs through the interior of the Sa Coma Fosca torrent, so it is not at all unusual for flash floods to occur that seep into the cave's interior. In fact, my first visit to the cave took place after heavy rains and everything seemed to indicate that a large quantity of water had entered.

Some activity is detected in certain parts of the cave, though not in the interesting group of columns that decorates it.

As for the cave's fauna, I could only observe inside it a single specimen of bat in the highest part of the ceiling, but in several places one could see efflorescences of droppings from these animals.

There are many caves and pits in the area, so we only have to choose one to complete the day if the visit to Cova d'en Domingo turns out to be too short.

Cova d'en Domingo is considered a Natural Site of Interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

name: Cova d'en Moret
starting_point: La Vileta cemetery
best_season: Avoid the hottest hours in summer
access: Own vehicle or La Vileta bus (EMT line 8)
coordinates_utm: 465840 E, 4384345 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 465745 E, 4384140 N (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A magnitude IV collapse cave linked to the dissolution of Keuper gypsum (Triassic) at the base of Serra de na Burguesa. First explored in 1956 by the EME group, it was lost and rediscovered in 2011. It features two main halls — the upper hall with a large wall flowstone, and the expansive Blocks Hall with impressive gypsum blocks, flowstones, stalactites, gours, and notably beautiful eccentric formations along the southwest wall. CO2 accumulations in deeper areas require caution.
full_description:
A bit of history

Between 1955 and 1956, the first Mallorcan speleology group, the "Equip Mallorquí d'Espeleologia" (EME), began speleological work in the "Serra de Tramuntana" (GINÉS, 1993). "Cova d'en Moret" was one of the caves this group explored and surveyed, beginning work on June 29, 1956 and finishing on November 4, 1956 (GINARD, 2009), in addition to carrying out biospeleological work. Everything seems to indicate that "Cova d'en Moret" had been missing from the world of speleology for a long time, along with some others the group had worked on. In fact, the existing coordinates of the cave did not help much in locating it, nor did the location map, nor the size of the entrance. Of magnitude IV, it is one of the largest caves in the area and one of the most interesting. The name of the cave perhaps refers to the person who may have found it, since Moret is a surname of Catalan origin, specifically from Girona and Barcelona, so many people carry it on the island. Unconfirmed, there appears to be a path with the same name in the area that may also have influenced the naming.

Rediscovery and exploration

July 9, 2011

J. M. Torres, a visitor to the area, contacted V. Pardo to inform him of the discovery of what he believed was an unrecorded cave. Pardo in turn contacted J. Bermejo, who after some checks and accompanied by V. Pardo went to the cavity, bringing with him the survey of "Cova d'en Moret" to verify whether it was indeed the same cave or an uncatalogued one. After confirming that the cave was the one surveyed by the Equip Mallorquí d'Espeleologia (EME), they took a few photos and left the site.

July 23, 2011

J. Bermejo and R. Minguillón began the survey of the cave with the intention of updating it, having to abandon the work a couple of hours later due to the considerable concentration of CO2 in the deepest part.

August 6, 2011

Again J. Bermejo, this time accompanied by V. Pardo, continued the survey work, taking advantage of a few free hours, and finished the plan of the "Column Hall."

August 10, 2011

J. Bermejo together with G. Herráez dedicated more than two hours to the survey. It is curious — having a cave so close to the city, instead of helping finish the work sooner, at least in this case, is prolonging it excessively.

August 18, 2011

Again J. Bermejo, accompanied by G. Herráez and two of his nephews, headed to the cave with the intention of finishing the survey. R. Minguillón joined the team a little later. Once the objective was achieved — at least that was the feeling, since one never knows — we concluded the day's work after three hours.

Description:

With a NE-SW orientation, this is a collapse cave linked to the dissolution of Keuper gypsum (Triassic) that forms the base of the Serra de na Burguesa, like some of the cavities found in the area of this work.

It is accessed through a small entrance of 0.93 x 0.65 m, descending along a ramp that exceeds 45° at some points with a height of less than fifty centimeters. Although there is an installation at the access entrance for using rope, it is possible to descend safely by using opposition technique.

Upper Hall

Once past the access ramp, we find ourselves in an elongated hall running SE-NW, with dimensions of 12 x 18 x 3 m, whose northeastern side is occupied by a large wall flowstone. We must continue the descent to the bottom of the hall where we will find the access beneath the large flowstone to our right.

In addition to the flowstone, a small rimstone pool in the deepest zone stands out in this hall, as well as the abundance of botryoidal speleothems, especially in the vicinity of the access to the Blocks Hall.

Blocks Hall

As its name indicates, upon entering the Blocks Hall, the great quantity of large rocky blocks occupying the floor of the hall stands out, which we must navigate around to continue our visit.

Its dimensions are 48 x 25 x 4 m and it is formed by two levels: a lower level where clastic processes predominate, and an upper level, at whose southwestern end reconstructive processes predominate.

First we descend to the lower level where we can appreciate the abundant presence of banded gypsum blocks in gray and white colors, with a saccharoidal appearance, where the existence of a large isolated column stands out. Only at the southern end of this level can we see a small area with speleothems (stalagmites, stalactites, columns, and some eccentric formations).

Next we ascend again to move to the southwestern end of the Blocks Hall, where the most beautiful area of the cavity is found, as it is the most concretioned zone. In this area we can enjoy abundant flowstones, stalagmites, stalactites, rimstone pools, and curtains. The eccentric formations that cover the entire southwestern wall stand out for their singular beauty. Both to access and to exit this area, we must pass through a beautiful stalagmitic curtain that separates it from the blocks zone.

A warning that the cavity occasionally presents CO2 accumulations in the areas farthest from the entrance. During our visit to the cave on July 23, 2011, we had to abandon work earlier than planned.

name: Cova d'en Palem
starting_point: Park past Km 6 of the Ma-1043. Continue on the road about 150 m to the barrier that gives access to the path leading to the cave.
equipment: Helmet and lighting only, plus a small rope for the final ramp if wet
description: Accessed through a small 50x70 cm entrance hidden behind a strawberry tree, this cave features a main chamber of approximately 50x35 m with diverse speleothems. The deepest point (-18 m) shows evidence of ancient water levels with phreatic speleothems reminiscent of those in Cova des Pas de Vallgornera. The most decorated area contains eccentric stalactites, flowstones, gours, and a distinctive disc-shaped formation.
full_description:
Description

Sheltered by a strawberry tree, we find the small entrance of 50 x 70 cm through which we will descend without any difficulty to enter through a strenuous gallery of about nine meters in length by one and a half meters in width, in which we can never stand upright. Initially it descends gently until reaching a point where we have to get past a low section by lying flat on the ground. Right at this point, when it rains, a small puddle forms that will wet our chest as well as the elbows and knees of our coveralls, if we are wearing this garment — which I certainly recommend. Already at this point, on the right side as we advance, a narrow inaccessible crack descends 6 m; a little further on something similar occurs on both sides. Soon the gallery begins to gain height until reaching a small room of about 14 meters in length by approximately half a dozen in width, sparsely decorated except on the east side where some small stalagmites and numerous stalactites grow in a crack. Worth noting is the existence of several micro rimstone pools (gours) at the base of the largest and thickest stalagmite, which is responsible for supplying them with water. At the time of my visit on November 3, 2008, these were full of water, and all the speleothems in this corner were active. The room also contains blocks of considerable size resulting from ceiling collapses. At the other end of the room, a narrow crack separates it from the continuation of the cave except for some sections due to successive collapses.

To continue the visit, we can do so from the end of the room where, next to some formations, the opening allows us to descend a little over a meter to a dusty ramp that will deposit us on a ledge where a barrier built by the cave seems to block our way. We find ourselves in the main chamber of the cave, of considerable size — approximately 50 x 35 meters. We are in the vicinity of the most decorated area of the cave, and I will describe how to traverse it. I recommend continuing to the deepest part, always keeping the stalagmitic barrier to our right, until reaching a pair of windows, the left one being the one that allows us to continue — the right window makes it more difficult since a small column right behind the entrance obstructs our passage. Putting our feet through first, we will cross the window to access a ramp possibly covered by a film of water (depending on the season), which will not pose great difficulty to descend if we lean on a small column. Once at the bottom, we are at the deepest part of the cave at -18 m depth. It is a gallery with a very low ceiling running in an E-W direction. To reach the lowest point, we will again have to lie flat to get past a very low section and reach the sinkhole that is responsible for draining the cave's water. Two things are worth highlighting here: first, if we look carefully at the ceiling of this area, we can observe how all of it was long ago filled with water — it was therefore a lake in which curious phreatic speleothems formed, some of which are reminiscent of the famous "lightbulbs" of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera.

To continue the visit, we will not return the way we came but rather through another sort of window found near the sinkhole, climbing up a ramp to another gallery-like passage that also runs E to W, just like the lower one. We will ascend another level where another tiny window will place us in the main chamber next to a couple of rimstone pools. Eccentric stalactites decorate the ceiling in this area along with other formations. To continue the visit, we can do so from two different spots: one is from the highest part, and the other is to the right of the window through which we arrived here.
