If we choose the latter option, we will again have to lie flat — or rather, lie on the floor flowstone that harbors tiny rimstone pools full of water that will wet us again. If we want to continue through the upper part of the chamber, we need only climb a ramp that will deposit us near a large rimstone pool in the most decorated area of the cave. Of note is the disc-shaped or "ensaimada"-shaped speleothem that harbors the waters of the rimstone pool. In this place we can find all types of formations, including a great variety of eccentric stalactites. Before leaving this area through a sort of doorway — at one end of which the effects of solifluction on several columns can be appreciated — we can head upward in a NW direction until reaching a stalagmitic massif next to a small room of slightly more than half a dozen meters on a lower level. On our return, we will exit through the aforementioned "doorway" in order to continue visiting the eastern area.

The time has come to prepare to "suffer" a bit. In this area the ceiling height in some places does not exceed 30 cm — floor flowstones covered with a film of water, ceilings that crumble with just the touch of a finger, dust in great abundance — in short, a bit of everything, but it is worth visiting from the bottom to the upper part, which is none other than the entrance hall or vestibule of the cavity, from which all that remains is to exit.

name: Cova des Bastons / Cova C-11
starting_point: Port of Alcúdia or Coll Baix beach
route_type: Terrestrial, aquatic, and subaquatic (subaquatic section not covered)
approach_time: One hour by boat from Alcúdia port
best_season: Must check sea conditions before visiting
access: Boat or kayak from Alcúdia port. Coll Baix beach is closer but the trail is storm-damaged.
equipment: Nothing special
description: A sea-accessible cave with four main areas. The spacious entrance hall (30x54x12 m) features botryoidal speleothems and coraloids. A small 4 m well leads to the Paleonivell chamber with marine phreatic speleothems from ancient Mediterranean sea levels. The Lake chamber is the most beautiful section, with formations and a floating calcite layer covering the water surface. Beyond the lake, 200 m of cave-diving passage contains staff-shaped stalactites.
full_description:
Description.

Entrance Hall

It is a spacious hall of 30 x 54 x 12 m, accessed after navigating the impressive collapse of large blocks that occupy practically the entire entrance. Formations are not too abundant inside, although they can be found in some places. Noteworthy are some botryoidal speleothems in the center of the hall, a small number of coraloids on the east wall, and a beautiful flowstone in the area farthest from the entrance, just to the left of the point where the ascent begins to continue visiting the cave, as well as a couple of stalagmites in the central area and some inactive gours.

The Well

It is a small well located approximately halfway through the cavity that does not exceed 4 m in depth, accessed via a traverse line that was installed at some point by a group of German cave divers, thus sparing us from having to use progression equipment.

Paleonivell Chamber

Measuring 9 x 20 x 9 m, noteworthy in it is a group of beautiful formations that house some phreatic speleothems as a result of an ancient Mediterranean level that today is only visible through said speleothems and the marks that have been left on the walls of the chamber. Calcareous breccias can also be seen in this part of the cave.

Lake Chamber

With its 15.5 x 16.5 x 8 m, it is perhaps the most beautiful place in the aquatic-terrestrial zone of the cavity, as it houses beautiful ceiling formations as well as a layer of calcite in suspension that covers the entire surface of the lake.

From this point, the visit to the rest of the cave can only be done through cave diving for approximately 200 longitudinal meters, in which formations abound, among them beautiful stalactites shaped like staffs.

Note: The speleological outing can be completed by visiting Cova C-10, which is located right above it.

name: Cova des Cavallers
equipment: No equipment needed except helmet
description: A cave consisting of a single highly compartmentalized chamber with abundant speleothems. Its central area features a notable fault mirror resulting from ceiling detachment. Known since antiquity, it was visited by Archduke Ludwig Salvator.
full_description:
A cave formed basically by a single highly compartmentalized room, in which speleothemic formations abound. In its central zone, a notable fault mirror can be observed, the result of the detachment of the ceiling. Known since antiquity, it was visited by Archduke Ludwig Salvator.

Access entrance to the cave.

Beautiful row of columns in the zone near the entrance.

Cavers on an ancient ceiling of the cavity.

name: Cova des Coll des Vent
starting_point: La Vileta Cemetery
coordinates_utm: 465705 E, 4384926 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 465595 E, 4384720 N (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: 15m rope
description: A modestly-sized but complex cave near the Coll des Vent pass, located just meters from a lime kiln along the trail. It contains multiple interconnected chambers including the low-ceilinged Sala Baixa and a near-vertical fracture (S'Escletxa) requiring a rope descent. The cave features beautiful flowstones, small gours, dolomitic breccias, and well-decorated chambers across four distinct sections.
full_description:
Description.

In the vicinity of the Coll des Vent and just ten meters to the right of the path, past a lime kiln, is the entrance to this modestly-sized cavity.

Despite its small dimensions, the cave is not without a certain complexity, although it is not out of place among a large number of caves nestled in the Serra de Na Burguesa.

Entrance Hall (Sala d'Entrada)

A typical hall oriented SE/NW, with a relatively gentle slope tending toward submersion in the deepest zone. The actual dimensions of the hall are not visible, since practically all its extremities are filled with stones that prevent seeing possible continuity. The ornamentation is found in the southern zone, formed by beautiful flowstones that harbor small gours. In the ceiling area of the hall, dolomitic breccias are visible. In the SW zone of the hall (flowstone zone), a window gives continuity to the cave — in reality there are two, the one on the left being the most accessible.

Lower Hall (Sala Baixa)

To access it from the Entrance Hall, we do so through the aforementioned window by crawling. Once inside, we immediately understand the reason for its name. The hall has an S/N orientation like the Entrance Hall, and is heavily concretioned. The cave continues in a SW/NE direction. When we reach a place where three columns are practically aligned, we descend to a lower level and from there proceed through a narrow passage that places us in a zone where a nearly vertical fracture opens before us.

The Fissure (S'Escletxa)

It follows the same orientation as the Entrance Hall, although with a greater tendency toward the west. It has a width that does not exceed 4 m and a length of 13 m. To begin the descent, one must use a rope secured to a thick stalagmite. Before reaching the bottom of the fracture, there is a platform formed by the accumulation of blocks.

Flowstone Hall (Sala de sa Colada)

Once 'landed,' if we take the SW direction we will come to a kind of balcony from which the end of the fracture can be observed, about five meters below. On the contrary, if we head in the NE direction we will approach the Flowstone Hall. This is a small hall 5 m wide through which a beautiful flowstone descends. Looking at it from the front, on the left at slightly more than 1 m in height, a small suspended room is located, and another at the other end, at whose far end clearing would be necessary to continue. It has not been cleared since it does not seem to invite much more and some formations would have to be broken.

name: Cova des Coloms I
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma-4014 road connecting Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, a path leads approximately 1.5 km to a half-open metal gate with a chain. After crossing, the trail leads to Cala Varques, then along the coast to Cala Falcó. From there, swim approximately 250 m to reach the cave entrance.
clothing: Neoprene wetsuit and water shoes recommended
distance: 1,730 m to Cala Falcó plus 250 m by sea
best_season: Any except when the sea is rough
equipment: Helmet, headlamp, neoprene wetsuit, canyoning backpack, fins (optional), waterproof compartment for camera. For vertical access: 40 m rope, 3 carabiners, and a rope protector.
description: One of the most beautiful caves on the island, accessed by swimming through a narrow sea-level gallery into a stunning interior. The cave contains three lakes with crystal-clear brackish water, extensive stalactite formations including eccentric varieties, impressive stalagmites rising from the water, and fine sand beaches. The deepest lake reaches -18 m and contains submerged decorated chambers, indicating the water level was once much lower.
full_description:
We find ourselves before one of the most beautiful caves on the island without any doubt, with the added bonus that access to it is of great interest as the route passes through a marvelous cliffside area, and to top it off we will swim approximately two hundred and fifty meters to reach the interior of the cave.

Before continuing, I want to warn that access to the cave is through the sea, which implies a risk that we must try to mitigate as much as possible simply by taking the following precautions: once at Cala Falcó, we will check the state of the sea, and if we are not sure about it, we leave it for another day, since we are in an area with abundant caves so we can make use of the day equally. In the cove itself there is the cave of the same name, which is worth visiting. Just think about one thing: if the state of the sea in the cove is not good, at the entrance of the cave it is going to be much worse since the waves break with force against the mouth. One more thing: if our stay in the cave goes on too long, it would not be a bad idea to peek out at the entrance from time to time to check the state of the sea.

Access to the cave proper is through a narrow gallery that we will traverse swimming with the water separated from the ceiling by approximately thirty centimeters, to finally emerge into a small chamber where we will remove our fins before continuing to advance to the next one, where we immediately begin to discover that we are in a special place. The ceiling, which I don't think is much more than four meters high, is covered almost entirely by thousands of stalactites. Some of the chamber walls are also covered by parietal flowstones, not to mention the marvelous stalagmitic massif at the back of the chamber that provides continuity to the cave. The entire floor remains carpeted by a layer of fine sand.

To continue the visit, we can go either to the left or to the right of the aforementioned massif, since the two paths meet up later. If we go to the left, we will soon reach the smallest of the three lakes found in the cave — and stunningly beautiful, with the ceiling completely covered in fine stalactites, some of them eccentric, as well as all their extremities — which we must cross by swimming and climb through a crack to leave it behind and continue on our way. Soon we will arrive at a new lake, this one larger, where the right-hand path from the first chamber converges. Like the previous one, it is of great beauty, with a striking highlight being an impressive stalagmite that rises several meters out of the water, surrounded by smaller ones. It is worth noting that a small sand beach decorates one end of this little jewel. We will continue on our way over dry land, always surrounded by stalagmites, columns, and gours of different sizes, until we reach the third and last lake, which marks the end of the cave. What to say about this place — when certain people I know have seen photos of this area of the cave, they cannot stop marveling, and it is no wonder. However, the sensations one can feel being present there are indescribable. It is the largest lake in the cave and the deepest, as it apparently reaches -18 m. The cave's concretions also extend beneath the waters, where there is a small beautifully decorated chamber, which tells us that at some point the water level was at least eighteen meters lower — or water did not exist in this place — which allowed the cave's concretions to form. As I was saying earlier, we are in a chamber partially covered by a lake of crystalline brackish water, where it is of course essential to swim from one end to the other. Standing out are the great quantity of eccentric stalactites that crowd together on the walls, not to mention an impressive stalactite of several meters with a banner-like shape.

If you enjoy photography, you could spend hours and hours firing the camera without time seeming to pass, and that is why at the beginning I mentioned the importance of monitoring the state of the sea, as it can change easily during the course of our visit.

There is another very interesting option for accessing the cave for people with rappelling knowledge, which is to descend into the cave using vertical progression equipment (there are several installed anchors that we can use) via an overhang of about twenty-five meters that will leave us in the water just a few meters from the narrow funnel that provides access to the terrestrial zone of the cave.

If we have time left over, in the area there are multiple caves to visit besides the one at Cala Falcó mentioned earlier. Also very close to the cove is the Cova del Moro, of great speleological and archaeological interest, and the Cova des Llimacs, small but infrequently visited.

As M. Trias (1992) says, the Cova des Coloms is not a sea cave but rather a cave with a marine entrance created by the erosion of the sea, which subsequently flooded the already existing cave, and which possibly began its formation through a diversion of the waters of the torrent that carved Cala Falcó.

For further reading:

Miquel TRIAS (1992): NOVES DADES SOBRE LA COVA DES COLOMS 1. Endins, 17-18: 21-23 Palma de Mallorca.

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): Les coves de la zona de Can Frasquet cala Varques. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.

Phreatic speleothems of the coastal caves of Mallorca: their contribution to the study of the Quaternary. JOAQUÍN GINÉS 1, 2, PAOLA TUCCIMEI 3, 4, JOAN J. FORNÓS 1, ANGEL GINÉS 2, 5, FRANCESC GRÀCIA 6 AND PIERLUIGI VESICA 7 BULLETIN No. 3 SEDECK / YEAR 2002 / SPANISH SOCIETY OF SPELEOLOGY AND KARST SCIENCES.

Current state of scientific knowledge of the karst and caves of the Balearic Islands. ÀNGEL GINÉS (1) AND (3) AND JOAQUÍN GINÉS (2) AND (3). (1) Department of Biology (Ecology Area), University of the Balearic Islands. (2) Department of Earth Sciences, University of the Balearic Islands. (3) Speleological Group EST, Palma de Mallorca.

name: Cova des Corral des Porcs
starting_point: Municipal sports center (polideportivo) parking
best_season: Avoid summer due to high CO2 concentration
access: From Palma, take the train from Estación Intermodal
equipment: 1 rope (15m), 1 rope (20m), 2 slings, carabiners
description: A LIC-protected karstic cave featuring multiple chambers connected by narrow passages, with notable geological features including a fault mirror, coraloid speleothems, dissolution channels, and eccentric stalactites. CO2 levels can reach 2.7-5%, making summer visits inadvisable. The cave shows significant subsidence processes throughout.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova de sa Guitarreta is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities catalogued as LIC. Currently (see date on the cave's information sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Ministry of the Environment and Territory, so before making any visit, the required permit must be obtained to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Entrance Zone I

A small vestibule of 5 x 4 m depth ends in a window of reduced dimensions through which one must lie down to cross by crawling, connecting to a 9 m long corridor with a low ceiling that leads into a chamber of larger dimensions, where a reasonable quantity of formations are present. To the north of the chamber, in a considerable descent, one goes down to a sort of funnel-shaped ramp that leads into a difficult area to traverse due to the scarcity of space, finally opening into the Sala de s'Entrada II (Entrance Chamber II).

Sala de s'Entrada II (Entrance Chamber II)

To access the Sala de s'Entrada, one must first crawl a few meters until finding a window that, after an easy downclimb, places us inside. It is a structural chamber of 10 x 9 x 4 m with a teepee-shaped ceiling morphology, formed by the subsidence process that occurred when the subsurface was emptied. The chamber is not devoid of formations; in fact, it is reasonably decorated, although it appears to lack activity. On the NE side, a small opening connects to the Entrance Zone I, while to the north, the cave continues.

Es Racó (The Corner)

This is a small corner of 3.5 x 5 m, hidden behind a gour in the Sala de s'Entrada II. The reduced dimensions of the entrance between two small columns mean that one must even remove their helmet to access the interior of this small corner. Number 11 on the topography.

Pou des Caos (Chaos Shaft)

A window situated at the head (SE zone of the chamber) allows descent into a ten-meter-deep shaft with two levels. On the first of the two levels encountered during descent, only the existence of some formations is worth noting. On the lower level, the enormous collapse suffered as a result of the subsidence process that occurred here, which may have also affected the Sala de s'Entrada II, is clearly visible. The large rock blocks pile up against each other while leaving spaces between them, giving a certain sense of insecurity in the place.

Sala des Mirall de Falla (Fault Mirror Chamber)

This is one of the largest chambers in the cavity and also houses a significant number of speleothems. Perhaps the most significant feature of this place is the subsidence process that undoubtedly was related to the Pou des Caos, in which a detachment caused a beautiful fault mirror on the eastern side of the chamber.

Galeria sense Nom (Unnamed Gallery)

On the SW side of the Sala des Mirall de Falla, a gallery opens behind a barrier formed by small columns, of little interest. It follows the same direction as the rest of the fracture, eventually becoming impassable.

Galeria des Paleonivells (Paleolevels Gallery)

Following the NW-SE direction like the rest of the cave's main fracture, this gallery is accessed by climbing very carefully from the Sala de sa Cascada (Waterfall Chamber). The most noteworthy feature of the place is what appear to be ancient paleolevels marked on a block that has been moved from its original position, which may have been part of an ancient deposit where water level oscillations inside left their mark.

Pou des Coraloides (Coraloid Shaft)

With a maximum depth of eight meters, it contains a 16 m passable level following the same orientation as the rest of the cave's fracture, with variable height. Inside, and hence its name, large quantities of coraloid speleothems predominate, among which the botryoidal variety is abundant, covering a large part of the walls.

Sala de sa Cascada (Waterfall Chamber)

With a width of 6 m, a length of 20 m, and a height of 9 m, it is the largest chamber in the cavity.

Galeria des CO2 (CO2 Gallery)

Entering this small gallery of 3 x 7.5 x 2 m through the reduced entrance involves making contact with mud that will accompany us from this point until reaching the Sala de ses Excèntriques (Chamber of the Eccentrics). It is in this zone of the cave where measurements taken to check the amount of carbon dioxide (CO2) present reached a concentration of 2.70%. In measurements carried out in previous years, the CO2 concentration reached 5%.

Galeria de sa Corrosió (Corrosion Gallery)

It begins right at the eastern zone of the Sala de ses Excèntriques, in the approximately four-meter-deep shaft, through a lamination passage of considerable slope through which the suffering speleologist must travel, dealing not only with the lack of space but also with water and mud. Throughout the entire route, the gallery environment is affected by corrosion processes, with abundant speleothems present.

Sala de ses Excèntriques (Chamber of the Eccentrics)

A beautiful small chamber of 11 x 18 x 2 m. In it, a significant stratigraphic detachment is visible, which is currently part of the ceiling of the Galeria de sa Corrosió. Many speleothems are housed within, notably a column of approximately one meter in diameter covered with eccentrics for the most part, as well as "curtains" of delicate formations that separate this chamber from the Sala des Canals de Dissolució.

Sala des Canals de Dissolució (Dissolution Channels Chamber)

Measuring 12 x 9 x 3 m, access to this chamber from the Sala de ses Excèntriques is through a small barrier of formations, among which multiple eccentric stalactites are found, via a small window. Traversing some parts of it generates certain difficulty. Perhaps the most significant feature is in the ceiling zone, where dissolution channels can be contemplated through which pressurized water was responsible for the formation of this zone of the cavity.

name: Cova des Diners
starting_point: Son Toell (Porto Cristo)
distance: 105 m from where the vehicle is parked
access: Own vehicle, or take the Porto Cristo bus from Palma
equipment: Most of the cave is accessible without vertical progression gear, though some shafts at the extremes require it. A 30 m rope, sling, and a couple of rope protectors are sufficient to access any part of the cave.
description: A labyrinthine cave classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC) under EU protection, requiring official permission to visit. The cave features large formations, impressive gours, a notable fault mirror spanning 40 m, and the dramatic 'Descent to Hell' area. Navigation is extremely challenging; a marked route has been designed for first-time visitors to prevent getting lost. The cave has the highest conservation grade and must not be contaminated with candles or markers.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova des Diners is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI.

At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

Introduction

Due to the difficulty that exists in this labyrinthine cave for orientation, we have designed a route that can serve well for a first visit, said route covering a surface large enough to give a perspective of the cave, but we must not let our guard down since even with this layout one must take the utmost care not to get lost. Under no circumstances should candles or anything else that could contaminate the cave be brought inside to mark the way back, as doing so could lead to its contamination and ultimately a serious fine from the Ministry of the Environment. One must not forget that the Cova des Diners is under the European Union and has the highest level of conservation.

Route Signposting

On the topographic map that accompanies this cave, a route has been designed marked in green with lines of two thicknesses: the thicker one allows us to follow the route, while the thin one allows us to make side trips and return. On this route, the use of vertical progression equipment is not necessary, nor is any rope, although one must always be watchful not to slip or put one's boot into any of the many "traps" that exist throughout the cave. We have also designed circles with a black background and orange numbers that are placed strategically on the topo map to help us locate ourselves.

Description

From the entrance to point 1

We will access the cave through the door that is fitted to be closed with a metal barrier but which is currently dismantled inside (square with green background on the topographic map). Once inside, head toward the other entrance, clearly visible by the light entering from outside. A small escarpment of approximately 2 m must be descended to continue advancing through a gallery until it widens. As a reference, on the left side one can observe an elegant colonnade of more or less one meter in height (point 1 on the topo).

From point 1 to point 2

If we ascend along the flowstone, we will soon encounter steps carved into it, and a little further ahead a "wall" that we must pass through. Once on the other side of the "wall," we find ourselves at point 2 on the topo.

From point 2 to point 3

We begin the descent from point 2 between columns of considerable size, one of them affected by a subsidence process at its midpoint. The distance between point 2 and point 3 is around 20 m in length and ends right where the cave becomes more spacious and the floor practically horizontal.

From point 3 to point 4

Heading 200° N with a length of 20 m, in a gentle descent to point 4. Along the way we will encounter formations of great size and thickness, as well as some gours on our right, while at the height of point 4, on our left after a brief descent, we will reach a gour of considerable size.

From point 4 to point 5

This section heading 160° and approximately 18 m ends right at a large rock fallen from the ceiling that has left a gap in the ceiling shaped like a "teepee" (like a Canadian tent). This section is not of great interest.

From point 5 to point 6

We will begin by climbing to the highest area of the rock, and from it we will start walking 30 m heading 100° N, this time through a low-ceilinged zone where we can never stand upright until the end of it, where we must downclimb on our right over a small escarpment.

From point 6 to point 7

In this section of approximately 35 m heading 190° N, we will pass through a zone where the ceiling is even lower than in the previous section, and on our left an escarpment will accompany us for the entire stretch, giving way to the most complex and demanding zone of the cave. In the final part of the section.

From point 7 to point 8

40 m in length heading 325° N, this brings us to the vicinity of the Baixada a l'Infern (Descent to Hell), which we will reach after descending an escarpment where the ceiling is very low.

From point 8 to point 9

This 20 m section is the one that definitively places us at the head of the Baixada a l'Infern, and its heading is 250° N.

From point 9 to point 10

This route is for taking a look at the Baixada a l'Infern as far as we see fit, heading south, and then returning to the starting point and subsequently to point 8.

From point 8 to point 11

A round-trip route heading 280° N, through a gentle slope until we overlook the East Hall.

From point 8 to point 5

This 13 m section heading 40° N connects with point 5 and point 4, previously visited.

From point 4 to point 12

This section of approximately 35 m in length with a heading of 285° N approaches nearly the western side of the cave. Along the way we will pass between formations of great size in the final part. Of note is the impressive fault mirror that can be contemplated on the left, with a length of 40 m.

From point 12 to point 13

Approximately 20 m in length at 30° N, it ends right at a point where a crack in the floor begins, more than 20 m long, and will serve us for making an excursion to the Sala de sa Basseta in the next section. On our right, the possibly tallest cone of collapse blocks in the cave can be seen.

From point 13 to point 14

We will deviate from the route by a few meters to enter a beautiful chamber, which is none other than the Sala de sa Basseta, accessed heading 312° N over 10 m through a narrow passage where a small pit must also be carefully negotiated. The visit to this place is worthwhile, after which we return to the starting point.

From point 13 to point 15

First heading 45° N over 40 m in length, this relatively comfortable section crosses a block of large dimensions. It also hides some interesting zones to the west, though of difficult access.

From point 15 to point 3

Covering 37 m heading 170° N through perhaps the most comfortable zone of the cave, which is not lacking in formations, some of them of great size. Along the way we will encounter some gours that at certain times of the year are covered with water. We pass through point 3.

From point 3 to point 2

As we already know it, I will take the liberty of not providing any description.

From point 2 to point 1

On this occasion we will exit with an approximate heading of 345° N, although perhaps this data may not be too necessary, since we only need to keep in mind that we must advance "hugging" the right side, and that will be sufficient. As soon as we begin, we will encounter a first gour of some depth, and if it holds water, we may end up putting a foot in it. A little further on we will find another that usually stores a large quantity of calcite particles. We continue walking in a gentle descent, being careful not to miss a beautiful large gour on our right behind a small flowstone. Once past it, we continue advancing through low-ceilinged terrain, always keeping the reference that we must not stray too far from the wall that always runs on our right, until we reach a new gour that complicates our passage with water, but which can be navigated on its left side between two columns. The height of the cave becomes slightly more generous though without allowing us to stand upright. Never losing the wall on our right, we will head toward point 1, leaving on our left ground that has been disturbed for reasons that are not relevant to explain now. The column mentioned at point 1 has had a small cord tied around it for a long time. From this point, the exit should no longer cause us any problems.

name: Cova des Drac o des Rafal des Porcs
name_english: Dragon Cave (or Rafal des Porcs Cave)
starting_point: Cala de s'Almunia
clothing: Depends on season for approach. Coveralls recommended inside with good footwear.
equipment: If accessing through the ceiling entrance: 30m rope, sling, and 1 protector. Can be installed on natural anchor.
description: Classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC), this cave near the prehistoric settlement of Ses Talaies des Bauç requires official authorization to visit. The main chamber measures 43x80m and reaches 51m high, formed by successive collapses. The submerged section features abundant speleothems including columns on a small island, and a 400m long gallery with 1.4m average height extends from the cave.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova des Drac is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI. At present (see the date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Department of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter. At present, access to this cavity can only be made through the chasm.

Near the prehistoric settlement of Ses Talaies des Bauç is the Cova des Drac or des Rafal des Porcs, which of course had an important relationship with the settlement, since this settlement possibly would not have existed without the water that the cave holds.

DESCRIPTION

The Cova des Drac can be accessed through two entrances. One of them is located right in the upper center of the Main Hall of the cave in the form of a chasm, with approximate dimensions of 3 x 2 m. The interior is accessed by a free-hanging rappel of 11 meters; however, it is much easier to enter through the other entrance, as no equipment is needed for that. Steps carved into the rock allow us to access the cave once past a small metal barrier (when the entrance has free access). Once on the other side, we can stand upright and walk comfortably through a wide gallery until reaching a small escarpment that places us right in front of the great hall.

The entrance is currently blocked with two large stones.

MAIN HALL

The Cova des Drac consists mainly of a single chamber formed as a result of successive collapses over time, measuring 43 m wide by somewhat more than 80 m long and 51 m high from the water table in the flooded zone. Currently, the clastic cone occupies practically the entire space.

Traversing all areas of the hall does not present great difficulties, although it is necessary to find the way since in some points it is not possible to pass due to the existing escarpments. The most relevant difficulty for moving through the hall is a slippery floor on which one can stumble.

FLOODED ZONE

While in the terrestrial zone speleothems are not abundant, in the flooded zone of the cavity they are plentiful, harboring a great variety throughout this part of the cave, in which a large portion consists of an air chamber. Once past the first 17 m of the subaquatic siphon, a beautiful air chamber opens up, formed by a hall that houses a small islet on which two small but thick columns have developed, surrounded by delicate ceiling speleothems. A little further on, a 10 m deep well opens up, occupying an important part of the hall, and at its bottom emerges a beautiful column approximately 2 m in diameter which, due to a subsidence process, is currently separated from the ceiling of the hall.

LONG GALLERY (GALERIA LLARGA)

Although it is not mapped here, only its location indicated, there is a gallery in this cavity that can be visited without the need for vertical progression equipment, as it is a gallery with an average height of 1.4 m and a length of 400 m according to the survey by J. A. Encinas.

name: Cova des Genet
starting_point: Due to recent access problems to Cala Varques, check conditions before visiting. Alternatively, approach from the coast via Cala Romántica.
equipment: Vertical progression equipment and 12-13 m of rope required.
description: Discovered in 2008 near Cala Varques, this cave has a small 50x70 cm entrance leading to a 6-meter descent into a spacious main chamber approximately 27 meters wide. The hall is richly decorated with columns surrounded by dry gours, and a ceiling covered with thousands of fine stalactites. Uniquely, the cave is formed in sandstone rather than limestone, distinguishing it from neighboring caves in the area.
full_description:
While cycling in August with his mountain bike through the Cala Varques area, F. Bonnin, a dear companion of many "adventures" both in the island's mountains and in the Pyrenean mountain range, by chance found the entrance to the cave, so he did not hesitate to record the location coordinates with his GPS as he usually does each time he finds something, and immediately upon arriving home he called me by phone to inform me of the discovery in case it was of interest to me. The most curious thing for me was discovering that nothing cataloged was to be found at that location despite the area being sufficiently frequented by both tourists, speleologists, and hikers. On August 10th, after a visit to the Cala Falcó cave, I passed by the location to identify the cave entrance and get an idea of what I was going to find there, which I certainly did not guess, since the idea that slipped into my head in no way corresponded with the reality I found on my first visit.

For a long time, the cave also known as Cova des Xots, present in the area, has been known — apparently incorrectly — as Cova des Genet. A long time ago, J. Aguiló began cataloging the caves of the island of Mallorca, noting in a book by C. Veny their location and referring to a cave in this area of cathedral-like dimensions with the name Genet. It is clear that J. Aguiló exaggerated a bit in this description since, although the cave is of magnitude III, the dimensions, particularly of its main hall, do not quite reach that level. The fact is that, unless someone proves otherwise, the Cova des Genet is not the one that was previously believed to be so, but rather the one we are discussing here.

First Exploration

On September 7th, 2008, accompanied by A. Villar, J. A. Encinas, and M. L. Redondo, we set out to explore and also to survey the cave, but we only accomplished the first of these, in addition to photographing the cave, leaving the topography pending for a later visit, as we did not expect to find a cave of those dimensions and characteristics.

Second Exploration

On October 5th, 2008, a group consisting of J. A. Encinas, G. Herraez, J. A. Pérez, and myself accessed the cave with the intention of surveying it, always under the direction of J. A. Encinas.

Description

The entrance to the cave is located next to one of the trails that, starting from the beach of Cala Varques, border the coast in the direction of Cala Falcó. The dimensions of the cave entrance are 50 x 70 cm, and as we make our way into the interior of the 6-meter-deep shaft, it gradually widens, finally ending the descent with an overhang that deposits us on a pile of debris in which, alongside the stones that have been thrown in from the outside, there are bottles, cans, and bones of pigs and other animals whose carcasses were thrown by humans into the cave, since it is difficult, though not impossible, that some fell inside due to the small dimensions of the entrance.

Once "landed," we find ourselves in a small vestibule of almost circular shape where the first concretions are already visible, notably a small column a little more than 1.5 m from the floor near the place where the cave continues through a descending gallery with a slippery floor depending on the time of year. The cave "drops" from right to left, and on the right side a great quantity of formations and numerous stones from some collapses can be appreciated. Fractured column remnants can also be seen, as in some other parts of the cave, possibly from some terrain adjustment. The slope is quite steep while the cave simultaneously narrows. On the opposite side, however, access is much easier despite the numerous stones that carpet the floor, among which some vegetal "stalagmites" can be observed, which are also abundant in the Cova de s'Embut.

As we continue advancing, a small room appears to our left, a little before arriving at the great hall of this cave, where we will have no choice but to marvel at such beauty. Approximately 27 meters wide, this hall is filled with columns, some of them surrounded by gours unfortunately empty during both my visits, with a ceiling from which thousands of the finest stalactites hang, some of which can reach one meter in length, interspersed with some of greater thickness. On the north side of the hall, a small pool (dry at the time of the visit) serves as the cave's sinkhole where the water from a well-marked stream is discharged, collecting it from different points. The mark on the wall showing the level the water reached at some point is visible. A small room is found at the end of the main hall with no continuity, and another one hides behind a slope further to the right, accessed without any difficulty. Many of the columns in this cave are joined in two pieces, possibly from a terrain subsidence, but subsequently the column was completed again.

We have tried to find the connection between this cave and its neighbor the Cova des Colom II but it has not been possible to find it, although that does not mean it does not exist.

In addition to the previously mentioned curious vegetal "stalagmites," different speleothems hide in the corners of the cave, where it is even possible to observe some scarce eccentric stalactites.

From the file on the Cova des Xenet, or des Genet, J. A. Encinas (CCMallorca, unpublished work, 1,400 pages) provides me with the following extract:

"... C. Veny reproduces, in his 1968 work, the list of caves from the J. Aguiló archive, including the Cova des Genet, of which he says it has a 'small opening and inside, like a church.' If, as we believe, this is the present cave in the surroundings of Son Forteza de Manacor, the cited scholar must have interpreted the phoneme of the toponym in relation to the site being situated right on a hillock, at the orographic saddle between Cala Varques and Cala Falcó. But xenet was formerly a significant nautical term, a type of thick rope already quite frayed on the surface from use; xenilla in Catalan, when it is woven on purpose to leave fringes and make the rope comfortable to handle for a good grip. A type of rope still common in the verticalized accesses of some shafts and in certain caves. So in the referenced Cova des Genet, the scant ten meters of vertical drop could in its time be overcome by going down by arms through the opening, with feet against the wall, until the overhang of the bottom section required grasping the very specific quality of the fuzzy rope. Once in the spacious interior hall, one verifies the descriptive value of the simile used by the cleric J. Aguiló, as beautiful and powerful columns abound, along with dense zenith concentrations of fistular stalactites, eccentric filigrees, some singular vegetal formations formed under the shelter of punctual water drippings, and barely any archaeological evidence of anthropic frequentation, save for some fragments of nearly contemporary ceramics. The cavern is aligned with the immediate marine cave of Cala Varques (Coloms, C.10), from which it is separated underground by some fifty meters, following the same structural discontinuity and developing a gallery of subsidential character with appreciable vertical glyptochemical progression, reaching in Xenet (X.06) 74 m of projected length, with a maximum width of 32 and a total vertical drop on the order of a dozen..."

Unlike its neighbors in the area, the rock from which the Cova des Genet is formed is not limestone, but sandstone — a detrital sedimentary rock of variable color. Sandstone is very porous, showing various degrees of porosity.

Fauna

The fauna found at the time of the visit consisted of an unidentified spider as well as several blowflies that did not stop bothering us during the entire surveying work. Perhaps the absence of chiropterans (bats) caught my attention.

The Cova des Genet lends itself to spending a good photographic day inside, but if it is not sufficient to complete it, one only needs to visit some of the many caves found in the area.

For further reading:

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): Les coves de la zona de Can Frasquet – Cala Varques. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.

Francesc GRACIA, Bernat CLAMOR i Joan Josep LAVERGNE. (2000): LES COVES DE CALA VARQUES (Manacor, Mallorca). Endins, 23: 41-57. Palma de Mallorca

name: Cova des Gour de sa Purísima
name_english: Cave of the Gour of the Purísima
starting_point: Cemetery of La Vileta
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A small cave discovered in April 2009 near the Crull des Forms des Guix area, featuring a beautiful dry gour containing delicate columns and a stalagmite resembling a seated Virgin with child, which gives the cave its name. Notable for botryoidal speleothems, gypsum crystallizations, and various flowstone formations. Likely a remnant of a larger cavity fragmented by nearby quarry operations.
full_description:
On April 6, 2009, accompanied by G. Herraez, we set out to visit 3 small caves that I had pending when I was surprised to find that in the area, a few meters above the Crull des Forms des Guix, my companion pointed out the existence of a small hole that could be the entrance to a cavity. We immediately set about checking it, despite my initial doubts about the existence of a new cave in that location, since in the exhaustive work published on the caves and mines of the Pillar in ENDINS No. 27, this one did not appear. To our surprise, there was this small cave of very similar dimensions to its neighbors, as well as similar beauty.

The cave is oriented 11° N with entrance dimensions of 1.20 m wide by 0.70 m high, which we will access after climbing up the cliff face about 5 m without great difficulty.

The first few meters of the cave are through a crawlway that barely reaches one meter in height at any point, so we must crawl through it for about six meters, first passing on our left another small crawlway with an exit to the outside but inaccessible. At the end of the crawlway, a passage cleared by the decapitation of some columns, and next to a first dry gour, on the left side a narrow entrance opens, and once on the other side we can gradually stand up until reaching a vertical position. A little further ahead and to the left, a small crawlway heading SSW begins in an ascending direction but ends its course after three meters. The cave continues in a NNW direction, downclimbing alongside large blocks of loose pavement flowstone at first until reaching firm ground easily, from where we head through a narrow passage to a small entrance that gives us access to a beautiful gour, also without water, which houses several slender and beautiful columns inside it. Between two of them there is a small stalagmite with a certain resemblance to a seated Virgin with the child in her arms, and which together with the gour give this cave its name. The return can be made initially through a passage that begins on the left once the gour has been left behind.

Of note are the botryoidal speleothems, gypsum crystallizations mainly in the deepest part of the cave, as well as its beautiful gour mentioned above which is usually dry and in which one can see the marks of the maximum water level reached, not to mention some interesting curtains and a beautiful baroque column, as well as all types of flowstone, whether parietal, ceiling, or pavement. One must look carefully in every corner of the cave, no matter how small, as they will provide more than one pleasant surprise.

In summary, we find ourselves in a cave that is located in the upper left corner next to three others that form a square and which are possibly the result of a single cave destroyed by quarry operations.

name: Cova des Migdia
starting_point: From the Ses Barques viewpoint, follow the signposted path towards Sa Costera, leaving it at Balitx d'Amunt. Continue parallel to the property wall to the Tanca des Bous crossroads, then straight ahead until the path ends at a well. Take the wide track to the right for 40-50 m to a dry stone wall on the left, follow it to its end, then continue parallel to the cliff veering right to a pine-surrounded clearing.
equipment: None essential, though for safety a helmet is recommended
description: A large single-chamber cave on a pine-surrounded clearing near Sóller, similar to Avenc de Son Pou. Features a massive entrance with stone stairs for comfortable access, weathered formations, and fig trees growing inside from pigeon droppings. The cave is classified as a site of special historical-archaeological interest and can be combined with an ascent of Puig de Balitx.
full_description:
The Cova des Migdia or des Coloms is located on a flat area where it is easy to walk, surrounded by pines, precisely in a place where rocky terrain with large cliffs and very rugged ground abounds.

If you already know the Avenc de Son Pou, this cave is very similar to it, as it consists of a single large chamber with a large entrance oriented to the southwest, if I remember correctly at the time of writing these lines. Accessing the interior of the cave can be done comfortably, as stone stairs have been built for this purpose.

A large fig tree has grown inside the cavity next to the stairs, and another small one grows on the walls, possibly due to the droppings of pigeons that roost or have previously roosted there.

More or less in the center of the cavity, some interesting formations remain standing in this cave, although very eroded and covered with moss, as well as some flowstones here and there.

The cave is of very large dimensions and makes us feel very small and defenseless when we walk through its interior trying to locate some crawlway that would allow us to access another area, but the search is in vain since the entire cave is within sight.

A visit is highly recommended and can be complemented with the ascent of Puig de Balitx (this excursion is described on the Mallorca Verde website), and if we don't want to follow the website's description, we can do it by accessing the summit from the cavity, which can be done in a short time although with a steep climb.

The Cova des Migdia is classified as a natural site of special historical-archaeological interest.

name: Cova des Mular
starting_point: Cala de s'Estany d'en Mas. Take the path closest to the cliff until reaching the visible quarry, then descend to the lower cliff platform via the old quarry path
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A coastal cave with a small entrance (80x90 cm) that measures about 48m long by 10m wide. The proximity to the sea allows storm waves to flood the interior above 3 meters, leaving posidonia seaweed fixed to the walls. The cave features ornate parietal flowstone, numerous small gours near the entrance, and a carved trident symbol interpreted as a stonemason guild mark or religious ideogram.
full_description:
Finding the entrance to the cave is not exactly difficult, but if we are not accompanied by a GPS it will take us a while to locate the small opening of only approximately 80 x 90 cm oriented at 314° NW. Once inside, we find a first vestibular area approximately ten meters wide, separated from the second by the downward slope of the terrain. The approximate dimensions of the cave are 48 m long by about ten meters at the widest point and a height of 5.45 m at the highest point.

Once in the deepest part of the cave, the water marks immediately catch our attention. The proximity of the cave to the sea, as well as the orientation of the entrance, allows it to flood during heavy storms with water exceeding three meters, as evidenced by the large quantity of posidonia seaweed fixed to the walls, some of it already covered by calcite.

The other end of the chamber is heavily decorated with concretions, with an ornate parietal flowstone standing out. The ceiling in this area is also reasonably well decorated with concretions, deserving attention.

From here, the cave begins to narrow while penetrating inland, accompanied by large parietal flowstones that surround us as we walk contemplating this gallery across a soft floor of sand mixed with posidonia — remains from previous floodings. Before reaching the end of the gallery, on the left side and about four meters up, there is a small, highly decorated corner that can be accessed via two sorts of steps and a support rope that is looped double around a column. The cave ends two or three meters just behind a narrow passage between columns, this also being the narrowest point of the cave, which seems to end in a funnel shape.

According to J. A. Encinas, the opening is in the splash zone and the retouched lintel of the entrance shows signs of the artificial groove for fitting a vertical closing slab, whose purpose was to protect the cavern from such storm surges — either for the purpose of using it as a funerary chamber or to hide contraband merchandise. However, it should be added that it preserves endokarst paleolevels in the form of morphogenic corrosion notches. Currently there is no slab anywhere near the entrance.

In one spot on the cave ceiling during my last visit, I was able to observe an area of approximately one square meter with a series of tiny lights that give the appearance of being minuscule gold nuggets due to their brilliance and golden color. It is the same effect that water drops sometimes produce in some parts of caves, but except in the Cova des Pont — just before accessing the chamber where Lake Victoria is located — I had never encountered this phenomenon before.

Before I forget, I must highlight the large number of small and medium-sized rimstone pools (gours) near the cave entrance, which with a bit of luck — if the cave is visited in winter — may be full of water.

On the interior side of the entrance, a trident is engraved*, interpretable as an initiatory ideogram of the "Goose Foot" — characteristic of the fraternal guilds of stonemasons — or alternatively as a maypole. J. A. Encinas.

Since a visit to this cave is not enough for a full day, we just need to choose one of the nearby caves in the area.

* Each of the three external points appears topped with a "Fleur-de-lis" or, alternatively, with small extreme strokes forming crosses. In the first case, it would have heraldic implications, while in the second it would be representing the scene of Golgotha, with the depiction of Christ in the middle and the two convicts on the sides. In any case, these are not de-satanizing cruciform marks of an ancient pagan site.

name: Cova des Pas de Vallgornera
footwear: Cut and perforated rubber boots are ideal
clothing: Neoprene essential, plus dry clothes (shorts and t-shirt)
access: Own vehicle. Location coordinates cannot be disclosed.
equipment: Neoprene, fins, electric light (carbide not permitted). Waterproof container for dry clothes and food, canyoning backpack.
description: One of the world's longest caves with over 55km of mapped passages as of 2008, ranking 24th globally. Discovered in 1968 during construction at Hotel Es Pas, it features extraordinary formations including moonmilk, stunning flooded galleries, and the enormous Sala que no Té Nom. Access is strictly restricted under EU Red Natura 2000 (LIC ES5310049) and requires special permission.
full_description:
Due mainly to the dimensions of this cave — more than 55 km of development as of June 2008, the twenty-fourth longest in the world at that time — I cannot provide a classic description of it, since that would require first knowing all the explored areas of the cave, which is not the case. So what I will try to do is structure the information by giving a brief overview from the moment the cave was discovered within the Hotel des Pas to the present day, in which people continue to work on it altruistically.

Brief History of a Discovery

April 26, 1968. Tomeu Covas was building a septic tank with a pneumatic hammer — the first compressor brought to Mallorca by the Dutch owners of the Hotel Es Pas — for the hotel's wastewater, when at a few meters depth the hammer slipped through, subsequently bleeding the hole and revealing the cave.

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera must also have or have had a natural entrance, since fossilized bat specimens have been found inside.

The First Exploration

April 17, 2008. Miquel Font Oliver, accompanied by photographer Magín Ciar, enters the cave and carries out a first exploration during which they take some photographs. Subsequently, new explorations were carried out, concluding in 1992. In July 1994, M.A. Barceló and P. Riera discover a passage to new galleries that give rise to successive explorations that have not yet been completed to this day.

What Could Have Been and Was Not

The hotel owner, with the idea of opening the cave to the public, shortly after its discovery hired two Belgian speleologists to conduct a feasibility study that gave a negative result. Thanks to this, today the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is protected, after having been subject for a long time to acts of vandalism in part. I must also say that due to the level of protection of the cave, it cannot be visited freely and can only be done with a special permit.

Antic Sector and the New Extensions

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera can be divided into two sectors: the Sector Antic (old sector) and the Noves Extensions (new extensions) (MERINO, 1993; 2000). In the Antic sector, topographic work was carried out from 1990 to 1992, when it was considered completed (MERINO, 1993). The development up to that point was approximately 2 km.

In 1994, Miquel Àngel Barceló and Pere Riera discover access to new galleries and chambers of the cave (Noves Extensions) and make important findings that determine that by the end of 1999 the cavity reaches a total development of 6,435 m (MERINO, 2000).

From 2002, members of the Grup Espeleòlogic Llubí and the Speleology Section of ANEM focus on reviewing specific places by investigating the existence of an air current. For two years they worked until they found a place where the air current disappeared, beginning clearing work to access a group of galleries and narrow passages between blocks that seemed to have no continuation, until Guiem Mulet, after spending several hours working among them, discovers the Sala que no Té Nom (The Room With No Name), an impressive chamber of approximately two hundred and fifty meters in length that gave way to an endless number of new galleries where work continues to this day.

Mallorca Verde Visits the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera

On June 21, 2008, I was invited by Guiem Mulet to visit the cave, which I gladly accepted as I had long wanted to see it. The group that formed that day consisted of Toni Mulet, Toni Merino, Toni Croix, Manolo Luque, Jordi Mulet, Miquel Artigues, Guiem Mulet, and myself. Later, in the Sala que no Té Nom, the group split in two, with Toni Mulet, Toni Merino, and Toni Croix continuing toward the north sector where they are exploring and surveying the area, while the other part of the group followed a different path.

As I was saying, the full group first accessed the Sala que no Té Nom, where the base camp is set up. To do this, once in the entrance room or Sala de les Arrels (Room of the Roots), we walked for a few minutes and then swam for between thirty and forty minutes through flooded galleries of indescribable beauty, notably the Llac de na Gemma (Gemma's Lake). Once the groups separated in the Sala que no Té Nom, we approached the recently discovered Sala Blanca (White Room), which very few people had visited at that point, to deliver an emergency first aid kit. To reach it, we again had to put on our neoprene suits and swim through narrow meanders, passing through the Llac Quadrat (Square Lake) until reaching the junction with the Galeria del Quilòmetre (Kilometer Gallery) from which it is accessed.

Back at the Sala que no Té Nom, we dedicated ourselves to visiting and photographing some of its beautiful corners. There are many things to see in this gigantic room, notably the substance moonmilk (photo 5), in a part of the room that I had never seen before. It is a white deposit formed by very fine crystals that, when wet, form a texture similar to cream cheese, and when dry, becomes powdery. Generally, moonmilk is made up of carbonate materials, for example, calcite, gypsum, and others. The substance moonmilk owes its name to a Swiss cave called Höhle Mondmilchloch (Moon Milk Cavern). Beautiful columns and thin, long stalactites are not lacking in the Sala que no Té Nom.

Subsequently, we went to make my first contact with the Galeria dels Espeleotemes (Gallery of Speleothems), which we reached after descending a ramp of approximately ten meters with the help of a rope installed there. Afterward, another rope installed as a handrail also facilitated walking until reaching the gallery, where we fired our cameras one last time before ending the visit to the cave after almost nine hours.

It is unforgivable to enter the cave without photographic equipment; the only problem is getting it into the cavity. I must thank Manolo Luque, who lent me a mini tripod that I was able to manage with, since the tripod that was waiting for me at the base camp could not be found.

For my part, I thank Guiem Mulet for allowing me to accompany them on a day I will never forget, and which I hope will not be the last.

Level of Protection of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance with number LIC ES5310049 of the Natura 2000 Network (Dir. 92/43 EEC).

The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera is considered a site of natural biological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventory of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

Further Reading

BULLETIN No. 3 SEDECK / YEAR 2002 / SPANISH SOCIETY OF SPELEOLOGY AND KARST SCIENCES.

Official page of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera: http://covavallgornera.org/

ANTONIO MERINO. Some unusual speleothems found in the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera. New observations. Endins, 31, 111-116. Palma de Mallorca.

ANTONI MERINO, ANTONI MULET, GUIEM MULET, ANTONI CROIX, and FRANCESC GRÀCIA. The Cova des Pas de Vallgornera (Llucmajor, Mallorca): 40 kilometers of topographic development. Endins, 31, 101-110. Palma de Mallorca.

name: Cova des Ponent
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A sizable cave at the base of a cliff near relay antennas, historically used as a shepherd's shelter. The main chamber (20x13.5 m) retains evidence of habitation including fire remains and ceramic fragments. A deeper, well-decorated room features columns, flowstones, and various formations, though many have been damaged over time. The cave still serves as shelter for feral goats and bats.
full_description:
Near the relay antennas and at the base of the cliff, this cavity of considerable dimensions is found — a shelter for shepherds in another era, as all the evidence suggests. Among other things: the place where the fire was made near the entrance, whose smoke residues are impregnated on the walls; ceramic remains even in the deepest areas indicate how they took advantage of the water that penetrated the cavity — a rather scarce resource throughout the entire sierra and much more so in the summer season.

Description

Just after crossing the entrance of slightly more than one meter in height, we access the largest room of the cavity, with dimensions of 20 x 13.5 m and a height that "brushes" 5 m. Remains of a stone wall built in its day to protect against the inclemencies of the weather separate the cave from the exterior, and a little further inside to the north, one can still appreciate both on the floor and on the walls the remains of the fire that the shepherds once used, both for cooking and for warming themselves, and incidentally allowing the smoke not to invade the entire room. Advancing a bit further, a column shaped like a "Y" attached to the wall can be seen, the result of water filtration through a crack. If we continue advancing along this side of the room, we will begin to ascend gently until reaching three meters on a mound of earth and stones, the result of successive collapses along the eastern area. If we descend heading toward the southern area of the room, we will encounter the most decorated area, with parietal flowstones standing out. The continuation is made by passing over the one-meter-high step that leads into an ornate gallery of approximately five meters in length, which at some points does not exceed fifty centimeters, with variable width. Once past it, we can stand up and access a small room to the south, located at 1.5 m height, circular in shape with an approximate diameter of five meters and a height that does not exceed one meter. On our return, we will continue our way, leaving to our left a small room of 4 x 3.5 m that can only be accessed by crawling. A little further to the west, we will descend into a very decorated room of 10 x 6 m with all types of formations, from small columns to floor, ceiling, and wall flowstones.

The condition of the formations in this cavity is not too good. The mistreatment they have suffered is noticeable everywhere — in some cases to make the cavity more habitable and in others possibly to trade in them, something that occurred frequently in other eras.

In summary, this is a cave that is worth visiting, that served — like many others in the sierra in which it is located — to provide shelter and supply water to the shepherds who in other times traversed these landscapes. Currently it is not very active and continues to serve as shelter for the feral goats of the area.

Fauna

During the two visits made to the cave, I was able to observe some unidentified specimens of chiroptera or bats.

The "Cova des Ponent" can be considered of speleological interest as well as biological, archaeological, and paleontological interest.

name: Cova des Pont
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma-4014 road from Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, follow the path through a chained metal gate to Cala Varques, then coast to Cala Falcó and take the track to the cave
clothing: Depends on the time of year
equipment: Neoprene essential to visit the entire cavity
description: Documented since 1884, this cave was connected to the neighboring Cova des Pirata via cave diving in 1998-89, forming a combined 2,105m system. Accessed through a large entrance (~14m diameter) via a stone bridge built during a failed commercial venture, the cave features a grand chamber about 100m long with crystal-clear lakes, impressive columns, and the gated Lake Victoria with its soda straw stalactites reflected in the water. The cave is on private land and requires owner permission to visit.
full_description:
The Cova des Pont, along with the Cova des Pirata, have been documented since 1884, being the first caves in Mallorca to be connected through cave diving by the Welsh group Cwmbran Caving Club (CCC) during the years 1998 and 89. The connection was made after forcing a narrow passage that connected the two caves, increasing the total length to 2,105 m. The two caves are part of the same endokarst system, where collapse processes due to gravitational readjustments of the vaults and walls have caused the separation of both caves by land.

The mouth of the Cova des Pont is of considerable dimensions, approximately 14 meters in diameter, and is concealed by vegetation so that one can walk past it on the path without noticing it. To access it, one must get past a gate locked tight with several padlocks and begin descending via the steps of a stone bridge built when there was an attempt to commercially exploit it by opening it to the public, which gives the cavity its name. The descent is made, as always, among branches of fig trees, a tree present in multiple cavities in the area such as: the Cova de sa Figuera, d'en Bessó, Secret, de sa Figuera, etc.

Once we find ourselves on the first landing, having crossed the bridge and descended a few meters (more or less below the center of the entrance hole to the cave), we will begin the visit to the cavity throughout the non-flooded part of the cave, since to visit it in its entirety we must use wetsuits. We will begin the visit from left to right, starting from the west. To access the first chamber, we will pass behind some ghostly formations, crossing them on the right side and descending until reaching the lateral wall of the cave to find an easy passage and thus avoid the risk of a possible fall. Subsequently, we will need to gain height through a ramp only to descend again until reaching the lake, and then conclude the visit to this gallery, having to return once more to the starting point.

Once back at the landing, we will continue visiting the cave. To proceed, we will descend via the stairs where the steps soon disappear, turning into an earthen path. Then we need to turn slightly to the left and we will immediately access the "great chamber," where if we move along the left side we will soon reach a beautiful lake called the "Carbonate Lake," which, like Lake Victoria, has a sort of natural pier. Noteworthy in this great chamber are at least three columns that are isolated and distributed throughout it — one of them is completely white and another that immediately catches the eye is broken a little above the midpoint. This process is called solifluction, which can cause a column to separate both vertically and horizontally. Also interesting is the topic of light, as from almost any point in the chamber the entrance of natural light can be seen.

We can return to the path to continue our exploration, following its course through a small rise and then beginning the descent while immediately observing an impressive gallery featuring a great chaos of stones piled on top of each other. The dimensions of this chamber are about 100 m in length by about 40 m at the widest point, through which we must move carefully as the ground tends to be very muddy in some spots and there is the possibility of getting hurt. On the right side of the chamber, we will find a first small lake surrounded by concretions forming a charming corner. A little further on, at the end of the chamber on this side, we reach a much larger lake with crystal-clear water. At the back of the chamber, there is a low passage of about 40 cm in height which, once crossed, deposits us in a small chamber with a lake that in turn connects the Cova des Pont with the Cova des Pirata. After leaving this small chamber and returning to the previous one, we can appreciate the backlit effects that make this spot a good place for taking interesting photos using the natural light entering the chamber. As we exit, if we approach the walls on the right side, we will see how it is possible to find water forming a small lake.

Once back, we can continue the visit by accessing Lake Victoria, following the path again and then leaving it via stairs carved into the rock whose first steps are very deteriorated, so care must be taken not to slip on them. Lake Victoria is found behind an oxidized iron bar door closed with an old latch. Every time I cross the door, I wonder what the reason was for isolating this chamber from the rest of the cave by building a partition wall with a window and the access door itself. Noteworthy in this chamber, besides the lake, are a sort of pier that extends into it for several meters and a fairly well-decorated ceiling where soda straw stalactites accumulate, which in turn are reflected in the water when the lake is illuminated.

With the visit to the chamber containing Lake Victoria, we conclude the visit to the cave, so all that remains is to retrace our steps along the path toward the exit.

Right below the entrance stairs, one can access a small chamber where multiple earth collapses can be seen, and which connects to the first chamber described here. This visit can be omitted if desired, as it offers no further interest after everything already seen.

Both the Cova des Pont and the Cova des Pirata and the Cova des Xots are on private land, so permission must be requested from the owners to visit them.

For further reading:

MALLORAL, D. and MATEU, T. 2006: Mallorca, Beauties in the Darkness... Ed. Espelo Mallorca, S. C. Sant Llorenç des Cardassar.

GARCIA, J.; DELGADO, X.; FERRERES, J. 1986: Collection of Cavities of the Island of Mallorca. Exploracions, 10. Barcelona.

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): The caves of the Can Frasquet - Cala Varques area. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.

name: Cova des Puig del Rei
starting_point: Coll des Cocons, Km 2.200 of the MA-1015 road (Palma Nova - Calvià)
equipment: Helmet and lighting
description: A multi-chamber cave system with evidence of prehistoric, Arab, and modern human use, including ceramics, lithic tools, and marine shells. Features numerous interconnected rooms — the Stalactite Room, Broken Stalagmite Room, Gypsum Room, and others — connected by tight crawlways. A notable gypsum block was found in one chamber, and the cave displays diverse pink, green, and gray coloring from the underlying carniola and dolomite bedrock.
full_description:
Description

Sala de S'Entrada (Entrance Room)

Once inside, we find ourselves in an alveolar collapse chamber, this being the one with the greatest development in the cave, with dimensions of 5 x 12 x 2.5 m. The room, as mentioned earlier, was adapted in modern times. The reconstructive processes that are so abundant in other areas of the cave are practically nonexistent in this location.

Galeria Baixa (Lower Gallery)

An L-shaped gallery of 2 x 8 m with a low ceiling that narrows until it becomes impassable in the final section. Sparse in formations, it may have been used as a burial place, as its floor is abundant with remains of rosemary and other aromatic plants.

Rampa de Sa Ceràmica (Ceramics Ramp)

This is a ramp with approximately 30% incline that connects the Sala de S'Entrada with the lower galleries (figure 6C). It is composed of the accumulation of stones and ceramic remains thrown from the Sala de S'Entrada when it was adapted, and in fact the access had to be unblocked.

In this location, prehistoric, Arab, and more modern ceramics have been found (figure 5 A,D). Some lithic tools obtained from the coast have also been found (figure 5B), as well as shells of several Gibbula, gastropod mollusks of the family Trochidae (figure 5C).

Pas de Sa Pols (Dust Passage)

In the deepest part of the Rampa de Sa Ceràmica, on the south side, this arduous and dusty crawlway of 4 m in length is found, which in some places does not reach 0.40 m in height, and which gives continuity to the cave, connecting with the Sala de Ses Estalactites.

Sala de Ses Estalactites (Stalactite Room)

Finding this room of 5.5 x 6.5 x 2.5 m with a N-S orientation was a true surprise, as we did not expect the cave to continue since it did not appear on the existing survey.

The presence of two stalactites approximately one meter in length, placed on the ground one next to the other at the entrance of this room — which, although not devoid of reconstructive processes, lacks this variety of speleothems — allowed us to harbor the hope that the cave would increase its development.

Saleta dets Ossos (Bone Chamber)

Just upon entering the Sala de ses Estalactites, on the right side, a narrow hole allows us to descend to this small room of 3 x 4 m lacking further interest were it not for the bones found, possibly from a goat specimen that reached this place and could not get out.

Sala dets Forats (Room of the Holes)

At the back of the Sala de Ses Estalactites, on the right side, another narrow hole allows us to descend to the Sala dets Forats. It is a room with a N-S orientation and dimensions of 3 x 5 x 2 m.

We are in an area of the cave where reconstructive processes begin to be abundant.

From this room, the rest of the cave can be accessed either through the Pas Llarg (Long Passage) or through the Pas de Sa Finestra (Window Passage).

The first of these is found in the lower left part of the room and consists of a crawlway of 8 m in length. The extreme hardness of the floor, the presence of low formations, and the limitations of movement make this passage an ordeal to be avoided.

The second is found on the right side of the room and consists of a somewhat elevated crawlway, more than 2.0 m in length and of scant height, guarded by some thin columns, which connects to the Sala de S'Estalagmita Trencada.

Sala de s'Estalagmita Trencada (Broken Stalagmite Room)

Once past the Pas de sa Finestra, we enter one of the rooms with the greatest development (figure 7) of the cave, with dimensions of 5 x 7 x 2 m and a NE-SE orientation. In this room, reconstructive processes are abundant, and have given rise to a "forest" of stalagmites.

In the northwest area of the room, the existence of a sinkhole stands out.

Sala des Gour Cremat (Burned Rimstone Pool Room)

After passing a small passage where human alteration is evident — a cut formation can already be observed — and after a brief downclimb, we find ourselves in the Sala des Gour Cremat.

It is a room with a N-S orientation, with dimensions of 3.5 x 6.5 x 2 m, so named because of the rimstone pool (gour) it houses in the SW area, which was partially hidden behind formations that were sectioned. Inside the rimstone pool, fragments of the chipped rock pieces are still found, as well as remains of a campfire and pieces of charcoal.

The existence of some eccentrics of unquestionable beauty stands out, and on its west wall, a small opening where gypsum blocks of the Sala des Guix can be observed below.

Sala des Guix (Gypsum Room)

A room of 4.5 x 2.5 x 2.5 m. A pleasant surprise for us was finding a block of gypsum (guix) of considerable size without calcic coating, isolated in the center of the room, in which two transversally cut tuyeres can be appreciated (figure 6A), and which gave it its name. It is possibly the remains of a much larger block that, after a dissolution process, could have caused the formation of the room.

Sala des Plat (Plate Room)

This room of 2.5 x 3 x 1 m houses within it a large number of speleothems, among them a rimstone pool that occupies a great part of the floor, where a small plate can still be observed. Access to the room can be made through a small window from the Sala des Guix and from the Sala de Sa Copa.

Sala de Sa Copa (Cup Room)

Located in the most distant area, above the Sala des Guix, it can be accessed from said room or from the Sala des Plat. Its dimensions are 3 x 4 m. A column fragment shaped like a cup found in it gave rise to its name.

The pink, green, and gray colors of its ceiling stand out, due to the outcrops of the bedrock (carniolas and dolomites) in which the cavity has developed.

Sala de s'Escletxa (Crack Room)

The exit to the outside can be made through this room of 4.5 x 6 m, taking the NE zone of the Sala des Guix as a starting point. The room is characterized mainly by a central crack more than 4 m in length, from whose interior the arduous Pas Llarg can be accessed.

name: Cova des Xots
starting_point: At km 9 of the Ma-4014 road from Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, there is a parking area for vehicles
equipment: A small rope may be needed to reach the deepest area
description: Accessed via a four-step ladder, the cave opens into a large mostly horizontal chamber with wet, slippery floors. The deepest section contains a murky pool at -26m that acts as a sinkhole for the entire doline. Part of the same endokarst system as Cova des Pont and Cova des Pirata, the cave features interesting columns and numerous stalactites, though it is smaller and less decorated than its neighboring caves.
full_description:
Access to the cave is made by descending a four-step ladder fixed to the wall. The actual cave entrance measures 10 by 1.30 m with an orientation of 1° N. Once inside the cavity, a large chamber opens before us, mostly horizontal with some divisions. The cave floor is covered with dry stones near the entrance, but as we go deeper they begin to be wet and slippery.
