If we take a more or less straight direction through the widest part of the cavity, which runs along the left side as we advance, we can observe on the right side a ledge at a height of approximately one meter and fifty centimeters that accompanies us until we reach a point where the cave loses its horizontality and begins to descend abruptly but passably, provided one proceeds with great care and has some experience traversing this type of terrain.

To visit the ledge area mentioned above, we can return toward the cave exit and ascend a small slope to reach the height of the ledge, which we can access and move along on top of. Through this area, we will also reach the aforementioned slope where we can observe a column with a shape that is at least different from any I have seen to this day.

In the deepest part of the cave there is a sort of pool that can be accessed using a rope to avoid a possible slip, as the floor is always wet and slippery. I must say that the Cova des Xots serves as a sinkhole for the entire doline in which it is located, and water accompanied by branches usually enters it, as evidenced by the tree trunks — some of considerable size — that can be observed throughout the cave. At the bottom of the lower chamber, a sort of funnel that can be accessed deposits us right in the aforementioned pool of murky water, 1.50 m deep, according to M. Trias (1977).

The cave is well decorated with some very interesting columns as well as a considerable number of stalactites, although it is much smaller and perhaps less beautiful than its sisters Cova des Pont and Cova des Pirata, which are located nearby. Nevertheless, it is worth visiting.

The Cova des Xots, as well as the Cova des Pirata and Cova des Pont and others in the area, are part of the same endokarst system, where collapse processes due to gravitational readjustments of the vaults and walls have caused the separation, on a human scale, between the caves. Therefore, nearby cavities in many cases may form or may have formed in the past part of the same subterranean network, often becoming isolated from the rest by collapse processes.

The Cova des Xots is also known in the island's speleological community by the erroneous name of Cova des Genet, as that is not its true name. The Cova des Genet is in the area but had been forgotten until September 7, 2008, when it was explored by Mallorca Verde, J. A. Encinas, M. L. Redondo, and A. Villar, and identified thanks to the information provided in the book by C. Veny about the cave listings from J. Aguiló's cave archive. All this despite it being one of the most beautiful caves in the area, reaching a magnitude III with a concretion level of almost one hundred percent among many other attractions, including some helictite stalactites as well as some "vegetal stalagmites."

name: Cova del Estudiant
starting_point: As this is a ZEC (Special Conservation Zone) cavity, it can only be visited with a permit from the Conselleria Medi Ambient.
distance: 220 m approximately from Can Gomila house
access: Private vehicle, or the classic Sóller train from Palma
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A formerly tourist-exploited cave near Sóller, now a ZEC protected site requiring a permit. The cave features abundant speleothems including flowstone, columns, large stalagmites, and eccentric stalactites, along with remnants of its tourist era such as stairs and electrical wiring. It contains two underground lakes connected by siphons explored by cave divers. Commercially operated before the Spanish Civil War, it was later sealed by its owner.
full_description:
Description

Once commercially exploited for tourism, today all that remains from that era are the conditioning works carried out in its interior, visible mainly in the large number of stairs that had to be built. During the visit, the remains of the cables from the electrical installation are still visible, as well as the occasional fuse box. The tourist exploitation of the cave began before the Spanish Civil War, being abandoned afterwards. The entrance was sealed by its owner, and it remained so until well into the nineteen-seventies (Martí Romero, 1975).

Until not long ago, the entrance to the cave was closed by a small metal door one meter high by sixty centimeters wide, with a window above the door of forty-five by eighty centimeters kept open so that it could be visited. Previously, there is a natural doorway of two by one point ten meters.

As soon as we enter the cavity, which is oriented E-W, we find a kind of vestibule about seven meters long in which the ruins of a round table surrounded by a bench can be found, which was surely used to accommodate the cave's visitors before the start of the tour. The visit began by going up some stairs to reach a small landing, and then continuing to gain height by another small flight of stairs before beginning the descent to a small ledge where we can visit the first lake, which is also a siphon. In the 1980s, Mallorca began to be frequented by German and Czech cave divers. Meanwhile, immersions were also being carried out by Mallorcans in different cavities on the island; thus Miquel Garau of the Grup Espeleològic EST explored in 1974 40 meters of siphon 2 of the cave, which is located at the farthest end of the cave (Francesc Gracia, 2002). This first lake-siphon is the shallowest as well as the smallest. We will return again to the main gallery using an alternative path, since two paths were set up in the cave to access the lake, so as not to repeat the return route.

In this part of the cave is where the greatest lithochemical concretioning can be observed, so we will walk surrounded by all types of formations until reaching a new fork. If we continue to the right, our path will follow stairs with small steps that point towards the ceiling of the cavity at a very steep angle, although quite well preserved. These stairs were built to bring visitors closer to a beautiful corner where the cave's concretioning is in good condition, with parietal flowstones of different colors, columns, enormous stalagmites, as well as a good number of eccentric stalactites standing out.

Back on the main gallery, we will continue the visit to the cave surrounded by great concretioning until reaching the second lake of the cave, where we will end the visit unless we are equipped with the necessary gear for cave diving, in which case, once the siphon is passed, a terrestrial section separates us from another small lake where the cave continues. I do not know if at any point the cave was considered to have been fully explored.

NOTE: The Cova del Estudiante is one of the 30 ZEC (Special Conservation Zone) cavities that currently exist on the island of Mallorca. For this reason, access is prohibited without the corresponding permit from the Conselleria Medi Ambient.

name: Mina la Nacional
starting_point: Drive to Pas de Son Cabastre, then walk parallel to the GR 221 towards Coll de sa Basseta, then descend through Coma d'en Llobera to Comellar de Malagate.
approach_time: Approximately 1 hour from Comellar de Malagate
access: From Palma by own vehicle, or bus to Esporles
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: An abandoned mining complex near Esporles, discovered by chance in 2008. The mine operated from 1892 to at least 1920, carved from a natural cave with large chambers supported by natural and one artificial pillar. Due to ongoing collapses and unstable geology (gypsum content), entering is dangerous and requires extreme caution.
full_description:
Access

We find ourselves before a mining complex located near Esporles, forgotten in time despite the interest it surely held in its era. The mine is located in the Comellar d'en Malagate, specifically in Ses Rotes des Pinar de Canet and near the houses of Ses Planes, currently converted into a horse breeding farm. It would be easy to access the mine through this estate, but it is not possible as it is private property, so the approach route must be done following the GR 221 from Esporles to the Coll de sa Basseta, named after the small bassa (pool) found behind a wire fence next to the GR-221 signpost, and from there begin the descent through the Coma d'en Llobera, from the Catalan Llop, accompanied on our left by the Torrent des Pinar de Canet. We can also access the location by taking the turnoff to the SANGRILA development, which is opposite the entrance to the well-known George Sand development on the Valldemossa to Banyalbufar road.

Location of the Complex

As often happens in these cases, only a stroke of luck has brought this mining complex to light, as a mountain cyclist F. Bonnin found it and contacted me to inform me. Once my curiosity was piqued, we arranged a day to make an excursion to the area which allowed me a quick visit during which at first glance I could verify for myself how several trenches were semi-hidden by undergrowth, in addition to the entrance mouth to one of the galleries. With a quick visit to what would later be called the "lower gallery," I concluded the visit on December 31, 2008.

Some History

Perhaps the most difficult part has been searching for information about this place, since not even the Esporles town council, where the mining complex is located, could provide any, having total ignorance of the existence of any mine in their territory. To obtain any reliable information, I had to resort to interviewing elderly people born in Esporles, as some of them still preserved in their memory some recollection of the place, despite the fact that the exploitation period may have caught them very young or some had simply not yet been born. This allowed me, among other things, to clarify the name of the site where the mine and quarry are located, since current maps, possibly due to not studying the subject in depth, mark the place as the "Coma d'en Llobera," which is very close, instead of the "Comellar de Malagate," which is its real name. I was able to find the name of the complex in Joan Miralles i Monserrat's book "Studis d'Onomàstica," which in turn referenced another book by Onofre Rullan Salamanca, "La toponímia del terme municipal d'Esporles i la seva rodalia," Palma 1984. In fact, the name of the Serra de Malagate where the mine is located indicates its name, as Malagate in Spanish is Malacate (from the Mexican malacatl, spindle, spinning thing). n. A machine similar to a capstan driven by horses. It is widely used in mines to extract minerals and water. II Hond. and Mex. Spindle for spinning. Specifically, the area where the complex is located is called "Ses Rotes Velles"; if we look up the Catalan word "rotes" in a dictionary, we find "rotatiu - adj 1 MEC That originates a rotation." This possibly refers to the aforementioned machine or alternatively to the tafona (olive oil mill), a mill where olives were ground and which, as I explain a bit further below, was located at this site prior to the mining exploration. The adjective "Velles" (old) possibly indicates that the agricultural exploitation of the area preceded the mining one.

Carts on a track transported the minerals, taking advantage of the slope of the terrain towards the kiln located at the now-closed cement factory. The return of the carts to the mine was apparently, though not confirmed, done by dragging them using animal power.

Facilities

The inventory of facilities, at least what I have been able to locate, consists of a house or large building at 466437 E, 4391782 N, 202 m, of which only two of the four walls remain standing along with some interior divisions. Initially, I thought it was part of the mine, but an investigation of the site by J. A. Encinas, who found a pressing stone dated 1835, seems to confirm that the building was nothing other than a tafona (olive oil mill) that was abandoned possibly at the beginning of the mining work, along with the local crops that were irrigated with water stored in a pond next to the building, which was filled by diverting the Torrent des Pinar de Canet.

Next to the path there is also a small hut at 466515 E, 4391784 N, 193 m, in perfect condition that houses a water well inside. A modern pulley hanging from the roof suggests it is still being used or at least was until not long ago. A cabin at 466619 E, 4391736 N, in perfect state of conservation, with a chimney and 3 bales of straw inside — I don't know if they served as a mattress at some point, but certainly they do not date back to the mining era. Lost among the undergrowth is a well at 466330 E, 4391750 N, 217 m, full of water, enduring the passage of time as best it can.

Everything indicates that before the mining exploitation, these lands were worked agriculturally, as evidenced by the remaining olive grove where some carob trees are also preserved, as well as the aforementioned tafona and the well that never fit the mining context. Once the mine began operating, everything was abandoned until the present day.

Map of the mining complex J. Bermejo.

Beginning of Exploration and Topographic Work

On January 25, 2009, accompanied by J. A. Encinas, M. L. Redondo, G. Herráez, and the experienced miner J. A. Pérez, we set out to begin the exploration, topography, and photography work of the mine, starting with the "upper gallery." After the first day of work, we had managed to finish the first part of the topography in addition to carrying out a complete photography project and locating the rest of the facilities that formed part of this mining complex, leaving the remaining work for subsequent visits.

For safety reasons and to avoid unwanted accidents, everything suggests that when the mine's exploitation work ceased to be profitable, some of the entrances were blown up with explosives, burying them until, over time, the work of water opened them up again.

Description of the Mine

North Zone of the Underground Complex

If we enter the interior of the mining cave through the most northeastern (NE) entrance near the clearing, the first thing to highlight is the collapse that has occurred at the entrance, where a large part of the facade has collapsed while concealing the entrance that was clearly visible when the topography was surveyed in 2009. Once inside, a beautiful stratum of dark lead-colored mineral of great thickness stands out on the south wall, as well as the collapses that accumulate a large quantity of clasts on the ground throughout this area. In practically the entire ceiling zone of this part of the mine, the strata have begun to separate from each other, threatening imminent collapses.

Currently, this zone of the mining cave is isolated from the rest of the underground complex, despite the fact that in 2009 a passage was cleared in the southwest zone — now collapsed again — which first connected to a small chamber and from there to the rest of the mining cavity.

Photo 1. Concretions in the South gallery. Photo J. Bermejo 2009.

South Zone of the Underground Complex

Another opening similar to that in the north zone is located to the southeast of the underground mining complex. Once inside, the dimensions of this place are impressive, formed by a single chamber whose ceiling is supported by thick natural columns along with one artificial column.

Here, if you look closely, during the mining exploitation work, sections were left unexcavated, thus creating natural columns to prevent the ceiling from collapsing, and in this place an artificial one was built to prevent a possible collapse. Currently, it can be seen that the column is cracking under the pressure exerted upon it.

The date and the name of the builder were engraved in the mortar of the column. These data have helped us to know at least until what dates the mine was active. From 1892 when the mining work began, work continued at least until 1920.

The entire floor of this zone of the underground complex is covered by the strata that the miners detached from the ceiling by exploiting the stratigraphic joints, in addition to the collapses that have occurred over time since the mine was abandoned. On a fragment of considerable size, the marks left by the tools used to detach them can be seen.

In the northwest zone of the chamber is the only place where lithochemical processes abound in the form of curtains, dog teeth, and active flowstones, as well as some mini gours, all of them in permanent activity. Also in the north zone, two small pools are located, one near the other, both crafted by the hands of the miners taking advantage of the water table in the area. Near them, delicate speleothems can be seen both in the ceiling zone and around the floor.

A cone caused by collapses is located in the north zone of the area that connected to the south zone after its clearance in 2009; currently the passage has closed again.

Pitfall

We are not certain whether the pit located above the central zone of the underground complex was at some point part of it, or if it is the result of a subsidence process.

While we were working on the topographic survey of the mine in 2009, a ceiling collapse connected the pit with the exterior just a few meters from the entrance, creating a new access point.

In the pitfall we were able to observe small pieces of amber attached to the roots that formed them, which in turn were adhered to one of the large stone blocks that had fallen from the ceiling. See photo 2.

Photo 2. "Amber" found inside the pitfall of the Cova Minera de sa Malagata. Photo J. Bermejo 2009.

Hidden Facilities

In addition to everything described in this work, two covered wells remain to be explored. The first is located a little above the entrance to the upper gallery, coordinates 466477 E, 4391663 N, 214 m. The other well is in the upper part and not far from the pitfall entrances, where the marks of drill holes that helped form it can be seen. It is very possible that these wells had no relation to the mining cave discussed here and are waiting for someone someday to bring them to light.

Summary

We find ourselves before a mine with an unusual design on the island, as the exploitation may have begun from a natural cave, so it does not consist of narrow galleries as is customary. The mine was in its day composed of a single large chamber shored up by natural columns of the terrain itself that were left unquarried and a single artificial shoring column where an inscription indicates that in 1920 it was in operation. Four entrances gave access to its interior; today, on the contrary, the mine is divided into four chambers plus the pitfall that has been generated due to terrain readjustments.

It is worth reemphasizing the danger of entering the interior due to the possibility of a collapse at any moment. This can be appreciated as soon as one enters any of the two main galleries or the pitfall, where large stone blocks pile up on the floor while others threaten to do the same. If we finally decide to do it, follow these recommendations: do not touch the walls or the ceiling, eliminate unnecessary noise as much as possible, try not to move stones from their place, and do not forget to notify someone of our visit to the place.

Minerals

The global analysis carried out on the mineral sample extracted from the interior of the cavity has shown that dolomitized calcite predominates at almost 80%, followed by quartz at 13%, and in lesser proportions clay and gypsum, the latter possibly being the cause of the cave's instability.

Photo 3. Some of the colorful minerals that can be found in the South gallery. Photo J. Bermejo 2009.

Fauna

A specimen of Pipistrellus pipistrellus, which is the smallest bat found in Europe, was found in the lower mine. To give an idea of its dimensions, the forearm measures between 28 and 33.5mm, with hairs that are blackish at the base and chestnut-colored at the tips, having short, small ears. This species feeds mainly on flying insects and mosquitoes. Notable is an abundant number of spiders, black and shiny on both the cephalothorax and extremities, with the rest of the body being a brownish color lacking shine. Two specimens of slugs were found near the entrance of the old mouth of the lower mine, in addition to some spider nests that were adhered to a thin root hanging from the ceiling near the same entrance.

Photo 5. Slug and bat found in the North gallery. Photo J. Bermejo 2009.

Period of Exploitation of the Mining Cave

The mining work began at the end of 1892, and it is certified on the artificial shoring column inside the upper gallery that the mine was being exploited in 1920, with the possibility that this year was the last in which work was done, since precisely in that year mining exploitations on the island began to be abandoned.

Main Excavation

466438 E, 4391830 N (coordinates taken at the start of the trench). DATUM EUROPEAN 1950. 193 m.

Covered by undergrowth and mature pine trees, near the old tafona, there is an old quarry, more or less circular with a diameter of about 100 m, which is accessed through a trench that begins at the end of a narrow path that starts about twenty meters from the well next to the path covered by the hut. In the book "Estudis d'onomàstica" by Joan Miralles i Monserrat, Josep Morán, published by L'Abadia de Montserrat, 2003, this quarry is referred to by the name Pedreres de Malagata, which is reasonable since it is located in the Comellar d'en Malagata, yet no mention is made of the mining cave. From this location, the extracted material was transported to the now-closed cement factory via the previously mentioned carts. What I have no record of is whether work was carried out simultaneously at both locations or not, since it should not be forgotten that this open-air quarry may have previously been a natural cave, as in other cases — "Sa Pedrera Grossa" at 465550 E, 4384178 N DATUM EUROPEAN 1950 in Palma comes to mind.

name: Cova Morella
starting_point: Kilometer 4.2 of the Colonya Valley road
coordinates_utm: 498720 E, 4410690 N DATUM EUROPEAN 1950 / 498629 E, 4410491 N DATUM WGS84
equipment: A rope may be needed to descend to the lake
description: Classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC), this cave requires authorization to visit. With approximately 370 m of development on a N-S orientation, it features two main chambers connected by steep, slippery slopes averaging 20-40 degrees. Notable features include abundant ceiling chimneys, spectacular stepped ceiling strata, and a lower chamber (Sala des Fang) that seasonally floods to form a shallow lake.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

Cova Morella is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI. At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

SPELEOGENESIS

It is clearly observed that this is a cavity of structural origin, even more so in the lower room (Sala des Fang) where the ceiling is composed of a single rock slab that runs almost parallel to the floor.

Notable is the scarce ornamentation of the cave, and even more so when in the neighboring Cova del Boc, located just a few meters away along the same line of cliffs, reconstructive processes abound.

Also noteworthy is the abundant presence of vertical conduits (chimneys) that line the ceiling without apparent order and in an isolated manner. Coinciding with some of these chimneys, sinusoidal grooves are observed on the ceiling.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

We find ourselves before a cavity with a development of approximately 370 m and a N-S orientation.

Access is through an entrance of 16 x 2.5 m whose western zone is collapsed by large blocks. Movement through the cave is delicate due to the slipperiness of the floor and the fact that the average slope is about 20° in the Sala de s'Entrada and about 40° in the Sala des Fang (it is a ramp cave).

Sala de s'Entrada (Entrance Room)

The first thing that catches the attention, before entering the cave, is the existence of an ivy that, originating from the room and after climbing a stalagmitic column, reaches the ceiling of the cavity and exits to the outside. Also worth highlighting is a beautiful maidenhair fern that hangs from the ceiling near the mentioned column.

With dimensions of 34 x 42 x 3.5 m and a N-S orientation, this collapse room, with a funnel morphology, runs along a slippery slope whose floor in the zone closest to the entrance is carpeted by cryptogamic vegetation formed by different types of plants.

Notable are the chimneys of different sizes observed on the ceiling, some of them ornamented. Through them, the cave receives a significant water input from the outside, which contributes to most of the floor in this room being always wet.

Beneath some of these chimneys, various ceramic containers were placed over time with the intention of collecting the water that fell from them.

Although reconstructive processes are not too abundant in this room, they are distributed mainly in the eastern zone, where some bulbous stalactites and some rimstone pools near a basin are worth noting.

Worth special mention are the spectacular overhead strata of stepped morphology, which have such an inclination that at the end of the room, at the access to the Sala des Fang, they come very close to the floor.

Sala des Fang (Mud Room)

With a NE-SW orientation, this room of 45 x 34 x 7 m corresponds to the zone farthest from the entrance and the deepest part of the cavity.

It is a monoclinal ramp room, with a steep slope in a southeast direction, and with a small escarpment halfway through it of about 2.0 m in height.

Only the eastern side of the room, developed over grey-green marls, presents a somewhat lesser slope, which allows access to the deepest zone without the need for progression equipment. The steep slope and the presence of very slippery materials (marly clays) that are occasionally wet make for a very dangerous and exposed descent.

The lowest zone of this room (-42 m) acts as the cave's sink and is paved by extremely numerous rounded cobbles. The presence of abundant chocolate-colored clay covering the cobbles in this zone gives the room its name, and indicates that during periods of heavy rainfall, water from the higher zones of the cave temporarily pools in this area, forming a small lake of shallow depth. Notable is the presence of marked polygonal tension cracks in the clays, which would indicate high plasticity of these.

The most relevant constructive forms correspond to the flowstones developed on the ramp which, depending on the time of the visit, may be covered in mud or present an immaculate white.

On the western side of the ramp, there are numerous debris flows developed over the marls, which allow an ascent with certain safety to the upper zone of the room, but the existence of the aforementioned escarpment prevents reaching the access to the Sala de s'Entrada.

Only in the upper western zone of the room is there a small corner where speleothems of stalagmitic type are observable, some of them with botryoidal coating, of marmoreal white color. In said zone, a sink of approximately 1.50 m in depth has developed, coinciding with the existence of two chimneys of considerable dimensions, highly ornamented.

In this room, the existence of vertical conduits (chimneys) of relevant sizes stands out even more. Although the steep slope of the room means that looking up at the ceiling must be a considered act, it is worth it, since from many of them beautiful flowstones hang.

Foradet de n'Encinas (Encinas's Little Hole)

A small chamber of difficult access measuring slightly more than 1.0 x 2.6 m, located halfway up the ramp of the Sala des Fang.

A tiny window allows access to it, not without some difficulty. The interior is covered mainly by a parietal flowstone, some small stalactites and stalagmites that in some cases have begun to form incipient columns. The floor of the deepest zone of the chamber is covered with sediment from the outside.

A graffiti stands out in this chamber indicating the name of one of the authors of the first known topographic survey of the cave, J. A. Encinas, and the date of its creation, 1967.

name: Cova des Pirata
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma 4014 road (Porto Cristo to Porto Colom). A path leads approximately 1.5 km to a half-open metal gate; once through, a trail leads to Cala Barca, then along the coast to Cala Falcó, from where a track approaches the cave.
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed, only helmet and lighting.
description: A large, beautifully decorated cave connected underwater to the nearby Cova des Pont, once developed for tourism in 1987 but now closed behind a metal gate. The cave features cathedral-sized formations, active gours, flowstone, and an underground lake. Two main routes are available: the southern path offers the most spectacular formations with a gentle descent to the lake, while the northern path descends steeply through blocks.
full_description:
I must make a clarification before beginning the description of this cave. On this website, the Cova del Pont and the Cova del Pirata are described separately as if they were two different caves, which is not true since both are connected by an underwater gallery. Of course, at the time this connection was unknown, and for that reason they were given different names despite being the same cave. It should therefore be clear that the Cova del Pont and del Pirata are the same cave with two entrances, each with a different name. At the beginning of my adventures in the fascinating world of speleology, this fact had me quite confused, since this clarification did not appear anywhere, while sometimes I would come across the name Pont/Pirata and other times the two names did not appear together at all. Unless we have the technical means and necessary knowledge for cave diving, we will have no choice but to visit the cave by entering it separately.

The Pirate Cave is not without its own legend, which tells of a wounded pirate who took refuge inside to heal his wounds, feeding on the milk provided by the goats of the area.

These caves were formerly equipped for tourist exploitation in 1987, coinciding with the agricultural fair held in Manacor. They are currently closed behind a metal gate.

The entrance to the cave is through an artificial door 0.90 m wide by 1.85 m high, oriented 125° SE. Once on the other side of the gate, we have two options to begin the visit: one is starting from the right side as we enter (south) and the other from the left (north). I am going to begin the description of this beautiful cavity by starting from the south side. If we look carefully at the chamber in which we find ourselves, ready to begin the visit, the conditioning works done for the tourist visit can be seen, notably the benches that were built for the visitors' rest. From this place, we will choose whether to continue the visit through the south zone (right side) or north zone (left side).

If we choose the right side (the most interesting): Through a natural doorway adapted for the commercial exploitation of the cavity, we will access a second vestibule and an active rimstone pool completely full of water, right in front of a beautiful flowstone. Here we can again choose whether to head north or south. I will describe this second option, which follows a well-defined path along which we will advance on a gentle descent, where we will find the occasional stair step that is still preserved today, and where we will soon find ourselves surrounded by a great quantity of formations of all types. Also in this place, the first solifluction processes can be observed (these are horizontal fractures with predominantly vertical displacement), one of which is very striking and, looking for a resemblance, looks like a church pulpit.

Without leaving our path and always on a gentle descent — though being careful not to slip, as at some points on the path there is that possibility — we will reach the lake. But first, we will leave behind some path junctions that will allow us to move through the rest of the cave, since this part of it is filled with formations of cathedral-like dimensions.

Once at the lake, which we will have reached through a steep slope either via the path's steps or by downclimbing, the visible part of the lake is rather narrow but long, and in some places it is interrupted, though by navigating obstacles we can travel its entirety. Through this lake, the Cova des Pirata connects with the Cova des Pont, and if we look carefully we can observe some ropes that mark the connection between them, so they should not be touched.

On the return, we will be able to contemplate the cave from a different angle than on the descent, so I strongly recommend looking from side to side, as we should not miss anything, until we arrive back at the rimstone pool to continue the visit through the north side, taking the rimstone pool as the starting point.

The visit of the north side of the cave begins through a narrow passage formed by flowstones, then turning sharply to the left and beginning a steep descent, more pronounced than the one on the right side, where formations are also much scarcer, with many blocks scattered on the ground, and where a kind of portal between a large flowstone stands out. From the portal, we will continue to the right, walking through a passageway. Once through it, we will follow the path to a sharp left curve that runs parallel to the cave's back wall, and then we will find some stairs that will deposit us in the entrance chamber — the one with the benches.

The Cova des Pirata is classified as a natural site of special speleological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventorial List of Natural Underground Features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

For further reading:

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): Les coves de la zona de Can Frasquet - Cala Varques. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.

ESTELRICH, M. 1905: Las cuevas del Pirata de Manacor (Mallorca). Guía y descripción de sus principales maravillas. Palma

name: Cova Serra Pla de s'Aljub Gros
name_english: Cave of the Serra Pla of the Large Cistern
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes housing development. Drive to the top and park at the end of Calle de la Televisión. Continue on Camí des Ri de ses Creus to a V-shaped junction, take the right branch towards Coll del Pators and follow the main trail to coordinates 461136 E, 4378755 N (DATUM ED50), then leave the trail — the cave is less than 90 m away.
coordinates_utm: 461215 E, 4378770 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 461121 E, 4378565 N (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: Helmet and lighting only
description: A small magnitude II cave discovered by chance in May 2010 on the slope near Puig Gros de Bendinat. Despite its modest size, it features abundant lithochemical formations including ceiling concretions, a large wall flowstone, and small active stalagmites. Wildlife observed includes a bat, arachnids, and empty snail shells. The cave is considered of speleological interest.
full_description:
On our way with R. Minguillón heading toward "Cova Fosca" for its survey on May 22, 2010, a stroke of luck allowed us to locate the entrance of this small cavity. A quick look at it — since no vertical progression equipment was needed — and we continued on our way, leaving the complete exploration and survey for another day. Finally, on June 5, 2010, after exploring an avenc in the area, we set about carrying out the planimetry work of this small magnitude II cavity, accompanied by A. Rubia.

The entrance to the cavity is 2 x 3.2 m and is oriented 40° N. Access to the interior places us in a small vestibule of just under a dozen meters long by approximately the same width, with a maximum height that does not exceed five meters. A wall of large rocks separates the vestibule from the rest of the cavity on the northern side. As in many other caves in the area, this one has been adapted by humans for use in not too distant times. Once past the vestibule, we continue the exploration of the cavity descending along a debris ramp with a 25% gradient until reaching a depth of about twelve meters, where the practicable end of the cave can be considered to be, as it continues for another four meters but these are hardly accessible.

Despite its small size, the lithochemical processes in the cavity are reasonably abundant, with notable ceiling concretions throughout the descent. A large wall flowstone again divides the cave, and in this area there are abundant small stalagmites, some of them fully active. Ceiling collapses are clearly visible here, composed mainly of two large blocks currently covered by calcite.

The fauna we could observe at first glance consisted of a specimen of chiroptera (bat) whose daytime rest we interrupted, as well as some specimens of arachnids and small empty shells of gastropod mollusks (snails).

A piece of pottery of not too great antiquity, possibly belonging to a Mallorcan jug, was found on our first visit to the cavity — nothing unusual, as many other cavities in the area attest, since ceramic containers were used to collect water from rimstone pools or were simply placed directly beneath stalactites to collect the water dripping from them.

The dimensions of the cavity do not fill a complete day, but that should not worry us since the area has no shortage of other small cavities that can allow us to enjoy a beautiful day out. At coordinates 461328, 4378579 we can find "Cova Fosca" just 120 m away, and at a little less than 350 m lies "Avenc d'en Miquel" at 461555, 4378705. In the case of this small avenc, we will need equipment to descend the dozen meters of depth.

"Cova Serra Pla de s'Aljub Gros" is considered of speleological interest.

name: Coves y Mines del Pilar
name_english: Caves and Mines of the Pillar
starting_point: The approach is made from the cemetery of La Vileta, following the access map for this cavity.
access: Own vehicle, or EMT urban bus line 8 towards Son Roca
coordinates_utm: EUROPEAN 1950: Upper entrance 464851 E, 4383856 N; Lower entrance 464765 E, 4383810 N. ETRS89: Upper entrance 464749 E, 4383656 N; Lower entrance 464663 E, 4383610 N
equipment: Helmet and lighting only, unless accessing the gallery below the Pillar gallery through the shaft, which requires vertical progression equipment and a rope of slightly over 20 m
description: A large cave system with three entrances on the SSW slope of Coll de Cans, within an old mining area near La Cantera de Son Quint in the Serra de na Burguesa. The cave features extensive speleothems, a chaotic boulder chamber, a bat room, and lower mining galleries with historically extracted minerals. Once briefly open to the public, it includes both natural and mine-modified passages across multiple levels.
full_description:
Note: The lower access built for tourist exploitation is currently closed with a barrier.

The cave is situated on the SSW slope of "Coll de Cans," in an old mining field near "La Cantera de Son Quint," and is included among the mountains of the "Serra de na Burguesa" JORDANA, R. 2005.

The Coves des Pilar is of large dimensions with three entrances currently open, one in the form of a shaft. Only the upper entrance appears to confirm that it is a natural entrance, since the lower entrance has the shape of a mine opening, but it cannot be ruled out that it was a natural entrance before mining operations began. The shaft-like entrance was possibly opened by human action to take advantage of natural light.

Along with the Cova des Pirata/Pont and the Cova des Estudiant, the Cova del Pilar was also open to the public for a short period of time.

Description

To begin describing this cavity, I will start by entering its interior through the upper entrance, which is oriented 56° NE with dimensions of 3 m wide by approximately 2.5 m high. We will enter the interior of the cavity through a small vestibule where, on its left side, a large number of columns stand out significantly, and from a sort of balcony, one can appreciate the next chamber of the cave, which is several meters below us and which we will reach by downclimbing on the right side, being careful not to slip.

Situated in this second chamber, we can immediately feel overwhelmed by the great quantity of speleothems that cover its walls, which immediately invites us to take out the camera and start shooting. With dimensions of approximately 30 meters long by 20 meters wide and an uneven floor carpeted in some places with large rocks, this chamber certainly leaves no one indifferent, while at the same time making us wonder what the rest of the cave might have in store.

To continue the visit, we will proceed along the right side (east). The chamber we encounter is of enormous dimensions, with a length possibly exceeding 50 m and a width of no less than 30 m, notable for its lack of formations but impressive for the great chaos of blocks covering the floor surface, with one enormous block standing out among all the rest. We will advance through this chamber, which some call the "Chaos Cavern," along its left side, always seeking the best way to navigate between the rocks until we reach a sort of promontory from where one can see how outside light illuminates this part of the chamber through a rectangular artificial opening in the ceiling of approximately 4x3.5 m (464795 E, 4383835 N, 295 m). From this location, we can head right again, retracing slightly to find a chamber where a curious column formed by the joining of thinner ones (at least that is what it appears to be) marks the spot from where we will begin accessing the "Bat Room" (Sala de ses Rates Pinyades). The access to this room is laborious as it is somewhat labyrinthine, and the route to reach it passes through tight spaces where there is also the possibility of getting lost, at least temporarily. A large number of bats inhabit this room, as evidenced by the floor covered in droppings, although during my first visit I could only find one specimen, which surprised me; I subsequently found some isolated specimens in the lower mining galleries of the cave. Returning from the "Bat Room," we will continue the visit by heading just slightly to the left of the previously mentioned artificial opening that lets in outside light, beginning to descend until we reach the 5 by 8 meter room or vestibule of the lower entrance, which is reached after passing through an 11-meter trench and a 9-meter mining gallery, from where we have several options.

1. Exit the cavity through the aforementioned gallery.
2. Explore the "Pillar Mining Gallery" that departs from the vestibule.
3. Descend the stairs next to a stalagmite to visit some small recesses.
4. Venture into a hidden mining world that we should not miss.
5. Once outside, visit the "Trench Gallery" located in the E direction just 30 m away.

If we choose the first option, once outside we still have the possibility of visiting the mining gallery "Trench Gallery," which is part of the Guix mining complex, located in the E direction just about 30 m away and accessed through a gigantic 45 m trench that gives it its name.

The lower entrance of the cave is oriented 74° ENE in the form of a mining gallery with a width of 1.62 m and a height of 2 m. Although today the entrance has the typical shape of a mining gallery, it is possible that it was previously a natural entrance later adapted.

The second option takes us into the "Pillar Gallery," which departs from the vestibule oriented 85° E with a length of 56 m and an artificial stone ceiling, until reaching a rectangular artificial shaft of approximately 14 m that leads to another lower gallery and blocks the passage to the other side. One can see the collapse of the gallery beyond a beautiful semicircular stone arch on the other side of the shaft. Caution! Do not fall — an accident already occurred some years ago involving a young person, which was covered in the local press.

For the third option, well-defined stairs allow us to descend to visit some small recesses. These stairs depart next to an old column that apparently gave the cave its name, but due to its growth, today it is not only not a pillar — which it never was — but it is no longer a column either, as it is now very separated from the ceiling, though at some point it was connected.

We must access the "Lower Galleries Room" to enter a mining complex of great interest where we can discover in the gallery walls some of the minerals that were extracted at this location until the mid-20th century. I will not go into detail regarding the description of this place, only recommend maximum caution if one wishes to explore the entirety and advise against visiting for people unaccustomed to navigating this type of site.

If we approach to visit the gallery near the cave exit, the first thing we will encounter is a trench that gives the gallery its name, measuring 45 m, in which one can see at the end, in addition to the entrance to the gallery at our level, another one many meters above that was surely built earlier and can only be accessed by rappelling, since a shaft that was presumably used to access it or at least to illuminate it is currently collapsed.

name: Coves Petites
starting_point: Road to the Artá caves (Canyamel). Park near the 'Baixada del Rei' apartments at number 66, or at the Artá Caves parking lot.
clothing: Overalls recommended if accessing the wells
access: Own vehicle recommended, or BUS 31 from Palma
equipment: A 20 m rope and a 5 m rope, 1 sling, 1 carabiner
description: Located near the tourist Artá caves in folded Jurassic limestone, this cave features marine phreatic speleothems from when Mediterranean levels were 40 m above current sea level. The main chamber contains impressive columns and stalagmitic massifs, plus an 11 m deep sinkhole well. Evidence of historical travertine extraction remains visible, and the cave currently serves as a bat sanctuary.
full_description:
Description

Very close to the tourist cave of Artá, this interesting cavity is found which, although it does not reach its dimensions, is no less interesting for that.

Geologically speaking, the cavity is located in the folded Jurassic limestones that make up the mountainous area of the Eastern Ranges.

As in its neighboring Artá cave, inside the cave some marine phreatic speleothems can be seen in the form of bulbous stalactites related to ancient paleolevels from times when the Mediterranean water level reached 40 m above the current level.

Its two access entrances are found close to each other. Both absorbed the rainwater from the adjacent ravine, participating in the development of the cave.

The morphology of the cave is mainly formed by a vestibule where the two current entrances are found, where some speleothems in the form of columns and a stalagmitic massif that covers a large part of the western end can be seen. Once this massif is crossed, one enters the main hall of large dimensions, where speleothems of great size and beauty abound, formed mainly by impressive columns and stalagmitic massifs.

Another of the singular places in the cave is the sinkhole well of 11 m depth located in the southern area, which can be accessed with the help of a rope, although for safety the use of progression equipment is recommended. Once in the deepest area of the well, a small opening allows us to access the most remote area of the cave 3 meters further down, again with the help of a rope. It is a fairly active area where some shallow gours have formed in the northern zone.

As happened to other caves on the island — see the Cueva de s'Ónix in Porto Cristo and the Cuevas de Mármol in Calvià — the Coves Petites also suffered the consequences of travertine extraction to be used as ornamental stone in construction. Sufficient evidence of this remains in the northern area of the main hall where they left some abandoned blocks in which drill marks can be observed.

Currently, the cave serves as a refuge for bats. During our topographic and exploratory work, we were able to observe inside the cavity several specimens of cave bats, which is why they should not be disturbed, and it should be remembered that all bat species on the island are protected.

Not long ago, a villa was built right above the cave, which caused some rocks of considerable size to fall into its interior during construction. We do not know whether the existing legislation on these matters was respected during its construction. What is certain is the negative effect that this construction will have on the evolution of the cavity in the future.

Its proximity to the town has not been able to save it from some acts of vandalism that have occurred inside the cave on some formations in the form of graffiti, which, while they have not caused too much significant damage to the cavity, have damaged some interesting formations.

name: Crull des Puig Ventós
equipment: Only helmet and lighting; the 5 m well is accessed without vertical equipment due to its narrowness
description: A mechanical-origin fracture on the SE slope of Puig Ventós (274 m), approximately 38 m long, 0.5-5 m wide, and 17 m deep. Oriented NE/SW, it is largely filled with blocks of varying sizes. Notable for its botanical interest, hosting ferns (Asplenium species), mosses, lichens, and fungi, plus an unidentified bat species in the deepest area.
full_description:
Few are the additions by Mallorca Verde of this type of cavity, especially fractures completely open to the outside, despite the importance they can have in the environment in which they develop, both of geological and botanical type, among others. We hope that the addition of the Crull des Puig Ventós helps us raise awareness and at the same time appreciate the delicate ecosystems they may harbor inside.

Description

On the SE slope of Puig Ventós (274 m), this Crull (fracture) of mechanical origin opens, approximately 38 m in length, with a width ranging from approximately 0.5 m in the northern area to 5 m, and a depth of -17 m.

As I mentioned earlier, it is a simple extensional fracture with NE/SW orientation, in which its width is uneven, such that the northern zone is very narrow compared to the southern one, and also becomes impassable. The entire fracture is occupied by large quantities of blocks of all sizes, which prevent access to the deep zones, with the exception of a small well of a little more than 5 m at the base of a step in the northern zone. There are also blocks wedged in the walls of the final section of the fracture, at the SW end, some of them very large. It is worth noting the existence of a dry stone wall at this end of the fracture, perhaps delimiting some boundary.

As usually happens in this type of fracture, ornamentation is nonexistent or scarce, limited mainly to parietal flowstones and always in enclosed areas, since erosion makes them disappear from the moment they come into contact with atmospheric phenomena. In this case, the fracture still harbors some of them.

A few meters above this crull (fracture) is the Cova des Puig Ventós, very close to the summit, whose formative origins are the same, indicating that the dismantling processes that began in this area of Puig Ventós reach almost to the very summit.

It is worth highlighting the botanical interest that this type of geographical feature can harbor in some cases, where they sometimes house interesting terrestrial ecosystems. In this crull, an important diversity of plants coexist, among which different species of Asplenium (ferns), mosses, lichens, and some specimens of fungi fixed to the wall of one of the fracture's connections with the exterior stand out. In the deepest area of the surveyed zone, we were able to observe a specimen of chiroptera (bat), unidentified. Everything described here should serve to raise our awareness of the importance that these types of places can have and involve us in collaborating in their conservation.

name: Cova de Cal Pesso
starting_point: Puerto de Pollença
clothing: Overalls recommended, as frequent crawling is required and the crawlway areas are often waterlogged, especially in winter
distance: 340 m from the end of the street
access: Transunion bus company offers various options to reach Pollensa
equipment: Vertical progression equipment plus a 15 m rope for the first obstacle. For the shaft: 100 m rope. Additional 20 m rope as a walkway for the final section of the cave.
description: One of Mallorca's most well-known caves, located in the Cavall Bernat range near the Roman ruins of Bocchoris. Classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC) requiring official authorization to visit. The cave features a tube-like gallery structure with crawlways, a 65 m shaft, impressive crystallizations, curtains, gours, and delicate columns, with the best-preserved formations in the rear sections that few visitors reach.
full_description:
Important

The Cova des Diners is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities catalogued as LIC. Currently (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

Description of the cavity

Without a doubt, we are before one of the most well-known caves on the island of Mallorca, as many people access its interior, some of them venturing inside without any knowledge of the environment and lacking any information about the cave. This cave is also used to introduce students in the various speleology courses held in Mallorca by different clubs.

We will enter the cavity through a small pit of more or less one meter in diameter through which we will descend until reaching a fork where we will take the left side, since if we make a mistake and try to advance through the right one, what already happened to an acquaintance of mine could happen to us — he mistakenly tried to pass through and got stuck for a good while during which he could neither advance nor retreat, with the resulting fright.

We will ascend a small ramp and then descend to a small balcony where we will encounter the first difficulty of our visit. It involves overcoming a small ramp of about 8 meters that is usually wet, and to avoid surprises on the return, we will tie a rope to a stalagmite and begin the descent until reaching the base of the ramp, where we will find easy anchoring for our rope, which we must do to prevent any prankster from removing it.

Once the obstacle is overcome, and to continue our visit, I will make a small clarification: the structure of this cave consists of a sort of "tube" or gallery with some small branches to the left and right as well as some small shafts, so it is difficult to get lost, and if this happens, it will never be for long.

The Cova de Cal Pesso has the reputation of lacking formations. As almost always, it is best not to pay too much attention to this type of claim, since in this case it is completely false, as can be verified throughout the visit to the cave. It is possible that some of the people who make such claims have not visited the entire cave, which leads them to say this. In fact, concretions already begin to appear at the base of the ramp.

To continue our visit, we will head NE, beginning a gentle descent until reaching the end of this first room, where we will pass through a row of columns to position ourselves in the second room, divided by another row of columns. We must advance along the left side of the room until we find the mouth of the first crawlway. If our visit to the cave coincides with winter, surely the entire crawlway area, or at least a large part of it, will be waterlogged, so getting wet and dirty with mud is guaranteed. The only thing we can do is wear the most appropriate clothing, which in this case is overalls. Once through this first crawlway, we will come out into a small room and then another of similar characteristics, finally arriving at another with a slightly higher ceiling where we will have to cross the last crawlway, which places us next to the 65 m shaft (the shaft is not described). We continue our path descending to the right until overcoming a slide — this is a wing of the cave that leads us to visit a gallery where we can find interesting crystallizations at its end, and where a rope should also accompany us to navigate the slippery slides.

Back in the room, we can observe how the ceiling drops considerably, and we must cross to the other side between some columns. In this room, the ceiling once again reaches great height, with notable formations at the back and to the left on a ramp — a large number of concretions among which a slender column surrounded by small stalagmites stands out. In the central part of the column, it is thicker, and it begins to thin again as it approaches the ceiling. This column has characteristics similar to another that we will find a little further ahead in a beautiful corner, but to reach it we must first overcome the most delicate part of the cave, aside from the great shaft.

Just a little beyond the previously mentioned thin column, we will encounter a much thicker one, to whose left is a 9-meter shaft and another slightly smaller one in front. To the right of the column, a "corridor" forms between it and another row of columns through which we will pass, installing a traverse line (there is no problem fixing the rope to a natural anchor). The ramp we will traverse to install the traverse line is covered by a thin layer of water and can sometimes also be muddy. Additionally, one must overcome a sort of small rounded step that can certainly give a sense of danger, especially to the person doing the installation. An alternative may be to climb over the row of columns on the right side to bypass this delicate passage and thus cross to the other side, since the permanent view of the two aforementioned shafts raises our adrenaline. Once the aforementioned obstacle is overcome, we are in the final section of the cave, this being the best-preserved part since most people who visit the cave turn back when they encounter the two shafts.

In the room where we find ourselves, one can immediately appreciate what I wrote earlier, as it is excellently decorated with all types of formations, with an impressive set of curtains standing out, along with a wide gour and the extremely thin column mentioned earlier, surrounded by small stalagmites, while from the ceiling hang multiple lance-shaped stalactites threatening to impale us. On the other side of this room and to our left, another group of columns in a row stands out, which we will access using a sort of corridor formed by tiny stalagmites that will serve as support, thus avoiding the almost certain "slip," since the ramp is soaked with water. We have reached the end of the cave, where we can still visit a couple of small rooms but without great interest, so we can consider the visit finished and begin the return.

name: Cova Tancada des Cap de Menorca
name_english: Closed Cave of Cap de Menorca
starting_point: Drive to Alcúdia, then head towards La Victoria until the junction at Bar la Bodega del Sol. Turn right onto Camino de Muntanya and follow it to a gate where it joins the Coll Baix path. Continue on the unpaved, dusty road to km 4.800 (measured from Bar la Bodega del Sol).
clothing: Interior humidity is close to 80%, so a single t-shirt is sufficient.
best_season: Avoid winter, especially when the sea is rough.
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting.
description: A stunning baroque cave on the Alcúdia peninsula featuring abundant speleothems, stalactites, stalagmites, and columns throughout a large decorated chamber. The cave has carved stone steps from a former attempt to develop it as a tourist attraction. Access involves a challenging trail with some dangerous sections near cliffs. Designated as a site of special biological interest.
full_description:
Once at the entrance, we have to advance through a gallery that begins to descend and ascend via steps carved into the rock when there were plans to develop the cave for tourism. However, throughout the cave we must be careful not to slip, as it is currently entirely active and the floor is very wet.

As soon as we peer into the great chamber, we are amazed by the marvel that appears before our eyes, as we find ourselves in a baroque cave where concretions decorate everything our eyes can reach. Two small gours are found to our left, filled with water, although I must say that one of them has been completed artificially — I am not referring to the contents but to the container. As soon as we begin the gentle descent into the enormous chamber, we pass by two slender columns situated in front of one of the most beautiful corners of this cave.

It is easy to continue our visit following the reasonably well-marked path that crosses the cave from one end to the other, allowing us to contemplate everything that surrounds us. After ascending a gentle ramp, we come to a staircase of five steps, and once past it, a cluster of columns and stalagmites decorates the center of the chamber, leaving no one indifferent, and which can be circled on either side, though the "official" path runs along the left side of the group of columns, requiring the use of hands in more than one place. One should stop at this spot to marvel at this bundle of columns of all shapes and thicknesses. Now it is our turn to descend to the zero level using the steps carved into the rock, to then begin again an ascent of approximately the same distance to a landing from which one can see a ramp that, if followed, will take us to the end of the cave, descending without being able to regain an upright position. This is a place where on some occasions we may feel a certain oppression due to the very high percentage of humidity, from where we will begin the return along the same path, trying to observe the formations we missed on the way in, as well as peeking into the small corners and holes that appear along our route, and we can head toward the exit of the chamber along its left side in the direction of travel.

Before leaving the cave, we can also access its second opening to the outside, which we can do through a crawlway that we can take on our right just after leaving the main chamber, descending a small visible ramp that deposits us in a small room with branches both to the left and to the right but with no continuation except straight ahead toward the small window to the outside.

An easy cave to visit in which only the approach presents difficulties, as the final section of the trail is in quite poor condition and a wrong step could send us plunging into the void.

Despite having read somewhere — I cannot remember where at this moment — that inside the cave, due to the interior temperature between seventeen and nineteen degrees and humidity between seventy and eighty-five percent, insect life does not exist, I take the opportunity in these lines to deny this, since during the visit of July 20, 2008, I was able to observe two moths fluttering around me. Other species found up to now inside the cave are the bat Pipistrellus pipistrellus, and the Southern European garden dormouse Eliomys quercinus, better known as "Rata Sarda" (Sardinian rat).

The Cova Tancada des Cap de Menorca is considered a site of special biological interest. J. A. Encinas S. Inventorial Listing of Natural Subterranean Sites of the Balearic Islands. 2006. Ediciones JAES.

If we use candles to mark the path, they must be removed on the way back, so that no traces of our presence remain inside the cavity. For some time now, some guides have been taking tourists to visit the cave and placing candles throughout the great chamber to illuminate it, then abandoning them afterward. On my last visit, we removed from the cave two supermarket bags full of said candles, which is why from this notebook I strongly recommend that we keep this cavity clean so that the people who come after us can enjoy it. It is impossible to get lost in the cavity, so we simply need to carry electric light and in any case pay attention to the steps we need to follow to exit to the outside.

For further reading:

DANIEL MAYORAL, TOMEU MATEU. 2007. "Mallorca, beauties in the darkness" Practical and visual guide to speleology in Mallorca.

name: Coves de Mármol
starting_point: Km 6 of the Ma-1043 road
best_season: Any, though summer can be very hot during the approach
equipment: Detailed in description (varies by section: 30 m rope, protectors, slings, carabiners)
description: One of the deepest caves (-85 m) in the Serra de na Burguesa, actually comprising four cavities from the dismantling of the original cave: Sistema Sud, Cova Nord, Avenc de Mármol I, and Avenc de Mármol II. The Sistema Sud features the spectacular Sala de ses Columnes with its forest of columns, while the Cova Nord contains the beautiful Sala de sa Columna Guapa. Frequently visited by hiking groups, though few explore its full extent.
full_description:
SOUTH SYSTEM

Coordinates: 461605 4379950 DATUM ETRS89

Altitude: 350 m.

Description

It is actually four cavities resulting from the dismantling of the original cave. These are the South System, North Cave, Avenc de Mármol I, and Avenc de Mármol II. We have taken the liberty of individualizing the name of each one in order to eliminate the existing confusion, where people cannot even agree on the number of cavities that make up the ensemble.

With a depth of -85 m, it is among the deepest in the Serra de na Burguesa, as well as being one of those with the greatest development. The cave is visited mainly by hiking groups because its level of difficulty is not very demanding; however, not all visitors explore the cave in its entirety — in fact, few people have really scrutinized all its corners. In this work we are going to make an exhaustive description of it.

Sala de sa Falzilla (Maidenhair Fern Room)

We will begin the description of the cave at the south entrance, through which we will access the Sala de sa Falzilla (Maidenhair Fern). It is a small room that in plan barely exceeds a dozen meters in width by approximately eight in depth. The height, if we disregard the ramp that overlies the next room, does not reach four meters. Right at the back of the room, not without great difficulty, due to the location and surely the lack of nutrients, a maidenhair fern plant grows — something that goes unnoticed by most visitors. In this area of the cave there is reasonable parietal and ceiling concretion.

We will continue the visit entering a small room where a column to our right stands out, and we will continue to the left, having to overcome a small obstacle that will leave us next to a beautiful stalagmite on our left.

From this point we have two possible descents: through a shaft known as S'Embut (The Funnel), or, a few meters higher, through Sa Rampa (The Ramp).

S'Embut (The Funnel)

If we opt for the first option, just past the stalagmite we will descend a small ramp to our left until reaching the mouth of the shaft. It is a quite narrow double shaft with a total height of about 4 or 5 m. To get past the upper shaft we will use the numerous holes and formations on the walls, while to descend the lower shaft we will have to hang from a thick stalagmite near its mouth and seek footholds at its base. It is important to take note when exiting the lower shaft, as on the return it is difficult to locate. Also, one must remember that it is a double shaft, so on the way back, once the lower shaft has been overcome, one must remember to keep ascending (there is a small widening between the shafts that can create the sensation of already being on the exit ramp).

Sa Rampa (The Ramp)

It has a gradient of 60% in the first two-thirds of the ramp and 50% in the final part, so the use of rope is advisable. Once its base is reached and heading S/E, a narrow passage is accessed that will test our skill, followed by a less compromising one that will place us very close to the lower area of the "funnel." Halfway down the ramp, there is a small room of about seven meters of no particular interest.

Sala de ses Columnes (Column Room)

From the base of the "funnel," heading S/W, we will descend through a small room with columns and enter a chaos of blocks. Once past the blocks, we will descend down a ramp toward the Sala de ses Columnes.

Before our eyes a room of great proportions appears — approximately thirty-five meters in length by about twenty-five at its widest part, which is the farthest, where the forest of columns accumulates (figure 6). The approximate development is one hundred and ten meters, reaching a height that exceeds twenty meters.

The view from this spot is simply spectacular; it gives the sensation of being situated in the summa cavea of a theater, where at any moment it seems the show is about to begin since the curtain is raised and the stage shines in all its splendor. Once situated next to the "forest" of columns, beneath our feet the cave continues through the Pou de ses Tres Forats and the Galeria des Graffitis, as we are on top of a great chaos of cemented blocks.

Pou des Tres Forats (Three-Hole Shaft)

In the SE area of the Sala de ses Columnes, near a rimstone pool, one of the three entrances that give access to this area of the cave is found. Like the Galeria des Graffitis, it is a place formed by a chaos of blocks resulting from collapses produced over time in the Sala de ses Columnes, reaching -9 m in two levels.

Galeria des Graffitis (Graffiti Gallery)

Returning from the Pou des Tres Forats we can visit this place; to do so we will need to move toward the western area of the Sala de ses Columnes, among the forest of columns. Like the Pou, we are in a collapsed area, but on this occasion of greater size and depth, reaching -77 m relative to the main entrance of the cave. On the first level there is reasonable concretion, but not so in the deepest area, which is accessed after passing some narrow passages. The name comes from the graffiti found on its walls.

Saleta des Gour (Rimstone Pool Chamber)

It is a small room of 5 x 6 m accessed from the lower part of Sa Rampa. Despite its small dimensions, the Saleta des Gour houses a great quantity of formations within it, including a rimstone pool right at the room's entrance. In the northern area of the room it is possible to climb to an upper level through which one could connect with the rest of the cave, but a block collapse currently prevents this.

Galeria Minguillón (Minguillón Gallery)

It is a gallery running N-S, with a length exceeding fifty meters and a maximum width of around twenty meters. The height is variable, making it impossible to stand upright practically throughout the entire route.

We find two difficulties: the first is locating it, especially if we try to do so from the southern area of the cave, since the hole through which it is accessed, besides being very narrow, is difficult to locate despite the fact that whenever this area of the cave is visited we pass right by it. The other difficulty consists of orienting oneself inside the gallery. From the Sala de sa Colada it is easier; in that case the greatest difficulty is completing it because the narrow passage where one exits is not at all comfortable in this direction.

The gallery is a chaos of blocks piled one against another, blocking our way, so one must always be mindful of finding the continuation. Despite this, it is not devoid of formations — in fact it has some of great purity, though they are not active.

Sala de sa Colada (Flowstone Room)

This room of 13.5 x 14 x 3 m can be accessed from two different places. It is found at the end of the Galeria Minguillón, so if coming from the latter, reaching it allows us to breathe with tranquility, since from it one accesses the exterior, right below the great mouth of the Cova Nord. The most notable feature of this room is the great flowstone that occupies a large part of it, in addition to reasonable ceiling concretion.

Sala de ses Excèntriques (Eccentric Room)

It is a small room of 12 x 15 m found after the Sala de sa Colada. To access it directly, one can easily enter through the access mouth of the Sala de sa Colada until reaching a thick stalagmite; the continuation is to the east, descending through a flowstone and having to pass a narrow passage right at the beginning. Inside it houses mainly ceiling ornamentation in which a great quantity of eccentric stalactites stand out.

Saleta des Niu (Nest Chamber)

It is the continuation of the Sala de ses Excèntriques, but the connection between them is not easy, so it is better to go outside and enter it directly. It is a small room of 7 x 13 x 1 m that marks the end of the South System. It is reasonably concretioned, housing inside an abandoned nest of some small bird, which is why it receives its name.

NORTH CAVE (COVA NORD)

Coordinates: 461595 4379980 DATUM ETRS89

Altitude: 373 m.

The Cova Nord is part of the original cavity from which quarrying operations isolated it from the rest of the cavity. It is located 23 meters above the main (lower) entrance of the South System. Its spectacular entrance (figure 7) makes it visible at all times, even from great distances.

Saleta de ses Pedres (Stone Chamber)

In the quarry, once past the entrance to the Sala de sa Colada, we will continue ascending northward, having to overcome a small terrain difficulty before positioning ourselves on an upper level. We are now beneath the "hat" of the Sala dels Blocs, at the eastern end of the cave. From this point we will venture in search of the Saleta de ses Pedres, which is found right behind some columns at a distance exceeding a dozen meters. As we make our way toward the entrance, we can observe the great flowstone descending from the upper part of the Sala des Blocs. Once at the previously mentioned columns, we will downclimb into the interior of the room of 6 x 12 x 2.5 m. The floor of the room is covered with stones, possibly fallen from the upper zone of the Sala des Blocs over time. The concretion of the room is abundant, including some eccentrics as well as a beautiful parietal flowstone. Activity, as in the rest of the cave, is scarce.

Sala des Blocs (Block Room)

On our return, we will head to the Sala des Blocs. To do so, one must climb up the rock with a couple of delicate spots, so attention must be paid, especially during the descent. It is precisely at this location where quarrying operations were abandoned. Once positioned in the upper part, we are at the western end of the room. It is a collapsed room where great blocks cemented with calcite are piled up. Great care must be taken in some places in the room as gaps of several meters depth open up due to the piling of blocks. The Sala des Blocs has dimensions of 21 x 22 x 10 m.

Racó de s'Òrgan (Organ Corner)

It is a small corner hidden behind the stalagmitic massif found at the western end of the Sala des Blocs (figure 8). The most notable feature of the place is the aforementioned stalagmitic massif, with the appearance of an organ.

Sala de sa Columna Guapa (Beautiful Column Room)

Access to this room is found next to the Racó de s'Òrgan. A small downclimb positions us right at the entrance, which is partially illuminated thanks to light received from outside. The play of lights produced inside, by itself, already merits the visit. It is a room of 20 x 31 x 10 m that possesses great beauty despite the lack of activity and the great amount of dust present.

We can begin the route descending along the eastern side, where formations of all kinds abound — large curtains, columns, stalactites, etc. The eastern end of the room is entirely covered by a beautiful parietal flowstone, and the imposing Beautiful Column stands out above the rest of the formations.

If we observe carefully, we can see above us, to the right of a large block that seems to divide the room in two and which is crowned with formations, the entrance to a small room of difficult access. At the level of the Beautiful Column, we will descend the step, always staying close to the wall, to the deepest area of the room, where the Narrow Shaft (Pou Estret) is found. Returning and positioned once again on the aforementioned step, we will climb to access a beautiful hidden corner where large curtains stand out, which in turn harbor some eccentrics. We will advance, accessing another area of the room of greater amplitude where a great stalagmitic massif of great beauty stands out. From this place, natural light can be seen on the other side of a stalagmitic massif that we will have to pass to conclude the visit to the room.

Pou Estret (Narrow Shaft)

It is found in the deepest area of the room, beneath a large rock that has formed a separate space from the rest of the Sala de sa Columna Guapa. It is accessed through a crack, either by downclimbing — if one has practice in opposition technique — or with the help of a 15 m rope. Its 10.5 m of depth hold no interest other than having reached the lowest elevation of the cavity.

AVENC DE MÁRMOL I (MARBLE PIT I)

Coordinates: 461620 4379965 DATUM ETRS89

Altitude: 365 m.

Description

The Avenc de Mármol I is found at the same level as the entrance to the Saleta de ses Pedres, though a little more to the south and against the wall of the quarry cut.

Sala de ses Rates Pinyades (Bat Room)

After descending 13.5 m, we find ourselves in a room of 10 x 15 m where the light from outside creates beautiful backlighting effects and illuminates the rich variety of colors of its formations. If we look carefully, we can observe the collapses that have occurred inside the cavity, among which a large block shaped like a waterfall stands out. Despite everything, the room is very ornamented with formations of great beauty, with a beautiful aerial corner standing out as well as a beautiful "curtain" formed by a group of stalactites that hides access to another route in the pit.

Sala des Bloc (Block Room)

As soon as the descent begins, we immediately realize that we are passing through a chaos of blocks of all sizes piled on top of each other, as well as the great number of fallen formations mixed with the blocks. The chaos is so spectacular that everything suggests its origin could be related to the quarrying operations.

The Sala des Bloc, like the rest of the pit, follows the same dynamic as what was seen during the descent. That does not mean that in some places formations are not preserved that have miraculously survived the catastrophe. A great block occupies most of the ceiling of the room, which while firmly supported at one end, at the other gives the sensation that at any moment it could finish sliding.

Sala de s'Estalactita (Stalactite Room)

A little before reaching the deepest area of the pit, we find this room that still preserves some ornamentation, with a stalactite of great size standing out. It maintains the same tone as the rest of the pit. A little further down, a small well marks the end at -33 m.

Sala Amagada (Hidden Room)
