Returning to the Sala de ses Rates Pinyades, and to continue the visit, we will need to climb toward the beautiful curtain of stalactites and pass to the other side, where the Sala Amagada is already visible beneath our feet.

It is a small room of 7 x 6 x 3 m that we can access by downclimbing very carefully or with the help of the rope we used to descend into the cavity, as the access to the room is near the vertical. A small shaft of -6 m, accessible without having to use progression equipment, marks the end of this area.

Equipment

30 m of rope and a couple of protectors.

AVENC DE MÁRMOL II (MARBLE PIT II)

Coordinates: 461620 4379965 DATUM ETRS89

Altitude: 365 m.

Description

Just a few meters further south of the Avenc de Mármol I, on the same quarry cut line, the small mouth — approximately one meter wide and slightly less in height — is found, which allows us to enter this small pit of -14 m depth.

Rampa de ses Columnes (Column Ramp)

Just upon entering the interior, the descent is made northward down the ramp that gives the room its name, for about ten meters until reaching a small ledge that allows us to set a rebelay on a broken column. This is a good place to contemplate the beauty of the room, especially if the day is sunny, as the sun penetrating the interior highlights its beauty. Another small descent down the ramp, followed by a vertical of approximately four meters, places us on a pile of debris — this is the head of the Sala dets Detrits.

Sala dets Detrits (Debris Room)

It is a room of 10 x 14 x 10 m in which formations of all types are present. At the northern end there is a cone of debris formed by the accumulation of organic compounds that have been introduced from the outside over time. It is a horizontal room in which damage to formations is also visible, possibly caused during the quarrying operations.

Es Pou (The Well)

Right in the center of the debris cone is this small shaft of -4.5 m of no interest, which marks the maximum depth of the cavity.

Equipment

30 m of rope, 1 sling for the rebelay on the broken column, 1 protector, 1 carabiner.

name: Es Forat de s'Arqueòleg
access: Bus from Palma intermodal station, line 221 to Orient. First bus at 8:45, last return at 17:45 (as of June 17, 2016)
equipment: 1 rope of 60 m or two of 30 m. 4 protectors and several long slings
description: A vertical tectonic fracture on the western cliff of Puig de sa Font Seca, developed in Lower Jurassic limestone breccia and reaching -44 m depth. Discovered by chance while searching for another cave, the descent passes through three chambers with notable lithochemical formations. The final chamber is a small 2.5 x 2.5 m sump where the cave becomes impassable.
full_description:
In the Serra d'Amos and over calcareous breccias of the Lias (Lower Jurassic), this tectonic fracture is found on the western cliff of Puig de sa Font Seca, right at the boundary of the municipalities of Bunyola and Alaró.

It sometimes happens that while searching for one cavity, one has the fortune of finding a different one, which produces satisfaction, and even more so when it has not been possible to locate the one being sought. This is what happened to Jaume Deya, responsible for the discovery of the chasm.

The discontinuity of the terrain along with the work of water facilitated in this case the development of this vertical cavity of -44 m, in which small chambers formed at different levels where the reconstructive processes are significant and of great beauty.

After setting up the anchor point and after overcoming a kind of natural partition wall, we enter a steep narrow shaft. Before beginning the descent, it is advisable to place 2 protectors separated from each other by approximately 20 cm and a third one a few meters below where the rope friction is evident.

Sala dets Ossos (Hall of Bones)

At 11 m depth we find the chamber with the greatest development of the cavity, measuring 6 x 8.5 m. The lithochemical processes in this place are notable, where successive collapses have also developed. Some bones of a bovine thrown from the outside to this place have given the hall its name.

Sala de ses Pedres (Hall of Stones)

After a brief descent of a little more than 3 m we "land" on the highest area of the hall whose surface is covered with clasts. The dimensions of this small hall are 4 x 8 m. Noteworthy is the beautiful corner located to the north of the hall, next to the continuation of the chasm.

Saleta Final (Final Little Hall)

At 2.5 x 2.5 m, this is the sinkhole of the cavity. From this point the chasm becomes impassable; the water continues its path from this place through a small orifice sculpted in flowstone.

name: Escletxa de Maristel·la
starting_point: Son Trias recreational area
coordinates_utm: 462445 E, 4390385 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 462345 E, 4390180 N (DATUM ETRS89)
equipment: 2 ropes of 25m, several protectors and slings
description: A NE-SW oriented fissure near the Maristel·la hermitage, accessed through a small opening beneath a 2m rock. Below -11m, the fissure opens into the Sala de ses Banderes (Flag Hall), named for its abundant flag-shaped formations. Sa Rampa is a chaotic collapse chamber with calcite-cemented blocks and numerous speleothems. The deepest section, Sala de sa Troneta, is reached through a very tight passage and contains a sinkhole.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

In the surroundings of the esplanade of the Maristel·la hermitage and on the other side of a dry stone wall, this fracture is found with a NE-SW orientation, the majority direction of all those found in the area.

Access to the interior of the cavity is through an entrance of reduced dimensions located beneath a rock of nearly 2 m in height. A short downclimb places us on what we have called the Upper Level, formed by the accumulation of stones, earth, and organic matter wedged into the fracture. To the SW it reaches about a dozen meters and possible continuations can be glimpsed, while to the NE the descent begins through a considerable constriction combined with wedged stones that will put our ability to adapt our body to this type of terrain to the test.

Sala de ses Banderes (Flag Chamber)

At around -11 m, the fracture becomes a cave and formations begin to appear, initially mainly consisting of curtains. It is a chamber measuring 10 x 11.5 x 3 m.

The name of the chamber refers to the large quantity of curtain formations found in it, although it is not the only type of speleothem present.

Two pieces of graffiti found are evident proof that at least up to this point in the cave, others had preceded us. The description of one of the graffiti found could read OREJA, but the last character is not sufficiently visible; in the other, what appear to be the initials of some visitor can be made out.

Sa Rampa (The Ramp)

By climbing from the lower area of the Sala des Grafiti, one accesses this chamber which, as its name suggests, is a sub-vertical chamber measuring 12 x 28 x 2.5 m, with an E-W orientation and a considerable dip in the deepest zones.

It is a collapse chamber, quite chaotic, in which a large quantity of blocks accumulate, many of them cemented by calcite, on which a great number of formations have developed. The highest area of the chamber is the one that harbors the greatest number of speleothems.

Sala de sa Troneta (Throne Chamber)

A small chamber measuring 7 x 9 x 3 m, located just below the western zone of Sa Rampa, accessed with great difficulty due to a very tight passage.

At the bottom of the chamber there is a sink, which is the reason it has been given this name.

name: Escletxa de sa Fusta
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
clothing: A good set of overalls recommended
equipment: One 50 m rope, 2 bolts, a cord for natural belay, 3 carabiners, some rope protectors
description: A small but engaging vertical cave discovered in October 2011 on the Serra de na Burguesa, near the firebreak path to the antenna installations. The entirely vertical fracture is never wider than one meter with sections as narrow as 40 cm, reaching 25 m depth. Formations are sparse but present throughout, mainly coralloid and pavement-type, with stalactites only near the entrance. The ascent can be quite challenging due to the extreme narrowness.
full_description:
Location

On October 30, 2011, while walking through the area in the company of A. Villar, we located the cavity. Once we had taken its coordinates, we left with the intention of beginning its exploration as soon as possible, which I did in the company of T. Mateu on November 1, All Saints' Day.

Description

The "Escletxa de sa Fusta" is a small cavity of -25 m that nonetheless meets the necessary requirements for spending a good time practicing sport caving. Located on the firebreak of the path leading to the antennas, just a few meters from the trail, its approach, while not short, is relatively comfortable.

Like other cavities of the "Serra de na Burguesa," the "Escletxa de sa Fusta" is a completely vertical fracture with a maximum width that in no case exceeds one meter, where in some places we will have to overcome constrictions no wider than 40 cm.

Sparse in formations, they are nonetheless found throughout the cavity, especially coralloid parietal and pavement types; stalactites, on the other hand, are only visible near the entrance mouth.

We can fix the rope to the thickest pine tree, the one located in the direction of the antennas. We will then enter the pit and look for the spit where we will set up a rebelay. The descent through this part of the cavity presents no major problem except that at around ten and a half meters, when we reach the level of the first rock bridge, we will have to pendulum a little to settle onto it. This can be a good place to leave backpacks and other accessories we won't need — in fact, it is the only place to do so. A spit invites us to set up another rebelay, but it is not necessary; we can continue descending another three meters to a new rock bridge. Once past it, two meters further down, a new spit allows us to set up a second rebelay to continue descending until we reach a point where we can rig on a rock bridge to finish the descent. We will still have to step on a stone along the way, unless it has come loose from being stepped on by another caver. The ascent can be truly challenging due to the narrowness.

name: Escletxa dets Ossos
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization. Drive to the top, park at the end of Television street in a lot to the right at the end of San Carlos street. Continue on Camí des Ri de ses Creus to a V-shaped junction and take the right branch toward Coll del Pastors (main trail).
equipment: Only helmet and lighting
description: A NE/SW-oriented fracture cavity with a maximum depth of -13 m at the western end and -7.5 m at the eastern end, found alongside the neighboring Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat by Vicente Pardo. Abundant parietal flowstones line the walls, and animal bones (dogs, goats) are found at both ends. The site is considered of special speleological interest.
full_description:
Found at the same time as its neighbor the 'Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat' by Vicente Pardo, we find ourselves before a cavity typical of the place where it is located, as it is a fracture with NE/SW orientation with a maximum depth of -13 m at the western end and -7.5 m at the eastern end. Despite their proximity, only 10 m from the 'Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat,' everything seems to indicate the lack of communication between them as both run parallel to each other.

In few places does the cavity reach one meter in width, quite the opposite of the height where in some places it reaches half a dozen meters. The concretioning is abundant, mainly notable are the parietal flowstones that cover practically all of the remote area as well as the ceiling formations; on the contrary, the floor formations are covered with stones.

At both the western and eastern ends, bone remains of different animals are found, among them at least those of dogs as well as some goat specimens. Alongside them at the western end, there is a large number of pine cones brought from outside by some rodent that has skillfully had to overcome the difficulties involved in accessing this place.

Regarding the fauna, I could only observe during the topography work several spider nests in the area illuminated by exterior light, as well as a woodlouse and a specimen of bat in a non-hibernation state, since during the previous visit a few weeks earlier it was not found inside the cavity.

For the visit, it is not necessary to carry vertical progression equipment, nor rope to overcome the access from the entrance and the two small difficulties that exist at the western end, as long as one is accustomed to navigating these terrains. The visit to the eastern end is done by crossing a small tight spot with no greater inconvenience than having to do so by dragging one's back along the ground.

In summary, it is a small cavity in keeping with many others in this mountain range that I believe is worth visiting, even though the visit will not allow us to make use of a full day. However, we will enjoy a beautiful approach to it, in addition to being able to visit two new additions in that location, such as the 'Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat' (461100, 4379715 - 340 m) just 10 meters away, or the 'Cova del Ferro' (461225, 4379620 - 325 m).

The Escletxa dets Ossos is considered a site of special speleological interest.

To learn more:

ENCINAS, J.A., 1974. Bio-speleological inventory of the Balearic Islands, year 1973. Com. IV Simp. Bioespeleología. E.C.E., G.E. Pedraforca: 49-62. Barcelona.

GINÉS, A., 1982. Inventory of cave-dwelling species of the Balearic Islands. Endins, 9: 57-75.

name: Gruta de l'Amo en Martí
starting_point: From Carrer de Gavina (Seagull Street)
footwear: Mountain boots. For the aquatic zone, other footwear recommended depending on time of year.
clothing: If you want to explore every corner of the cave, bring low-value clothing.
best_season: Any, although swimming season is recommended
equipment: Only helmet and lighting for the land zone. For the submerged zone, appropriate diving equipment.
description: A coastal grotto accessed through a natural entrance formed by a ceiling collapse, featuring a multi-level hall modified by human hands and rich in varied speleothems including massive stalagmitic formations. The cave contains several rooms, underground lakes with marine water intrusion, and submerged galleries accessible by diving. The site faces conservation challenges due to heavy visitor traffic and vandalism.
full_description:
Description

The terrestrial access to the cave is through the natural entrance that was formed after the collapse that brought the grotto to the surface. For marine access, diving equipment is necessary and it can be done both from the southern zone of the Sala des dos Nivells, as well as from the Llac de sa Morena.

Esfondrament (Collapse)

The vegetation, formed mainly by fig trees and brambles, occupies a large part of this zone of the grotto, concealing especially the eastern zone. It is the typical zenith collapse that formed a large hole on the surface measuring 9 x 11 m, from which the fig trees that grow inside protrude, while at the same time making the grotto visible. Subsequently, the hand of man conditioned the area, displacing not without great effort the blocks of stone and building a staircase that connects with the Sala des dos Nivells.

Sala des dos Nivells (Two-Level Hall)

As we mentioned before, the cave is in some places conditioned by human hands, this hall being the place where the most alteration has been done by leveling the floor surface into two levels separated from each other by a stone wall. The upper level has approximate dimensions of 7 x 8 x 4 m while those of the lower level are 6 x 7 x 3 m. On both levels, a stalagmitic massif runs from north to south along the western side, separating it from Sa Rampa, while another runs along the eastern side on the lower level. Noteworthy is the artificial skylight of less than one meter in diameter located on the upper level of the hall and directly above the wall that divides the two levels. The use it may have been given could have been for smoke extraction, since this area of the grotto is reasonably illuminated with the light that enters through the natural entrance. Another skylight of similar characteristics is found in the neighboring Cova dets Amagatalls, which was possibly used for the same purpose. Rich in varied speleothems, the enormous stalagmitic massifs that separate it from both Sa Rampa and the Sala Occidental stand out. At least one corrosion dome is located in the northern zone of the hall. Near a pool in the southern part of the hall, there are some small gours, which are fed by seawater that crashes next to them; said pool allows communication with the sea.

Sa Rampa (The Ramp)

Parallel to the previous hall, this hall is found, in which a great quantity of formations abound, especially in the northwestern zone, with approximate dimensions of 11 x 25 x 4 m. The northern zone is the most labyrinthine as well as being the most ornamented, while in the southern area, the spaces are more open, bordering with the water that enters the cavity from the sea. The entire eastern end of the hall is occupied by the stalagmitic massif that separates it from the Sala des dos Nivells.

Pou Nord (North Well)

This is a small room of 5 x 6 x 3.5 m, located to the northwest of the cave and lacking in interest, accessed through a small opening.

Sala Occidental (Western Hall)

With dimensions of 11 x 8 m, it is accessed from the Sala des dos Nivells, after crossing a ramp of piled stones that culminates next to a small stone wall, which together with other large blocks fallen as a consequence of the collapse of the entrance area separates it from the rest of the cave. It is reasonably concretioned with several columns as well as some stalagmitic massifs.

Pou Sud (South Well)

Measuring 2 x 8 x 3 m, the Pou Sud reaches sea level; however, its accessibility from both the exterior and from any other place in the cavity is impossible, except for the terrestrial entrance from the southern zone of Sala Occidental where a small hole allows us to enter it. Stalagmites and the occasional small column decorate the place. The bottom is occupied by a small lake resulting from marine intrusion, which does not connect, at least in a traversable way, with the rest of the cave.

Llac de sa Morena (Moray Eel Lake)

With dimensions of 15 x 14 m, the Llac de sa Morena owes its name to a moray eel specimen that has established its residence in this place. The maximum depth of the lake is around one and a half meters, where in addition to the moray eel it is easy to find small shrimp. The sandstone ceiling of the hall that houses the lake is in some places affected by corrosion. From this place, the cave communicates directly with the sea.

Galeria de sa Creu (Gallery of the Cross)

This is a submerged gallery with dimensions of 12 x 12 m, in which the ceiling at some points does not reach one meter in height. At its entrance from the exterior, a small stainless steel cross can be observed in memory of the young person who drowned in this place.

Galeria des Polp (Octopus Gallery)

This is the gallery through which one can access the interior of the grotto most directly; its dimensions are 20 x 25 m, and the height never drops below one meter.

Cave Maintenance

One of the problems suffered by caves that are close to population centers, as is the case with the Gruta de l'Amo en Martí, is the large influx of visitors who enter them for various reasons, such as curiosity, to swim in the lake it houses during the summer season in the case of this cave, or simply to commit acts of vandalism, making graffiti, from which this grotto is not exempt either. The large number of visitors of all types that the Gruta de l'Amo en Martí receives contributes to the accumulation of all kinds of rubbish, from candles, bottles, blankets for overnight stays, etc.

At this point, one must ask: what could be the most acceptable solution so that this type of caves could be enjoyed by all who wished to and at the same time could be kept in good condition? It is not for us to give that answer, but someone will have to do so sooner rather than later.

name: Mina de Son Creus
starting_point: Bunyola to Orient road, through a gate at a sharp bend at coordinates 475030 E, 4395931 N, 314 m (UTM DATUM European 50)
equipment: 15-20 m of rope and at least a harness with a figure-eight descender, plus lighting
description: One of the most important historical mines in Mallorca, located on the Sierra de Alfábia between Bunyola and Orient. Exploited since Roman times for lead, copper, and glaze materials, it features an extensive labyrinth of narrow galleries with vertical ventilation shafts. Navigation is challenging due to low ceilings, narrow passages, and multiple branching tunnels, some flooded. Ruins of mineral smelting furnaces can still be found nearby.
full_description:
On this occasion, we are going to practice speleology in one of the most important mines, if not the most important, on the island of Mallorca. Now out of use, it is located in the Sierra de Alfábia and is none other than the Son Creus mine, situated on the left side of the road from Bunyola to Orient. Exploited for centuries, supposedly since Roman times, it has a horizontal entrance with a slight slope, which then branches into several galleries. A mule dragged the wagons towards the interior, and from there they descended on their own, loaded, down the gentle slope, braking so they would not gain speed. Lead, copper, and material for making the glaze used to varnish clay were extracted. Jaume remembers how his father, the last foreman of the mine, told him about the incredible weight of the small sacks that were sent, also by train — in this case from Sóller — to Palma.

From the Roman era, the remains of galena mines are significant. (Galena is a mineral of the sulfide group. It crystallizes in the form of cubes. The arrangement of ions in the crystal is the same as in sodium chloride (NaCl), sea salt. Chemically, it is lead sulfide, although it may contain variable amounts of impurities. Thus, its silver content can reach 1%.) Wikipedia Encyclopedia, http://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galena.

From the Byzantine era, there is only evidence from a lighting lamp found, dated between the 6th and 8th centuries AD.

The galleries had vertical shafts for ventilation, usually covered with branches to prevent goats or hunters from accidentally falling in.

The labyrinth of galleries in this mine is very large and extends in all directions, so entering the mine without experience can be complicated and is not recommended for everyone, a matter I leave to each individual's judgment. In my particular visit to this mine, I did so accompanied by J. A. Encinas, a person who, together with R. Minguillón, M. Luque, M. L. Redondo, P. Seguí, and P. Calafat, surveyed the mine over a period of approximately one year. We entered the mine through the Cova de sa Caseta and exited through the Son Creus entrance, which is at a lower level.

It is not comfortable to move through the mine's galleries, at least through the ones I traversed, since almost the entire time one must go completely bent over, scraping the backpack against the walls due to their narrowness. Nor do I dare to describe the route connecting the Cova de sa Caseta with the Son Creus entrance, as there are multiple branches.

Some of these galleries are flooded and some exits to the outside are through chimneys, making exit difficult. It is also possible that some galleries may be collapsed, so I am simply reflecting the potential dangers of entering this mine without experience.

The entrance to the mine through the Cova de sa Caseta, orientation 70°, is made through a short rappel that will deposit us inside, allowing us to advance almost always through very narrow galleries and steep slopes that in some cases exceed 45%, until exiting through the Son Creus mouth, which is oriented at 90°.

In the vicinity of the mine, the ruins of multiple furnaces where the mineral was smelted can still be found, with some of them in good condition, allowing one to appreciate the quality of the stone construction.

name: Pouet Escollerat de l'Ase
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: A rope of approximately 10 m, to be tied to a pine tree next to the pit entrance
description: A small but interesting pit near Cova del Tuputut, discovered on February 9, 2008. The circular entrance is 0.60 m wide and leads to a well-decorated alveolar cavity featuring stalagmites, stalactites, small flags, and fine soda straws. Water marks on the walls indicate historical water levels. The skeleton of a donkey and some goat remains are found inside.
full_description:
Near the Cova del Tuputut lies this small and interesting pit which, although its dimensions are not very large in either depth or width, is nonetheless interesting as it is a most charming pit that I found on February 9, 2008, a few weeks before its exploration.

The dimensions of the circular-shaped entrance are 0.60 m, and I have covered it with branches to prevent anyone from accidentally falling inside. Once the branches covering the entrance are removed, we only need to tie the rope to the pine tree next to it to begin the short descent which, after a small overhang, will place us on top of a detritus cone from which we can observe that we are in a highly decorated alveolar-type cavity where stalagmites, stalactites, small flags, and very fine soda straws can be seen.

It is worth noting in this small cavity the water marks on the wall that indicate the different levels the water reached during some of its stages, with the highest one approximately one meter and twenty centimeters from the ground, as some of the formations hanging from the ceiling clearly indicate.

Very close to the pit entrance, a significant collapse can be seen, apparently not very remote in time, which starts practically from the surface, so everything seems to indicate that very soon perhaps a new entrance to the pit may open up.

A sort of crawlway about one meter high seems to indicate the continuation of the pit, but it only seems that way as it is not so. However, I recommend going into it as far as it allows and then looking upward to contemplate a beautifully decorated corner.

The skeleton of an equine, possibly a donkey, is found in a corner of the cavity alongside that of some goats. The first one certainly could not have fallen into the pit due to the dimensions of the entrance, so everything indicates that someone took it upon themselves, once the animal was butchered, to throw its remains inside — a very common custom widespread on the island.

It is advisable to bring a camera when visiting this cavity, as we will not regret it.

We can complete the visit to this pit by visiting either the Cova del Tuputut, which is a little more than one hundred and fifty meters away at the antenna road junction, or the impressive 'Avenc de s'Esquerda,' which was also found and explored for the first time by Mallorca Verde.

name: S'Embut
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint (Génova). Follow the path past the radio station antennas, continuing toward Coll des Pastors.
equipment: Only helmet and lighting for a normal visit
description: A two-chamber cave at different levels, discovered on August 13, 2008. The upper chamber features unusual cone-shaped roots that are slowly calcifying into 'hairy stalagmites' from constant water dripping. The lower chamber is more richly decorated with 'fried egg' formations and shows signs of periodic lake formation. Animal bones including an equine skeleton are present.
full_description:
It is curious that a cave of these characteristics, composed of two chambers at different levels, sufficiently decorated and housing some interesting curiosities inside — among others, some eccentric stalactites — does not appear in the catalog of J. Encinas Sánchez S. 'Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands' 2006. But it is true, it does not appear, although that does not mean the cave had not been visited before August 13, 2008, which was the day when, soaked from head to toe in the company of A. Villar, my wife, I found it. The fact is that in the cave there is written evidence that it had already been previously visited by members of a speleological group from the island who, to set a 'good' example, left their mark in more than one place in the cave with black spray paint. We were also able to find a piece of ceramic inside, but once visually analyzed by J. A. Encinas, it was ruled out as having any interest.

Like many other caves and pits on the island, the cave came to light through a terrain collapse that formed a kind of dustpan or funnel, at the lower part of which the entrance to the cave proper is found. The approximate dimensions of this entrance are about 5.5 m wide by 1.20 m high, and it is oriented at 27° NNE.

On August 31, 2008, accompanied by J. Encinas and G. Herráez, we began the exploration, starting the descent into the interior of the cavity down a considerable slope for several meters until reaching a small landing where we will turn left to continue descending in a NW direction through a kind of gallery with a slope close to 30% at some points, where the ground is composed of loose stones and earth but without any danger. Soon a chamber appears before our eyes that is not excessively decorated, but once analyzed more carefully proved very interesting as it houses some curiosities inside. The approximate dimensions of the chamber are about 20 m wide by 13 m long and 10 m high. The floor of the chamber is far from horizontal as it is composed of a ramp that ends at the bottom of the wall. On the wall, at a height slightly less than one and a half meters, a horizontal line seems to indicate an ancient water level in this place, but since the cave contains a chamber at a lower level, it is difficult to understand how that mark would indicate the level of an ancient lake. Of course, another hypothesis is possible, namely that said mark on the wall, instead of indicating a water mark, was indicating the level of sediment stored before the continuation of the cave opened up and the sediment collapsed downward.

Some of the curiosities housed in this chamber are more than interesting, notably some cone-shaped roots that grow in it as if they were plants seeking moisture they cannot find underground. But the most curious thing of all is that on almost all of them, water drops continuously drip, and upon hitting the tip of the root, a kind of hollow has formed. At the same time, due to the high calcium carbonate content of the water, the roots are transforming into curious 'hairy stalagmites.'

As in many other caves and pits, a large quantity of bones appears before our eyes in this chamber, notably part of the skeleton of an equine. However, the search for ancient ceramics was fruitless, as we only found one piece of ceramic that belongs to a recent period.

Although formations are not too abundant in this chamber, it is not devoid of them. Noteworthy is a small corner at the back of the chamber, at a height of less than two meters, to the right of the 'sinkhole' that provides continuity to the cave. Worth highlighting are a couple of small 'balconies' as well as a beautiful flowstone next to a thick stalagmite. Three small columns in a line open an alternative passage for us to continue descending through the cave to the aforementioned 'sinkhole,' and if one day it becomes blocked — something that could happen at any time — using vertical progression equipment would allow us to continue advancing. But as long as this does not happen, it is best to descend through the 'sinkhole' down a slope that reaches 50% at some points; there is even a small drop that can be overcome without great difficulty.

The lower chamber, the most beautiful one, is very well decorated, with multiple 'fried eggs' spread across its surface. Additionally, at the time of my visit, I could see signs that at some point a small lake forms in this chamber. Despite the first visit to the cave having taken place in August, the cave is active in both this chamber and the upper one.

The continuation of the cave runs through a narrow well of approximately 8 m depth that can be accessed without the need for vertical progression equipment but lacks interest.

There are many caves and especially pits in the vicinity, so if we do not have enough to spend the day with a visit to this cave, we can go to any of them. The nearest cave of some interest known by Mallorca Verde is the Cueva Bandolera, located on July 2, 2008, by us and which is worth visiting. The coordinates of the cave are: 464196 E, 4379554 N. DATUM EUROPEAN 1950.

name: Secret de sa Figuera
starting_point: Km 5.400 of the PMV-4014 road
equipment: Only helmet and lighting
description: Located near Cala Magraner, this cave is notable for both its natural and archaeological value, harboring evidence of prehistoric human use including modifications to its access and interior. Features include a hall possibly related to historical smuggling activities and a prehistorically used entrance hall. The fig tree that gave the cave its name has since disappeared.
full_description:
The Cova des Secret de sa Figuera, located in the Cala Magraner area, is known for its natural and archaeological value, harboring evidence of anthropic use that includes modifications to its access or interior, characteristic of the prehistoric caves of the island.

Fig tree that gives the cavity its name, currently disappeared.

Room conditioned, possibly related to smuggling.

Entrance hall, of prehistoric use.

name: Secret des Moix
starting_point: Lisbon Street (Calle Lisboa)
best_season: Any, as long as the sea is calm
equipment: Only helmet and lighting, unless you want to access the aquatic zone where a wetsuit is needed.
description: A coastal cave near Cala Murada featuring a large single chamber approximately 30 meters long and 19 meters wide, with a pit-shaped terrestrial entrance. The cave contains a shallow lake with marine intrusion, whose ceiling is heavily decorated with baroque-style formations including fistular stalactites and curtains. Beyond the lake lies a small secondary chamber with additional speleothems.
full_description:
The Cova Secret des Moix is one more cave with characteristics typical of those found along the coastline in this area. The cave in question is located less than fifteen meters from the sea, with access to the interior of the cavity on the left side of the cavity. The terrestrial entrance has an opening in the form of a pit of considerable dimensions, and once inside it, a second entrance gives access to the interior; the dimensions of this second entrance are 2.5 m wide by 1.40 m high, oriented 335° NNW.

Once inside, a ramp of considerable slope covered with multiple stones and "easy" to traverse — although beware of a possible slip when the stones are wet — will deposit us at the bottom of the cavity, which is completely flat and covered with a layer of sand that enters the interior of the cave dragged by the waves through the marine intrusion of the cave, not to mention what enters through the terrestrial entrance as can be verified by the remains of posidonia that can be observed at the entrance of the cave. A wide groove in the sand crosses the cave from one end to the other through which the seawater flows to feed the lake.

Moving through the cavity therefore presents no complication, since it is a single large hall of about 30 m from the entrance to the lake, which is located directly opposite the entrance opening, and a width of approximately 19 m if we do not count the small room on the other side of the lake. The hall is not concretioned except at the shore where the marine incursion "dies" and already inside the lake where some interesting concretions can be appreciated, even on the ceiling, but not in the terrestrial hall. The ceiling height in the great hall ranges from several meters at the highest point to less than one meter near the lake.

On my first visit to the cave, I could only reach the shore of the lake, leaving it pending for a later visit, which happened almost a year later. The lake has little depth; in fact, in few places does it exceed one meter in depth, but that does not make it any less interesting since it is precisely the shores of the lake as well as the ceiling where it flows that hold almost all of the cave's concretions.

From the shore of the lake, and before we venture into its waters, visible before us on the other side of the lake is a sort of gallery more than one meter wide, which raises the question of whether the cave continues through said gallery or is simply a dead end. Well, it is neither one nor the other, or yes. The narrowing or gallery is about four meters long, leading to a mini room partly covered with water, about 4 meters wide and a little more than 6 meters long, and reasonably concretioned both on the ceiling and its surroundings, with some small stalagmites protruding from the water and a small column.

Returning to the shore of the lake, we can venture into it again on the right side, leaving a column to our right as well, and directly ahead it is also possible to see dry land at the other end of the lake, which encourages us to reach it. But unfortunately, the cave does not continue much at that point; in fact, it is practically not worth getting out of the water since from it one can contemplate what remains to be seen.

At the other end of the lake there is not much to see; in fact, the ceiling is close to the water surface and offers nothing of interest.

The entire lake ceiling is heavily concretioned in a very baroque style, so I recommend looking upward while traversing its waters, where fistular stalactites, curtains, etc. abound.

As I said earlier, this cave has a marine intrusion forming a mini beach upon entering the hall next to a small column. Once past the column (as long as the sea is calm), we can still progress a few more meters through a sort of gallery.

The Cova Secret des Moix is considered a natural site of special interest, specifically speleological. J. A. Encinas S. Inventorial Relation of Natural Underground Features of the Balearic Islands. 2006. JAES Editions.

Not far from here are the Cueva de ses Figueres and the Cueva dels Ases, which we can visit to make better use of the day, since the visit to this cave, especially if we do not explore the aquatic zone, does not require too much time.
